topics.title: All Arithmetic Operations - Addition, Subtraction (32 bit), Multiplication and Division (8 bit)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRUL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND IOT LABORATORY > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** All Arithmetic Operations - Addition, Subtraction (32 bit), Multiplication and Division (8 bit) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition **Arithmetic Operations in 8051 Microcontroller Assembly Language** refer to the fundamental set of machine-level instructions that perform mathematical computations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division) directly on data stored in internal RAM registers and the accumulator. As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCRUL408)** syllabus, this experiment requires students to write, debug, simulate, and execute 8051 assembly programs that handle **32-bit arithmetic** (addition, subtraction) and **8-bit arithmetic** (multiplication, division), understanding multi-byte data representation, **Carry Flag (CY)** propagation, and **Borrow Flag** handling. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2)**: Students must demonstrate proficiency in Keil µVision IDE, simulate the programs using the 8051 simulator, and verify results using memory window or logic analyzer tools. ## 1.2 The 8051 Arithmetic Instruction Set — At a Glance | Operation Type | Instruction | Operands | Affected Flag | |----------------|-------------|----------|---------------| | 8-bit Addition | `ADD` | A, src | CY, AC, OV | | 8-bit Addition with Carry | `ADDC` | A, src | CY, AC, OV | | 8-bit Subtraction with Borrow | `SUBB` | A, src | CY (borrow), AC, OV | | 8-bit Multiplication | `MUL` | AB | CY=0, OV | | 8-bit Division | `DIV` | AB | CY=0, OV | | Increment | `INC` | A / Rn / direct | None | | Decrement | `DEC` | A / Rn / direct | None | | Decimal Adjust | `DA` | A | CY | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Digital Cashier's Counter** > > Imagine you are a cashier with only an **8-digit mechanical calculator** (the accumulator), but your boss asks you to add two huge piles of money each containing **billions of rupees**. > > - **32-bit addition** is like carefully adding these large numbers **one digit at a time**, from right to left, while **remembering any carry** (leftover tens) and propagating it to the next column — exactly how you learned addition in primary school. > - **32-bit subtraction** is the reverse — borrowing from the next column when needed. > - **8-bit multiplication** is like the calculator's built-in `×` button that instantly multiplies two single-digit numbers and gives you a two-digit answer (high and low bytes). > - **8-bit division** is the calculator's built-in `÷` button that gives you both the **quotient** and the **remainder** in two separate displays. ## 1.4 Key Physical & Architectural Constants - **Accumulator (A)**: An **8-bit register** at SFR address **E0H** — the primary operand for all arithmetic. - **B Register**: An **8-bit register** at SFR address **F0H** — used exclusively with `MUL` and `DIV`. - **Program Status Word (PSW)** at **D0H**: Holds the **CY (Carry)**, **AC (Auxiliary Carry)**, **OV (Overflow)**, and **P (Parity)** flags. - **Internal RAM size**: **128 bytes** (00H to 7FH) for general-purpose data storage. - **Data type sizes used in this experiment**: 8-bit (1 byte) and 32-bit (4 bytes, occupying 4 consecutive RAM locations). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Multi-Byte Arithmetic — The Carry Propagation Principle ### 2.1.1 Why 32-bit Arithmetic is Required The 8051 is fundamentally an **8-bit microcontroller** — its data bus, ALU, and accumulator all operate on 8 bits (range: **0 to 255 unsigned** or **−128 to +127 signed**). However, real-world engineering applications (sensor data, timers, ADC outputs, industrial counters) frequently require values much larger than 255. Therefore, multiple bytes are combined to represent larger numbers: $$ N_{\text{32-bit}} = B_3 \cdot 2^{24} + B_2 \cdot 2^{16} + B_1 \cdot 2^{8} + B_0 $$ where **B0** is the **LSB** (Least Significant Byte) and **B3** is the **MSB** (Most Significant Byte). ### 2.1.2 The Role of the Carry Flag (CY) The **Carry Flag (CY)** in the PSW acts as a **"memory cell"** that records whether an addition produced an overflow beyond the 8-bit boundary. For multi-byte arithmetic: - For the **LSB byte**: use `ADD` (clears the incoming carry). - For **all subsequent higher bytes**: use `ADDC` (includes the carry from the previous byte). ``` Logical Flow: Byte 0: ADD ← Starts fresh (no carry-in) Byte 1: ADDC ← Includes carry from Byte 0 Byte 2: ADDC ← Includes carry from Byte 1 Byte 3: ADDC ← Includes carry from Byte 2 (final carry indicates 33-bit overflow) ``` > [!TIP] > **Engineering Insight**: Carry propagation is the foundational concept behind **arbitrary-precision arithmetic libraries** used in cryptography (RSA, ECC), financial systems, and scientific computing. The 8051's simple `ADD`/`ADDC` mechanism is the blueprint for every modern big-number implementation. ## 2.2 8-bit Multiplication — Hardware Multiplier The 8051 contains a **dedicated hardware multiplier** that performs unsigned 8-bit × 8-bit multiplication in **4 machine cycles** (4 µs at 12 MHz clock). $$ A \times B \rightarrow \underbrace{A}_{\text{Low byte (result low)}} : \underbrace{B}_{\text{High byte (result high)}} $$ - **Operand locations**: Multiplicand → `A`, Multiplier → `B`. - **Result size**: **16-bit** (maximum: `FFH × FFH = FE01H` = 65,025). - **OV flag**: Set if the result > 255 (i.e., if B ≠ 0 after multiplication). - **CY flag**: Always cleared. ## 2.3 8-bit Division — Hardware Divider The 8051's hardware divider performs unsigned 8-bit ÷ 8-bit division in **4 machine cycles**. $$ \frac{A}{B} \rightarrow \underbrace{A}_{\text{Quotient}} : \underbrace{B}_{\text{Remainder}} $$ - **Operand locations**: Dividend → `A`, Divisor → `B`. - **Quotient and remainder** are both **8-bit**. - **OV flag**: Set if the divisor was **0** (division by zero error). - **CY flag**: Always cleared. - **Maximum quotient**: 255 (when dividend = 255, divisor = 1). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Range / Unit | Flag Behavior | |---------|---------------|--------------|---------------| | 32-bit unsigned value | $N = \sum_{i=0}^{3} B_i \cdot 2^{8i}$ | 0 to $2^{32} - 1$ ≈ 4.29 × 10⁹ | N/A | | 32-bit signed range | $-2^{31}$ to $2^{31}-1$ | approx ±2.147 × 10⁹ | OV indicates signed overflow | | 8-bit multiplication | $P = A \times B$ | 0 to 65,025 (FE01H) | OV=1 if P > 255 | | 8-bit division | $Q = A \div B$, $R = A \mod B$ | Q: 0–255, R: 0–(B−1) | OV=1 if B=0 | | Carry propagation | $C_{\text{out},i} = A_i \cdot B_i + A_i \cdot C_{\text{in},i} + B_i \cdot C_{\text{in},i}$ | Boolean | CY set on overflow | | Borrow in subtraction | $\overline{C}_{\text{out},i} = \overline{A}_i \cdot B_i + \overline{A}_i \cdot C_{\text{in},i} + B_i \cdot C_{\text{in},i}$ | Boolean | CY=1 means borrow occurred | | Auxiliary Carry (AC) | Carry between **bit 3 and bit 4** | Used for BCD | Set/cleared by ADD/ADDC/SUBB | | Hex ↔ Decimal | $\text{Dec} = \sum d_i \cdot 16^i$ | N/A | N/A | | PSW address | **D0H** (bit-addressable) | SFR | CY=PSW.7, AC=PSW.6, OV=PSW.2 | > [!NOTE] > **Anti-Pattern Alert**: Many KTU students incorrectly write `SUB A, src` — there is **no such instruction** in 8051. The only subtraction instruction is `SUBB A, src` (always with borrow). For the first byte, you **must explicitly clear CY** using `CLR C`. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | Use of Multi-Byte Arithmetic | |--------------------|------------------------------| | **Industrial Process Control** | 32-bit counters for revolutions, distance, flow totalization | | **Digital Weighing Scales** | 24/32-bit ADC values requiring averaging and offset compensation | | **Real-Time Clocks (RTC)** | Time arithmetic (epoch seconds fit in 32 bits until year 2106) | | **Cryptographic Systems** | Big-integer arithmetic (modular exponentiation) | | **Motor Control (PWM)** | 16-bit multiplication for duty-cycle calculation | | **Sensor Fusion** | 32-bit fixed-point arithmetic in Kalman filters | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Program 1 — 32-Bit Unsigned Addition (Complete Exhaustive Walk-through) ### Problem Statement Add two 32-bit unsigned numbers stored in internal RAM: - **First number (NUM1)**: Stored at locations **30H (LSB), 31H, 32H, 33H (MSB)** - **Second number (NUM2)**: Stored at locations **40H (LSB), 41H, 42H, 43H (MSB)** - **Result (SUM)**: To be stored at locations **50H (LSB), 51H, 52H, 53H (MSB)** ### Numerical Example for Verification Let **NUM1 = 12345678H** and **NUM2 = 87654321H**. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{NUM1} &= 12\,34\,56\,78_{H} \\ \text{NUM2} &= 87\,65\,43\,21_{H} \\ \text{SUM} &= 99\,99\,99\,99_{H} \end{aligned} $$ ### Complete 8051 Assembly Program ```assembly ;============================================================= ; EXPERIMENT 2A: 32-BIT ADDITION (8051 ASSEMBLY) ; Author: Student, KTU 2024 Scheme ; Description: Adds two 32-bit unsigned numbers with carry ;============================================================= ORG 0000H ; Reset origin address LJMP MAIN ; Jump to main program ORG 0030H ; Program starts after vector space MAIN: ;-- Byte 0 (LSB): Use ADD (no incoming carry) -------- MOV A, 30H ; A ← NUM1[LSB] = 78H ADD A, 40H ; A ← A + NUM2[LSB] = 78H + 21H = 99H, CY=0 MOV 50H, A ; SUM[LSB] ← 99H ;-- Byte 1: Use ADDC (includes carry from Byte 0) ----- MOV A, 31H ; A ← NUM1[1] = 56H ADDC A, 41H ; A ← A + NUM2[1] + CY = 56H + 43H + 0 = 99H, CY=0 MOV 51H, A ; SUM[1] ← 99H ;-- Byte 2: Use ADDC ---------------------------------- MOV A, 32H ; A ← NUM1[2] = 34H ADDC A, 42H ; A ← A + NUM2[2] + CY = 34H + 65H + 0 = 99H, CY=0 MOV 52H, A ; SUM[2] ← 99H ;-- Byte 3 (MSB): Use ADDC (final carry = overflow) --- MOV A, 33H ; A ← NUM1[MSB] = 12H ADDC A, 43H ; A ← A + NUM2[MSB] + CY = 12H + 87H + 0 = 99H, CY=0 MOV 53H, A ; SUM[MSB] ← 99H ;-- Optional: Store final carry in 54H --------------- MOV 54H, C ; Save final carry (overflow indicator) HERE: SJMP HERE ; Infinite loop to halt program END ; End of assembly source ``` ### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Trace (Byte-by-Byte) | Step | Instruction | A (before) | Memory Op | Operation | A (after) | CY | Result Location | |------|-------------|------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|----|-----------------| | 1 | `MOV A, 30H` | ??H | 30H=78H | Load LSB of NUM1 | 78H | x | — | | 2 | `ADD A, 40H` | 78H | 40H=21H | 78H + 21H = 99H | 99H | 0 | — | | 3 | `MOV 50H, A` | 99H | — | Store LSB of result | 99H | 0 | 50H ← 99H | | 4 | `MOV A, 31H` | 99H | 31H=56H | Load byte 1 | 56H | 0 | — | | 5 | `ADDC A, 41H` | 56H | 41H=43H | 56H + 43H + 0 = 99H | 99H | 0 | — | | 6 | `MOV 51H, A` | 99H | — | Store byte 1 of result | 99H | 0 | 51H ← 99H | | 7 | `MOV A, 32H` | 99H | 32H=34H | Load byte 2 | 34H | 0 | — | | 8 | `ADDC A, 42H` | 34H | 42H=65H | 34H + 65H + 0 = 99H | 99H | 0 | — | | 9 | `MOV 52H, A` | 99H | — | Store byte 2 of result | 99H | 0 | 52H ← 99H | | 10 | `MOV A, 33H` | 99H | 33H=12H | Load MSB | 12H | 0 | — | | 11 | `ADDC A, 43H` | 12H | 43H=87H | 12H + 87H + 0 = 99H | 99H | 0 | — | | 12 | `MOV 53H, A` | 99H | — | Store MSB of result | 99H | 0 | 53H ← 99H | | 13 | `MOV 54H, C` | — | — | Save carry | — | 0 | 54H ← 00H | **Final Result in Memory**: `50H = 99H, 51H = 99H, 52H = 99H, 53H = 99H` → **SUM = 99999999H ✓** ### Verification Using Hexadecimal Arithmetic $$ \begin{aligned} 12\,34\,56\,78_{H} &= 1 \cdot 16^7 + 2 \cdot 16^6 + \cdots \\ &= 305\,419\,896_{D} \\ 87\,65\,43\,21_{H} &= 2\,278\,690\,593_{D} \\ \text{Sum}_{D} &= 305\,419\,896 + 2\,278\,690\,593 = 2\,584\,110\,489 \\ \text{Sum}_{H} &= 2\,584\,110\,489 \div 16 = 161\,506\,905\,R9 \quad (\text{LSB}) \\ &\rightarrow 99\,99\,99\,99_{H} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.2 Program 2 — 32-Bit Unsigned Subtraction ### Problem Statement Subtract **NUM2** from **NUM1** (both 32-bit) and store result in 50H–53H. ### Critical Pitfall: Borrow Initialization For 8051 subtraction, the `SUBB` instruction **always subtracts the carry flag** as a borrow. So the first subtraction **must** clear CY, or it will subtract an extra 1. ### Complete Assembly Program ```assembly ;============================================================= ; EXPERIMENT 2B: 32-BIT SUBTRACTION (8051 ASSEMBLY) ; Description: Subtracts NUM2 from NUM1 (32-bit unsigned) ;============================================================= ORG 0000H LJMP MAIN ORG 0030H MAIN: CLR C ; *** CRITICAL: Clear borrow flag *** ; SUBB always subtracts CY as borrow ;-- Byte 0 (LSB) -------------------------------------- MOV A, 30H ; A ← NUM1[LSB] SUBB A, 40H ; A ← A - NUM2[LSB] - CY = NUM1[LSB] - NUM2[LSB] - 0 MOV 50H, A ; Store result LSB ;-- Byte 1: Borrow propagates naturally via CY --------- MOV A, 31H ; A ← NUM1[1] SUBB A, 41H ; A ← A - NUM2[1] - CY (borrow from previous byte) MOV 51H, A ; Store result byte 1 ;-- Byte 2 -------------------------------------------- MOV A, 32H ; A ← NUM1[2] SUBB A, 42H ; Subtract with borrow MOV 52H, A ; Store result byte 2 ;-- Byte 3 (MSB): final CY=1 means NUM2 > NUM1 ------- MOV A, 33H ; A ← NUM1[MSB] SUBB A, 43H ; Subtract with borrow MOV 53H, A ; Store result MSB MOV 54H, C ; Save final borrow (1 = negative result) HERE: SJMP HERE END ``` ### Numerical Verification Let **NUM1 = 87654321H** and **NUM2 = 12345678H**. Expected result = **7530EC9H**. | Byte | NUM1 | NUM2 | Borrow In | Calc | Borrow Out | Result | |------|------|------|-----------|------|------------|--------| | 0 (LSB) | 21H | 78H | 0 | 21H − 78H − 0 = A9H (borrow) | 1 | A9H | | 1 | 43H | 56H | 1 | 43H − 56H − 1 = ECH (borrow) | 1 | ECH | | 2 | 65H | 34H | 1 | 65H − 34H − 1 = 30H | 0 | 30H | | 3 (MSB) | 87H | 12H | 0 | 87H − 12H − 0 = 75H | 0 | 75H | **Final Result**: `50H = A9H, 51H = ECH, 52H = 30H, 53H = 75H` → **7530ECA9H ✓** --- ## 3.3 Program 3 — 8-Bit Unsigned Multiplication ### Problem Statement Multiply two 8-bit numbers and store the 16-bit result in two memory locations. ### Complete Assembly Program ```assembly ;============================================================= ; EXPERIMENT 2C: 8-BIT MULTIPLICATION (8051 ASSEMBLY) ; Description: Multiplies two 8-bit numbers using MUL AB ; Result: Low byte in A, High byte in B (after MUL) ;============================================================= ORG 0000H LJMP MAIN ORG 0030H MAIN: MOV A, #25H ; A ← 25H (multiplicand = 37 decimal) MOV B, #10H ; B ← 10H (multiplier = 16 decimal) MUL AB ; A*B → A=low byte, B=high byte ;-- Store the 16-bit result -------------------------- MOV 30H, A ; 30H ← Low byte of result MOV 31H, B ; 31H ← High byte of result HERE: SJMP HERE END ``` ### Numerical Verification $$ \begin{aligned} 25_{H} \times 10_{H} &= 37_{D} \times 16_{D} \\ &= 592_{D} \\ &= 0250_{H} \\ &\rightarrow \underbrace{02_{H}}_{\text{High byte (B)}} : \underbrace{50_{H}}_{\text{Low byte (A)}} \end{aligned} $$ After execution: **30H = 50H** (low byte), **31H = 02H** (high byte). ✓ ### Edge Case — Maximum 8-bit × 8-bit $$ \text{FF}_{H} \times \text{FF}_{H} = 255 \times 255 = 65\,025_{D} = \text{FE01}_{H} $$ - After `MUL AB`: A = 01H, B = FEH, OV = 1 (because high byte ≠ 0). --- ## 3.4 Program 4 — 8-Bit Unsigned Division ### Problem Statement Divide an 8-bit dividend by an 8-bit divisor and store both quotient and remainder. ### Complete Assembly Program ```assembly ;============================================================= ; EXPERIMENT 2D: 8-BIT DIVISION (8051 ASSEMBLY) ; Description: Divides 8-bit dividend by 8-bit divisor using DIV AB ; Result: Quotient in A, Remainder in B (after DIV) ;============================================================= ORG 0000H LJMP MAIN ORG 0030H MAIN: MOV A, #64H ; A ← 64H (dividend = 100 decimal) MOV B, #0AH ; B ← 0AH (divisor = 10 decimal) DIV AB ; A / B → A=quotient, B=remainder ;-- Store the results -------------------------------- MOV 30H, A ; 30H ← Quotient MOV 31H, B ; 31H ← Remainder HERE: SJMP HERE END ``` ### Numerical Verification $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{64_{H}}{0A_{H}} &= \frac{100_{D}}{10_{D}} \\ &= 10_{D} \text{ remainder } 0_{D} \\ &= 0\text{A}_{H} \text{ remainder } 00_{H} \end{aligned} $$ After execution: **30H = 0AH** (quotient), **31H = 00H** (remainder). ✓ ### Division-by-Zero Handling If `B = 00H` before `DIV AB`: - A and B are left **undefined**. - **OV flag is set** to 1 to indicate the error. - Program should ideally check OV after division and branch to an error handler. --- ## 3.5 Consolidated Memory Map & Analytical Matrix | Program | Input Locations | Output Locations | Flags Affected | Critical Instruction | |---------|----------------|------------------|----------------|----------------------| | 32-bit ADD | 30H–33H, 40H–43H | 50H–53H (+ 54H for final CY) | CY, AC, OV | `ADD` for byte 0, `ADDC` for bytes 1–3 | | 32-bit SUB | 30H–33H, 40H–43H | 50H–53H (+ 54H for borrow) | CY, AC, OV | `CLR C` before LSB, then `SUBB` for all bytes | | 8-bit MUL | A and B registers | A (low), B (high) → 30H, 31H | OV (if > 255), CY=0 | `MUL AB` | | 8-bit DIV | A and B registers | A (quotient), B (remainder) → 30H, 31H | OV (if B=0), CY=0 | `DIV AB` | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Program Flow — 32-bit Addition ```mermaid flowchart TD startNode1([Start: 32-bit Addition]) --> init1[Initialize Memory:
NUM1 at 30H-33H
NUM2 at 40H-43H] init1 --> load1[Load LSB: A ← NUM1 at 30H] load1 --> addByte0[ADD A with NUM2 at 40H
First byte uses ADD, no carry] addByte0 --> store0[Store LSB result at 50H] store0 --> load1a[Load byte 1: A ← NUM1 at 31H] load1a --> adcByte1[ADDC A with NUM2 at 41H
Includes carry from byte 0] adcByte1 --> store1[Store byte 1 result at 51H] store1 --> load2[Load byte 2: A ← NUM1 at 32H] load2 --> adcByte2[ADDC A with NUM2 at 42H
Includes carry from byte 1] adcByte2 --> store2[Store byte 2 result at 52H] store2 --> load3[Load MSB: A ← NUM1 at 33H] load3 --> adcByte3[ADDC A with NUM2 at 43H
Final carry indicates 33-bit overflow] adcByte3 --> store3[Store MSB result at 53H] store3 --> savecy[Save final carry flag at 54H] savecy --> halt1([SJMP HERE: Infinite loop]) ``` ## 4.2 Program Flow — 32-bit Subtraction ```mermaid flowchart TD startNode2([Start: 32-bit Subtraction]) --> clrc[CLR C: Critical step to clear borrow] clrc --> load0s[Load LSB: A ← NUM1 at 30H] load0s --> subb0[SUBB A with NUM2 at 40H
A = A - NUM2 - 0] subb0 --> stores0[Store LSB result at 50H
Borrow propagates via CY] stores0 --> load1s[Load byte 1: A ← NUM1 at 31H] load1s --> subb1[SUBB A with NUM2 at 41H
A = A - NUM2 - CY] subb1 --> stores1[Store byte 1 result at 51H] stores1 --> load2s[Load byte 2: A ← NUM1 at 32H] load2s --> subb2[SUBB A with NUM2 at 42H] subb2 --> stores2[Store byte 2 result at 52H] stores2 --> load3s[Load MSB: A ← NUM1 at 33H] load3s --> subb3[SUBB A with NUM2 at 43H
Final CY=1 indicates NUM2 greater than NUM1] subb3 --> stores3[Store MSB result at 53H] stores3 --> savecy2[Save final borrow at 54H] savecy2 --> halt2([SJMP HERE: Halt]) ``` ## 4.3 8-bit Multiplication Data Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR multA([Operand: A register
Multiplicand 25H]) --> mulInst{MUL AB
Hardware multiplier
4 machine cycles} multB([Operand: B register
Multiplier 10H]) --> mulInst mulInst --> resultLow[Result Low byte
Stored in A: 50H] mulInst --> resultHigh[Result High byte
Stored in B: FEH] mulInst --> memLow[Move to RAM 30H] mulInst --> memHigh[Move to RAM 31H] memLow --> finalRes([Final Result in RAM:
31H = 02H, 30H = 50H
Combined: 0250H]) memHigh --> finalRes ``` ## 4.4 8-bit Division Data Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR divA([Operand: A register
Dividend 64H = 100D]) --> divInst{DIV AB
Hardware divider
4 machine cycles} divB([Operand: B register
Divisor 0AH = 10D]) --> divInst divInst --> quotient[Quotient
Stored in A: 0AH] divInst --> remainder[Remainder
Stored in B: 00H] quotient --> memQ[Move to RAM 30H] remainder --> memR[Move to RAM 31H] memQ --> finalDiv([Final Results in RAM:
30H = 0AH Quotient
31H = 00H Remainder]) memR --> finalDiv ``` ## 4.5 PSW (Program Status Word) Flag Layout — Reference Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR pswReg([PSW Register at SFR D0H]) --> cyBit[Bit 7: CY
Carry Flag] pswReg --> acBit[Bit 6: AC
Auxiliary Carry] pswReg --> rs0Bit[Bit 4: RS0
Register Bank Select 0] pswReg --> rs1Bit[Bit 3: RS1
Register Bank Select 1] pswReg --> ovBit[Bit 2: OV
Overflow Flag] pswReg --> pBit[Bit 0: P
Parity Flag] ``` ## 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Memory Layout ```mermaid flowchart TD ramBlock[Internal RAM 128 bytes
00H to 7FH] --> regBanks[Register Banks 0-3
00H to 1FH] ramBlock --> bitAddr[Bit Addressable Area
20H to 2FH] ramBlock --> genPurpose[General Purpose RAM
30H to 7FH] genPurpose --> num1Area[NUM1 Storage
30H to 33H
LSB at 30H] genPurpose --> num2Area[NUM2 Storage
40H to 43H
LSB at 40H] genPurpose --> resultArea[Result Storage
50H to 54H
LSB at 50H] genPurpose --> mulDivArea[Multiplication Division Storage
30H Quotient or Low
31H Remainder or High] sfrBlock[SFR Area 80H to FFH] --> accReg[Accumulator A at E0H] sfrBlock --> bReg[B Register at F0H] sfrBlock --> pswReg2[PSW at D0H] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Explain the role of the Carry Flag (CY) in 32-bit addition on the 8051. Why is `ADD` used for the LSB and `ADDC` for subsequent bytes?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Carry Flag (CY)** is bit 7 of the PSW register (SFR D0H) and acts as a 1-bit memory that records whether an arithmetic operation generated an overflow beyond the 8-bit boundary. In 32-bit addition on the 8051 (an 8-bit ALU), the result of each byte's addition may produce a carry that must be **propagated** into the next higher byte. The `ADD` instruction performs `A ← A + src` and discards any incoming carry — therefore it is used **only for the LSB** where no prior carry exists. The `ADDC` instruction performs `A ← A + src + CY`, including the carry from the previous byte's addition, and is used for **bytes 1, 2, and 3 (MSB)**. After the MSB operation, if CY = 1, it indicates a **33-bit overflow** (the true result exceeds 32 bits). This carry-propagation mechanism is the foundation of multi-precision arithmetic. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Differentiate between the `MUL AB` and `DIV AB` instructions of the 8051 with respect to operand location, result storage, and flag behavior.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | `MUL AB` | `DIV AB` | |-----------|----------|----------| | Operands | A = multiplicand, B = multiplier | A = dividend, B = divisor | | Operation | Unsigned 8-bit × 8-bit | Unsigned 8-bit ÷ 8-bit | | Result Location | A = low byte, B = high byte | A = quotient, B = remainder | | Maximum Result | FFH × FFH = FE01H (16-bit) | Quotient max = FFH | | CY Flag | Always cleared (0) | Always cleared (0) | | OV Flag | Set if high byte (B) ≠ 0 | Set if divisor (B) = 0 | | Execution Time | 4 machine cycles (4 µs @ 12 MHz) | 4 machine cycles (4 µs @ 12 MHz) | | Error Condition | None (always valid) | Division by zero → OV=1, undefined result | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: (a)** Write an 8051 assembly language program to add two 32-bit unsigned numbers. The first number is stored in internal RAM locations **30H (LSB) to 33H (MSB)** and the second number is stored in **40H (LSB) to 43H (MSB)**. Store the result starting at **50H** and save the final carry in location **54H**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** If the first number is **12345678H** and the second number is **EDCBA987H**, trace the execution and show the contents of all memory locations from 50H to 54H after execution. Explain the role of the CY flag in each step. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — Assembly Program (7 marks) ```assembly ORG 0000H LJMP MAIN ORG 0030H MAIN: MOV A, 30H ; Step 1: Load LSB of NUM1 ADD A, 40H ; Step 2: Add LSB of NUM2 (no incoming carry) MOV 50H, A ; Step 3: Store LSB of result MOV A, 31H ; Step 4: Load byte 1 of NUM1 ADDC A, 41H ; Step 5: Add byte 1 of NUM2 + carry MOV 51H, A ; Step 6: Store byte 1 MOV A, 32H ; Step 7: Load byte 2 of NUM1 ADDC A, 42H ; Step 8: Add byte 2 + carry MOV 52H, A ; Step 9: Store byte 2 MOV A, 33H ; Step 10: Load MSB of NUM1 ADDC A, 43H ; Step 11: Add MSB + carry (final overflow) MOV 53H, A ; Step 12: Store MSB MOV 54H, C ; Step 13: Save final carry flag HERE: SJMP HERE ; Step 14: Halt END ``` **Key Design Decisions (1 mark):** - `ADD` used for LSB (byte 0) since no prior carry exists. - `ADDC` used for bytes 1, 2, 3 to propagate carry from previous byte. - Final carry stored in 54H indicates whether result exceeds 32 bits. --- #### Part (b) — Step-by-Step Execution Trace (7 marks) Given: **NUM1 = 12345678H**, **NUM2 = EDCBA987H** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{NUM1} &= 12\,34\,56\,78_{H} \\ \text{NUM2} &= ED\,CB\,A9\,87_{H} \\ \text{Expected SUM} &= 12\,34\,56\,78_{H} + ED\,CB\,A9\,87_{H} \end{aligned} $$ **Detailed Trace Table:** | Step | Instruction | A (before) | Operand | CY-in | A (after) | CY-out | Memory Update | |------|-------------|------------|---------|-------|-----------|--------|---------------| | 1 | MOV A, 30H | ?? | 78H | x | 78H | x | — | | 2 | ADD A, 40H | 78H | 87H | 0 | FFH | 0 | — | | 3 | MOV 50H, A | FFH | — | 0 | FFH | 0 | 50H ← FFH | | 4 | MOV A, 31H | FFH | 56H | 0 | 56H | 0 | — | | 5 | ADDC A, 41H | 56H | A9H | 0 | FFH | 0 | — | | 6 | MOV 51H, A | FFH | — | 0 | FFH | 0 | 51H ← FFH | | 7 | MOV A, 32H | FFH | 34H | 0 | 34H | 0 | — | | 8 | ADDC A, 42H | 34H | CBH | 0 | FFH | 0 | — | | 9 | MOV 52H, A | FFH | — | 0 | FFH | 0 | 52H ← FFH | | 10 | MOV A, 33H | FFH | 12H | 0 | 12H | 0 | — | | 11 | ADDC A, 43H | 12H | EDH | 0 | FFH | 0 | — | | 12 | MOV 53H, A | FFH | — | 0 | FFH | 0 | 53H ← FFH | | 13 | MOV 54H, C | — | — | 0 | — | 0 | 54H ← 00H | **Verification by Hex Addition:** $$ \begin{aligned} 78_{H} + 87_{H} &= \text{FF}_{H} \quad (\text{CY}=0) \\ 56_{H} + A9_{H} &= \text{FF}_{H} \quad (\text{CY}=0) \\ 34_{H} + CB_{H} &= \text{FF}_{H} \quad (\text{CY}=0) \\ 12_{H} + ED_{H} &= \text{FF}_{H} \quad (\text{CY}=0) \end{aligned} $$ **Final Memory Contents:** - 50H = FFH (LSB) - 51H = FFH - 52H = FFH - 53H = FFH (MSB) - 54H = 00H (no 33-bit overflow) - **Final Result = FFFFFFFFH** ✓ **Role of CY Flag Analysis (2 marks):** In each of the four byte additions, no carry was generated because each column summed to exactly FFH (the maximum 8-bit value). Had any byte sum exceeded FFH (e.g., F8H + 20H = 118H), the CY flag would have been set to 1, and this carry would have been automatically included in the next `ADDC` operation, ensuring correct multi-byte arithmetic. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: (a)** Write an 8051 assembly program to **subtract** two 32-bit unsigned numbers. The minuend is stored at locations **20H–23H** and the subtrahend at **30H–33H**. Store the result at **40H–43H** and indicate a negative result (borrow) by storing FFH at **44H**, otherwise store 00H. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write separate 8051 assembly programs to perform **8-bit unsigned multiplication and division** using the dedicated hardware instructions. Show the contents of all relevant registers after executing with input values **A = F0H, B = 0BH** for multiplication and **A = FEH, B = 05H** for division. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — 32-bit Subtraction Program (7 marks) ```assembly ORG 0000H LJMP MAIN ORG 0030H MAIN: CLR C ; *** CRITICAL: Clear borrow flag *** ; SUBB always subtracts CY as borrow MOV A, 20H ; Load LSB of minuend SUBB A, 30H ; Subtract LSB of subtrahend (and CY=0) MOV 40H, A ; Store result LSB MOV A, 21H ; Load byte 1 SUBB A, 31H ; Subtract with borrow MOV 41H, A ; Store result byte 1 MOV A, 22H ; Load byte 2 SUBB A, 32H ; Subtract with borrow MOV 42H, A ; Store result byte 2 MOV A, 23H ; Load MSB SUBB A, 33H ; Subtract with borrow MOV 43H, A ; Store result MSB JNC POSITIVE ; If CY=0, no borrow, result is positive MOV 44H, #0FFH ; Borrow occurred, mark as negative SJMP DONE POSITIVE: MOV 44H, #00H ; No borrow, positive result DONE: SJMP DONE END ``` **Key Points (1 mark):** - `CLR C` before LSB is mandatory — forgetting it causes an off-by-1 error. - `SUBB` naturally propagates borrow via CY across subsequent bytes. - `JNC` (Jump if No Carry / No Borrow) is used to detect the sign. --- #### Part (b) — 8-bit Multiplication and Division (7 marks) **Multiplication Program (3.5 marks):** ```assembly ORG 0000H LJMP MAIN ORG 0030H MAIN: MOV A, #0F0H ; A = F0H (multiplicand = 240 decimal) MOV B, #0BH ; B = 0BH (multiplier = 11 decimal) MUL AB ; A * B → A=low byte, B=high byte MOV 30H, A ; Store low byte at 30H MOV 31H, B ; Store high byte at 31H HERE: SJMP HERE END ``` **Verification:** $$ \begin{aligned} F0_{H} \times 0B_{H} &= 240_{D} \times 11_{D} \\ &= 2\,640_{D} \\ &= 0A50_{H} \\ &\rightarrow \underbrace{0A_{H}}_{\text{High byte (B)}} : \underbrace{50_{H}}_{\text{Low byte (A)}} \end{aligned} $$ After execution: **A = 50H, B = 0AH, OV = 1** (since high byte ≠ 0), **CY = 0**. Memory: **30H = 50H, 31H = 0AH** ✓ **Division Program (3.5 marks):** ```assembly ORG 0000H LJMP MAIN ORG 0030H MAIN: MOV A, #0FEH ; A = FEH (dividend = 254 decimal) MOV B, #05H ; B = 05H (divisor = 5 decimal) DIV AB ; A / B → quotient in A, remainder in B MOV 30H, A ; Store quotient at 30H MOV 31H, B ; Store remainder at 31H HERE: SJMP HERE END ``` **Verification:** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{FE_{H}}{05_{H}} &= \frac{254_{D}}{5_{D}} \\ &= 50_{D} \text{ remainder } 4_{D} \\ &= 32_{H} \text{ remainder } 04_{H} \end{aligned} $$ After execution: **A = 32H (quotient), B = 04H (remainder), OV = 0, CY = 0**. Memory: **30H = 32H, 31H = 04H** ✓ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:** > > 1. **Using `SUB` instead of `SUBB`** (−2 marks): There is NO `SUB` instruction in 8051. The only subtraction instruction is `SUBB A, src`. Students who write `SUB A, src` lose full credit for that sub-part. > > 2. **Forgetting `CLR C` before 32-bit subtraction** (−1 to 2 marks): The first `SUBB` will subtract an extra 1 (the stale CY value), giving an off-by-1 error in the LSB. This propagates to all higher bytes. > > 3. **Using `ADD` instead of `ADDC` for bytes 1–3** in multi-byte addition (−2 marks): This breaks carry propagation and produces incorrect results whenever an intermediate byte generates a carry. > > 4. **Storing `MOV 30H, B` and `MOV 31H, A` after `MUL AB`** (swapped order): The convention is **A = low byte, B = high byte**. Students who reverse this order lose 1 mark. > > 5. **Not handling division by zero**: If the divisor (B) is 0, the result is undefined and OV=1. The program should ideally check this. Not checking costs 1 mark. > > 6. **Confusing the Carry and Borrow flags**: In 8051, **CY=1 after SUBB indicates a BORROW** (not a carry). Many students misinterpret this in viva examinations. > > 7. **Missing the `END` directive** or missing `SJMP HERE` (infinite loop): These are syntactic requirements that evaluators check first — missing them costs 1 mark each. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:** > > - The 8051 is an **8-bit microcontroller** — its ALU operates on 8-bit data only. > - For multi-byte arithmetic, **carry/borrow propagates** byte-by-byte from LSB to MSB. > - **ADD** vs **ADDC**: `ADD` ignores incoming carry; `ADDC` adds the incoming carry. Use `ADD` for byte 0, `ADDC` for bytes 1, 2, 3. > - **SUBB** is the **only** subtraction instruction — it always subtracts CY as a borrow. Use `CLR C` before the first subtraction. > - **MUL AB**: A × B → **A = low byte, B = high byte**. OV=1 indicates result > 255. > - **DIV AB**: A ÷ B → **A = quotient, B = remainder**. OV=1 indicates divisor was zero. > - The **Carry Flag (CY)** is PSW.7; **Auxiliary Carry (AC)** is PSW.6; **Overflow (OV)** is PSW.2. > - For 32-bit addition, the result range is **00000000H to FFFFFFFFH** (0 to 4,294,967,295). > - Final carry (after MSB ADDC) indicates **33-bit overflow** — store it for full credit. > - Final borrow (after MSB SUBB) indicates the result is **negative** (unsigned underflow). > - Standard Keil µVision simulation procedure: write code → assemble (F7) → debug → view memory window at addresses 30H–54H. > - **Memory Layout Convention**: LSB at the lowest address, MSB at the highest address of the multi-byte variable. > - **Hardware multiplier and divider** complete in just **4 machine cycles** (4 µs at 12 MHz oscillator). > - The B register is **only used** with MUL, DIV, and as a general-purpose register otherwise. > - For decimal/BCD arithmetic, use `DA A` (Decimal Adjust Accumulator) after ADD/ADDC.

topics.title: A Block of Data Transfer from One Memory Location to Another Memory Location (Internal/External Memory)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND IOT LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** A Block of Data Transfer from One Memory Location to Another Memory Location (Internal/External Memory)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Block Data Transfer** in the context of the **8051 Microcontroller** is defined as the systematic, sequential movement of a contiguous group of bytes (a *block*) from a **Source Memory Block** to a **Destination Memory Block** using register-indirect addressing, pointer manipulation (DPTR/R0/R1), and a loop counter. The transfer is governed by addressing mode selection based on whether the source/destination lies in the **Internal Data Memory (IRAM, 00H–7FH / 80H–FFH)** or the **External Data Memory (XRAM, accessed via MOVX instructions)**.

Mathematically, the block transfer operation can be expressed as:

$$
\forall i \in [0, N-1]: \quad \text{MEM}[D_{\text{base}} + i] \leftarrow \text{MEM}[S_{\text{base}} + i]
$$

where $S_{\text{base}}$ is the source base address, $D_{\text{base}}$ is the destination base address, and $N$ is the block length (byte count).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Postal Sorting Room"**
> Imagine a post office where a stack of **100 letters** arrives in **Box A** (Source) and must be reorganized into **Box B** (Destination). A clerk picks up **one letter at a time** from Box A, places it sequentially into Box B, and tracks the count using a **tally counter**. When the counter reaches 100, the clerk stops.
>
> - **Box A** = Source memory location (e.g., `30H` internal or `2000H` external)
> - **Box B** = Destination memory location (e.g., `50H` internal or `3000H` external)
> - **Clerk's hand position** = Pointer (`R0`, `R1`, or `DPTR`)
> - **Tally counter** = Loop counter (`R2`, `R3`, or `R7`)
> - **"Pick, Place, Move, Count"** = The four-step loop body: `READ → WRITE → INCREMENT POINTERS → DECREMENT COUNTER`

This is exactly what the 8051 executes in a tight assembly loop, typically completing in **N × (4–6 machine cycles)** depending on the addressing mode used.

### Standard 8051 Memory Metrics (Bold Constants)

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Internal RAM (8051 standard) | **128** | Bytes (00H–7FH) |
| Internal RAM (8052/Enhanced) | **256** | Bytes (00H–FFH) |
| SFR Region | **128** | Bytes (80H–FFH, special) |
| External Code Memory | **64** | KB max (16-bit PC) |
| External Data Memory | **64** | KB max (16-bit DPTR) |
| Machine Cycle (12 MHz crystal) | **1 µs** | Microsecond |
| Crystal Frequency Standard | **11.0592 MHz** | MHz (serial comms) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The lab experiment explicitly tests your ability to differentiate between **`MOV`** (Internal RAM/SFR), **`MOVC`** (Code Memory via `@A+DPTR` / `@A+PC`), and **`MOVX`** (External Data Memory). Misusing these three instruction families is the **#1 cause of program failure** in this experiment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 8051 Memory Architecture (Why It Matters)

The 8051 has a **Harvard-style segregated memory model** with physically separate address spaces for program code and data. This is fundamentally different from a Von Neumann machine and dictates which instructions can be used for block transfer.

| Memory Space | Size | Addressing Range | Pointer Register | Instruction Family |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Internal RAM (Lower)** | 128 B | 00H–7FH | R0, R1 (indirect) | `MOV` |
| **Internal RAM (Upper / SFR)** | 128 B | 80H–FFH | Direct only | `MOV direct` |
| **External Data Memory (XRAM)** | 64 KB | 0000H–FFFFH | DPTR (16-bit) | `MOVX` |
| **Program Code Memory** | 64 KB | 0000H–FFFFH | DPTR or PC | `MOVC` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for block transfer:** The instruction set is **non-orthogonal** — the same operation "copy a byte" requires different opcodes depending on where the byte lives. This is the central intellectual challenge of Experiment 2.

### 2.2 The Three Block-Transfer Operational Cases

The experiment encompasses **four combinations** of source/destination:

| Case | Source | Destination | Primary Pointer | Instruction Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Internal RAM | Internal RAM | R0, R1 | `MOV A,@R0` / `MOV @R1,A` |
| **2** | Internal RAM | External RAM | R0 + DPTR | `MOVX @DPTR,A` |
| **3** | External RAM | Internal RAM | DPTR + R0 | `MOVX A,@DPTR` |
| **4** | External RAM | External RAM | DPTR (two of them? — see note) | `MOVX` + temporary |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Case 4:** The standard 8051 has **only one 16-bit DPTR** (DPTR0). For external-to-external transfer, you must either (a) save one pointer in two 8-bit registers (DPH in R4, DPL in R5) and rebuild, or (b) use DPTR for one side and R0/R1 + P2 (port 2 high-byte latch) for the other. Enhanced 8051 cores (e.g., **8052 with dual DPTR** — Dallas/Maxim) allow DPTR0/DPTR1 switching via the `AUXR1` register.

### 2.3 Addressing Modes Used in Block Transfer

1. **Register Indirect** — `@R0`, `@R1` for internal RAM (0–127 and 128–255 in some models)
2. **Indexed** — `@A+DPTR`, `@A+PC` for code memory
3. **Immediate** — `#data`, `#data16` for constants and pointer setup
4. **Direct** — `direct` for SFR and high internal RAM
5. **Register** — `Rn` for counter and working data

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **must-memorize** for the KTU lab exam viva and the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

$$
\text{Total Transfer Time} \;=\; N \times T_{\text{mc,avg}} \times \text{Machine Cycle Time}
$$

$$
T_{\text{mc,avg}} \;=\; \frac{1}{f_{\text{osc}}} \times 12 \quad \text{(12 clock periods per machine cycle)}
$$

$$
\text{Memory Size (bytes)} \;=\; 2^{n} \quad \text{where } n = \text{address bus width in bits}
$$

$$
\text{8051 Data Pointer Range} \;=\; 2^{16} \;=\; 65536 \text{ bytes} \;=\; 64 \text{ KB}
$$

$$
\text{Loop iterations remaining} \;=\; R_n \text{ (counter value)}
$$

$$
\text{DJNZ operation: } R_n \leftarrow (R_n - 1); \text{ if } R_n \neq 0 \text{ then jump}
$$

$$
\text{Final DPTR value after block transfer} \;=\; \text{DPTR}_{\text{init}} + N
$$

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| External address from DPTR | `DPH:DPL` (high:low byte) | Hex pair | Construct 16-bit address |
| Counter reload value | `R2 = N` (block size) | Decimal bytes | Set loop length |
| Machine cycle at 11.0592 MHz | $12 / 11.0592\text{ MHz} \approx 1.085\,\mu\text{s}$ | Microseconds | Timing estimation |
| Program memory size | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | Bytes | Verify pointer bounds |
| Data pointer increment | `INC DPTR` (16-bit add) | 1 machine cycle | Traverse blocks |

### 2.5 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Application Context:**
> - **Sensor Data Logging:** Block transfer is used to move a buffer of ADC samples from a temporary array to an external EEPROM/Flash for storage.
> - **Communication Buffers:** In UART/SPI code, incoming bytes are stored in a ring buffer (internal RAM) and then transferred in blocks to a transmission queue.
> - **Display Drivers:** A character/image bitmap is transferred from code memory (via `MOVC`) to a graphics display controller's external memory (via `MOVX`).
> - **Bootloaders:** Firmware updates move code blocks from external Flash to internal execution memory.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: How a Block Transfer Loop Executes

Let us derive the **final memory state** after executing a known block transfer program. We will treat this as a **worked numerical problem** — exactly the type KTU examiners set in the lab ESE.

---

**Problem Statement (Model):**
Write and trace an 8051 assembly program to transfer a block of **10 bytes** from **internal RAM starting at 30H** to **internal RAM starting at 60H**. Assume the source block initially contains: `30H = 11H, 31H = 22H, ..., 39H = AAH`. Determine the contents of the destination block after execution.

---

**Step 1: Initialize the source pointer**

$$
\text{R0} \leftarrow 30\text{H}
$$

> The `MOV R0, #30H` instruction places the byte `30H` into register R0. R0 now serves as the **pointer** to the source. Each `MOV A, @R0` will read the byte at the address currently held in R0.

**Step 2: Initialize the destination pointer**

$$
\text{R1} \leftarrow 60\text{H}
$$

**Step 3: Initialize the loop counter**

$$
\text{R2} \leftarrow 0\text{AH} \quad (= 10 \text{ in decimal})
$$

**Step 4: Enter the loop body — first iteration ($i = 0$)**

| Instruction | Operation | Result |
|---|---|---|
| `MOV A, @R0` | Read memory at R0 (= 30H) | A = 11H |
| `MOV @R1, A` | Write A to memory at R1 (= 60H) | Memory[60H] = 11H |
| `INC R0` | R0 ← R0 + 1 | R0 = 31H |
| `INC R1` | R1 ← R1 + 1 | R1 = 61H |
| `DJNZ R2, LOOP` | R2 ← R2 − 1; if R2 ≠ 0 jump | R2 = 09H, jump back |

**Step 5: General iteration formula**

For iteration $i$ (where $i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{A} &\leftarrow \text{MEM}[S_{\text{base}} + i] \\
\text{MEM}[D_{\text{base}} + i] &\leftarrow \text{A} \\
\text{R0}_{i+1} &= \text{R0}_i + 1 \\
\text{R1}_{i+1} &= \text{R1}_i + 1 \\
\text{R2}_{i+1} &= \text{R2}_i - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Final iteration ($i = 9$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{A} &\leftarrow \text{MEM}[30\text{H} + 9] = \text{MEM}[39\text{H}] = \text{AAH} \\
\text{MEM}[60\text{H} + 9] = \text{MEM}[69\text{H}] &\leftarrow \text{AAH} \\
\text{R0}_{10} &= 3\text{AH}, \quad \text{R1}_{10} = 6\text{AH}, \quad \text{R2}_{10} = 00\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Loop termination check**

$$
\text{R2} = 00\text{H} \implies \text{DJNZ does NOT jump} \implies \text{Program falls through to next instruction}
$$

**Step 8: Final destination block contents**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MEM}[60\text{H}] &= 11\text{H}, \quad \text{MEM}[61\text{H}] = 22\text{H} \\
\text{MEM}[62\text{H}] &= 33\text{H}, \quad \text{MEM}[63\text{H}] = 44\text{H} \\
\text{MEM}[64\text{H}] &= 55\text{H}, \quad \text{MEM}[65\text{H}] = 66\text{H} \\
\text{MEM}[66\text{H}] &= 77\text{H}, \quad \text{MEM}[67\text{H}] = 88\text{H} \\
\text{MEM}[68\text{H}] &= 99\text{H}, \quad \text{MEM}[69\text{H}] = \text{AAH}
\end{aligned}
$$

The source block at `30H–39H` is **unchanged** (block copy, not block move). The pointers `R0` and `R1` both point **one past the end** of their respective blocks.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Variant

The following decision matrix is what you should be able to reproduce from memory in the exam:

| Transfer Type | Source Address Setup | Dest Address Setup | Loop Read | Loop Write | Counter Reg |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| IRAM → IRAM | `MOV R0, #S` | `MOV R1, #D` | `MOV A, @R0` | `MOV @R1, A` | R2 |
| IRAM → XRAM | `MOV R0, #S` | `MOV DPTR, #D16` | `MOV A, @R0` | `MOVX @DPTR, A` | R2 |
| XRAM → IRAM | `MOV DPTR, #S16` | `MOV R0, #D` | `MOVX A, @DPTR` | `MOV @R0, A` | R2 |
| XRAM → XRAM | `MOV DPTR, #S16` (use P2 for dest MSB) | `MOV R0, #D_low; SETB P2.x` | `MOVX A, @DPTR` | `MOVX @R0, A` | R2 |
| Code → IRAM | `MOV DPTR, #S16` | `MOV R0, #D` | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | `MOV @R0, A` | R2 |

---

### 3.3 Reference Assembly Programs (Full Listings)

#### **Program A: Internal RAM → Internal RAM (10 bytes from 30H to 50H)**

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     R0, #30H        ; R0 = source pointer
        MOV     R1, #50H        ; R1 = destination pointer
        MOV     R2, #0AH        ; R2 = counter (10)
LOOP:   MOV     A, @R0          ; A <- MEM[R0]
        MOV     @R1, A          ; MEM[R1] <- A
        INC     R0              ; source++
        INC     R1              ; dest++
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP        ; if (--R2 != 0) goto LOOP
HERE:   SJMP    HERE            ; halt (infinite loop)
        END
```

#### **Program B: External RAM → Internal RAM (20 bytes from 0000H to 60H)**

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     DPTR, #0000H    ; DPTR = external source
        MOV     R0, #60H        ; R0 = internal destination
        MOV     R2, #14H        ; R2 = 20 decimal
LOOP:   MOVX    A, @DPTR        ; read external byte
        MOV     @R0, A          ; write to internal
        INC     DPTR            ; next external address
        INC     R0              ; next internal address
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP
HERE:   SJMP    HERE
        END
```

#### **Program C: Internal RAM → External RAM (16 bytes from 40H to 2000H)**

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     R0, #40H        ; R0 = internal source
        MOV     DPTR, #2000H    ; DPTR = external destination
        MOV     R2, #10H        ; R2 = 16 decimal
LOOP:   MOV     A, @R0          ; read internal
        MOVX    @DPTR, A        ; write external
        INC     R0
        INC     DPTR
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP
HERE:   SJMP    HERE
        END
```

#### **Program D: External RAM → External RAM (Dual DPTR — Enhanced 8052)**

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     DPTR, #0000H    ; DPTR0 = source
        MOV     R4, #DPL_dest   ; save dest low byte
        MOV     R5, #DPH_dest   ; save dest high byte
        MOV     R2, #N          ; counter
LOOP:   MOVX    A, @DPTR        ; read from source
        PUSH    DPL
        PUSH    DPH
        MOV     DPL, R4
        MOV     DPH, R5
        MOVX    @DPTR, A        ; write to dest
        POP     DPH
        POP     DPL
        INC     DPTR
        INC     R4
        CJNE    R4, #00H, SKIP
        INC     R5              ; carry into high byte
SKIP:   DJNZ    R2, LOOP
HERE:   SJMP    HERE
        END
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Trap:** Program D is **not** the "standard" expected answer for an 8051 exam — the standard 8051 has only one DPTR. For the typical KTU viva/ESE, use the **P2 latch trick**: latch the destination high byte into Port 2 (which becomes the upper 8 address lines for external memory), then use R0/R1 for the low byte. `MOVX @R0, A` will then access address `P2:R0`.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 8051 Memory Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cpu["8051 CPU CORE"]
    prog["PROGRAM MEMORY\n64 KB Code\n0000H - FFFFH\nAccessed via MOVC"]
    iram_lo["INTERNAL RAM LOWER\n128 Bytes 00H - 7FH\n4 Register Banks R0-R7\nBit-Addressable 20H-2FH\nGeneral Purpose 30H-7FH"]
    iram_hi["INTERNAL RAM UPPER / SFR\n128 Bytes 80H - FFH\nSFRs Port Latches Timers"]
    xram["EXTERNAL DATA MEMORY\n64 KB 0000H - FFFFH\nAccessed via MOVX\nHigh address via P2\nLow address via P0 or R0/R1"]
    stack["HARDWARE STACK\nGrows Upward\nSP points to top"]
    romcode["KEIL / ASSEMBLER\n.INC FILES\nVector Table at 0000H"]

    cpu --> romcode
    cpu --> prog
    cpu --> iram_lo
    cpu --> iram_hi
    cpu --> xram
    cpu --> stack
```

### 4.2 Block Transfer Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([START]) --> initSrc[Load Source Pointer R0 or DPTR]
    initSrc --> initDst[Load Destination Pointer R1 or DPTR]
    initDst --> initCnt[Load Counter R2 with N bytes]
    initCnt --> loopRead[LOOP: Read source byte into A]
    loopRead --> loopWrite[Write A to destination]
    loopWrite --> incPtr1[Increment source pointer]
    incPtr1 --> incPtr2[Increment destination pointer]
    incPtr2 --> decCnt[Decrement counter DJNZ R2]
    decCnt --> checkCnt{Counter equals 0?}
    checkCnt -->|NO| loopRead
    checkCnt -->|YES| halt[HALT - Block transfer complete]
    halt --> endNode([END])
```

### 4.3 Block-Transfer Functional Architecture (P2 Latch + R0 Method for XRAM↔XRAM)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC["SOURCE EXTERNAL BLOCK XRAM 0000H-0009H"]
        s1["00: 11H"]
        s2["01: 22H"]
        s3["02: 33H"]
        sN["..."]
        s10["09: AAH"]
    end

    subgraph TMP["TEMPORARY ACCUMULATOR"]
        acc["A Register\n8-bit working register\nHolds the byte in transit"]
    end

    subgraph DST["DESTINATION EXTERNAL BLOCK XRAM 4000H-4009H"]
        d1["00: xx"]
        d2["01: xx"]
        d3["02: xx"]
        dN["..."]
        d10["09: xx"]
    end

    s1 -- "MOVX A @DPTR" --> acc
    acc -- "MOVX @R0 A via P2 latch" --> d1
    s2 -- "INC DPTR" --> acc
    acc --> d2
```

### 4.4 Addressing Mode Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Memory Type?}
    q1 -->|Internal RAM 00-7FH| q2{Source or Dest?}
    q1 -->|Internal RAM 80-FFH SFR| directAddr["Use DIRECT addressing\nMOV direct, A"]
    q1 -->|External XRAM| xramUse["Use DPTR for full 16-bit\nOr R0/R1 with P2 high-byte"]
    q1 -->|Code ROM| movcUse["Use MOVC A @A+DPTR\nor MOVC A @A+PC"]

    q2 -->|Source| movR0["MOV A, @R0\nR0 holds 8-bit address"]
    q2 -->|Destination| movR1["MOV @R1, A\nR1 holds 8-bit address"]
```

### 4.5 Memory Layout Map (Concrete Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INTMEM["INTERNAL DATA MEMORY 8051"]
        r0["00H-1FH: Register Banks RB0-RB3\nDefault bank: Bank 0 at 00H-07H"]
        bit["20H-2FH: 16 bytes bit-addressable\n128 bits 00-7F"]
        gen["30H-7FH: General Purpose RAM\nScratch / variables / buffers"]
        sfr["80H-FFH: SFRs\nP0 P1 P2 P3\nTCON TMOD TH0 TL0\nSP PCON etc"]
    end

    subgraph XTRAM["EXTERNAL DATA MEMORY"]
        lowpage["0000H-00FFH: Page 0\nOften external RAM chip"]
        user1["0100H-1FFFH: User data 1"]
        user2["2000H-3FFFH: User data 2"]
        io["4000H-7FFFH: Memory-mapped I/O"]
        hram["8000H-FFFFH: High region"]
    end
```

### 4.6 Program Execution State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Init
    Init: Initialization\nLoad pointers\nLoad counter
    Init --> Read
    Read: Read Byte\nMOVX A, @DPTR\nor MOV A, @R0
    Read --> Write
    Write: Write Byte\nMOVX @DPTR, A\nor MOV @R1, A
    Write --> IncPtr
    IncPtr: Increment Pointers\nINC R0 / INC DPTR
    IncPtr --> CheckCnt
    CheckCnt: Check Counter\nDJNZ R2 LOOP
    CheckCnt --> Read: R2 not zero
    CheckCnt --> Halt: R2 equals zero
    Halt: Halt\nSJMP HERE
    Halt --> [*]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]**  
> Differentiate between `MOV`, `MOVC`, and `MOVX` instructions in the 8051 with respect to memory access.

**Model Answer (3 marks — full marks distribution):**
- `MOV` (1 mark): Used for accessing **internal data memory** (RAM 00H–7FH and SFRs 80H–FFH). Operates on the register file, bit-addressable area, and general-purpose RAM.
- `MOVC` (1 mark): "Move Constant" — used to read from **program code memory (ROM)**. Indexed addressing only, with forms `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` and `MOVC A, @A+PC`.
- `MOVX` (1 mark): "Move External" — used for the **external data memory space** (64 KB XRAM). Uses `@DPTR` (16-bit) or `@R0/@R1` (8-bit) with P2 providing the high byte.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]**  
> Why is `R0` (or `R1`) used as a pointer in 8051 block transfer rather than a direct address?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Indirect addressing enables loops** (1.5 marks): A direct address is fixed at assembly time, so a loop cannot traverse successive memory locations without re-assembling. `@R0` dereferences whatever address is in R0, which can be `INC`-remented inside the loop.
2. **Code compactness and reusability** (1 mark): The same two instructions (`MOV A,@R0` and `MOV @R1,A`) handle any N-byte block, parameterized only by initial pointer values and counter.
3. **R0/R1 are the only registers that can be used for indirect internal RAM** (0.5 mark): R2–R7 do not support `@Rn` addressing for internal RAM reads/writes.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> The two most common **mark-losing errors** in block transfer programs are:
> 1. **Forgetting `INC DPTR` or `INC R0`** — the loop copies the *same byte* over and over to *successive* destination addresses. This is worth **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Using `MOV` for external memory** — `MOV` cannot access XRAM. Always use `MOVX`. This mistake can cost **−3 to −4 marks** if the entire program is functionally broken.
> 3. **Not initializing the counter** — the program loops 256 times (initial R2 = 0 = 256 iterations after DJNZ) or infinite loops. **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Missing `END` directive** — assembler may generate garbage. **−0.5 mark** but always include it.
> 5. **Mixing up DPTR setup with two separate `MOV DPH` / `MOV DPL` instructions in wrong order** — common viva pitfall.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — IRAM → XRAM Transfer**

> **Q.A [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Set B]**  
> **(a)** [7 marks] Write an 8051 ALP to transfer a block of **15 bytes** from internal RAM location **40H–4EH** to external RAM starting at address **5000H**. Draw the flowchart.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] If the crystal frequency is **11.0592 MHz**, calculate the **total execution time** of the transfer. State the contents of DPTR and R0 after the loop terminates.

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Algorithm (1 mark):**
1. Initialize R0 ← 40H (source pointer)
2. Initialize DPTR ← 5000H (destination pointer)
3. Initialize R2 ← 0FH (counter = 15)
4. Repeat: Read internal → write external → increment both → decrement counter
5. Halt

**Program (4 marks):**
```asm
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     R0, #40H        ; source pointer
        MOV     DPTR, #5000H    ; destination pointer
        MOV     R2, #0FH        ; counter = 15
LOOP:   MOV     A, @R0          ; read internal
        MOVX    @DPTR, A        ; write external
        INC     R0              ; next source
        INC     DPTR            ; next destination
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP        ; loop if not done
HERE:   SJMP    HERE
        END
```

**Flowchart (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.2 (Block Transfer Algorithm Flowchart) — full marks if all 5 steps (init pointers, init counter, read, write, increment, check) are shown in correct order with decision diamond for counter check.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Count machine cycles per loop iteration (3 marks)**

| Instruction | Bytes | Machine Cycles |
|---|---|---|
| `MOV A, @R0` | 1 | 1 |
| `MOVX @DPTR, A` | 1 | 2 |
| `INC R0` | 1 | 1 |
| `INC DPTR` | 1 | 2 |
| `DJNZ R2, LOOP` (taken) | 2 | 2 |
| **Total per iteration** | — | **8** |

**Step 2: Total cycles for 15 iterations (1 mark)**

$$
\text{Total cycles} = 15 \times 8 = 120 \text{ machine cycles}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to time using crystal frequency (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{machine cycle}} &= \frac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \\
&= 1.085\,\mu\text{s per machine cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= 120 \times 1.085\,\mu\text{s} \\
&= 130.2\,\mu\text{s} \\
&\approx 0.13 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Final register values (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R0}_{\text{final}} &= 40\text{H} + 15 = 4\text{FH} \\
\text{DPTR}_{\text{final}} &= 5000\text{H} + 15 = 500\text{FH} \\
\text{R2}_{\text{final}} &= 00\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — XRAM → IRAM Transfer (Internal Choice)**

> **Q.B [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Set A]**  
> **(a)** [7 marks] Write an 8051 ALP to transfer a block of **20 bytes** from external RAM starting at address **2000H** to internal RAM starting at **60H**. Explain the role of each instruction.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] Modify the program to transfer a block from external RAM **0000H–00FFH (256 bytes)** to internal RAM **30H–12FH**, and explain how you handle the situation where the destination exceeds the standard 128-byte internal RAM boundary.

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Program (3 marks):**
```asm
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     DPTR, #2000H    ; source = external 2000H
        MOV     R0, #60H        ; destination = internal 60H
        MOV     R2, #14H        ; counter = 20 decimal
LOOP:   MOVX    A, @DPTR        ; A = external[DPTR]
        MOV     @R0, A          ; internal[R0] = A
        INC     DPTR
        INC     R0
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP
HERE:   SJMP    HERE
        END
```

**Explanation of each instruction (4 marks — 0.5 each):**
- `MOV DPTR, #2000H` — loads the 16-bit external address into DPTR; required for MOVX
- `MOV R0, #60H` — loads the 8-bit internal address into R0; used for indirect internal access
- `MOV R2, #14H` — R2 holds the byte count; DJNZ decrements and tests
- `MOVX A, @DPTR` — reads one byte from external data memory at DPTR into A (2 cycles)
- `MOV @R0, A` — writes A to internal RAM at the address in R0 (1 cycle)
- `INC DPTR` — increments the 16-bit pointer (handles carry from low to high byte automatically)
- `INC R0` — increments the 8-bit pointer (wraps from FFH to 00H if not stopped)
- `DJNZ R2, LOOP` — decrement R2; jump to LOOP if result is non-zero

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**The Problem (1.5 marks):**
Internal RAM is only **128 bytes** (00H–7FH). A 256-byte block at `30H–12FH` wraps around internal RAM (30H–7FH = 128 bytes, then continues at 00H–0FH, overwriting registers and stack). Moreover, the **standard 8051 cannot access internal RAM 80H–FFH indirectly via R0** — only the lower 128 bytes are R0/R1-indirect accessible.

**Solution A — Limit block to 128 bytes (2 marks):**

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     DPTR, #0000H
        MOV     R0, #30H
        MOV     R2, #00H        ; 256 decimal = 00H (DJNZ starts from 00H = 256)
LOOP:   MOVX    A, @DPTR
        MOV     @R0, A
        INC     DPTR
        INC     R0
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP
HERE:   SJMP    HERE
        END
```

> Note: `MOV R2, #00H` followed by `DJNZ` decrements to `FFH`, `FEH`, ... → **256 iterations**. This is a classic 8051 idiom.

**Solution B — Use SFR direct addressing for upper half (3.5 marks):**

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     DPTR, #0000H
        MOV     R0, #30H
        MOV     R2, #80H        ; first half: 128 bytes to lower RAM
LOOP1:  MOVX    A, @DPTR
        MOV     @R0, A
        INC     DPTR
        INC     R0
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP1
        ; R0 has wrapped to 00H — switch strategy
        MOV     R2, #80H        ; second half: 128 bytes to upper RAM 80H-FFH
LOOP2:  MOVX    A, @DPTR
        MOV     R0, #80H        ; base of upper RAM (direct address required)
        MOV     @R0, A          ; write to 80H
        INC     R0              ; direct addressing is INCR not indirect here
        ; This naive version is WRONG — re-examine:
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Self-correction warning:** Writing to internal RAM 80H–FFH requires **direct addressing** (`MOV 80H, A` etc.) because `@R0` cannot reach it. The correct code uses an offset into a direct-addressed location. Many students lose 2 marks here by trying to use indirect addressing for the upper 128 bytes.

**Correct Solution B (3.5 marks):**

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     DPTR, #0000H
        MOV     R0, #30H
        MOV     R2, #50H        ; 80 bytes lower
LOOP1:  MOVX    A, @DPTR
        MOV     @R0, A
        INC     DPTR
        INC     R0
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP1
        ; Now continue with direct addressing for upper RAM 80H-FFH
        MOV     R2, #80H        ; 128 bytes upper
        MOV     R0, #80H        ; base
LOOP2:  MOVX    A, @DPTR
        MOV     @R0, A          ; writes to address 80H+offset
        INC     DPTR
        INC     R0
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP2
HERE:   SJMP    HERE
        END
```

The trick: `INC R0` works on R0 itself, and `MOV @R0, A` writes to internal RAM at that R0 value. For the **lower 128 bytes**, this is pure indirect access. For the **upper 128 bytes (80H–FFH)** on enhanced 8052 cores, R0 indirect access is valid (the MOV instruction reinterprets the SFR/upper-RAM address automatically based on the addressing mode bit).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Lab Exam (Experiment 2)**

- ☐ **Three instruction families:**
  - `MOV` → Internal RAM + SFR (00H–7FH direct/indirect, 80H–FFH direct only)
  - `MOVC` → Code memory (read-only, indexed)
  - `MOVX` → External data memory (DPTR or R0/R1 + P2)
- ☐ **Pointer registers:** R0, R1 for internal; DPTR (16-bit) for external/code
- ☐ **Counter register:** Any R2–R7 (commonly R2 or R7); loaded with block size N
- ☐ **Loop primitive:** `DJNZ Rn, label` — decrement, test, conditional jump (2 machine cycles)
- ☐ **Standard loop body order:** READ → WRITE → INC source → INC dest → DEC counter
- ☐ **Block size for 256 bytes:** Use `MOV R2, #00H` then `DJNZ` — it decrements to FFH and gives 256 iterations
- ☐ **External memory high-byte address:** Latch into P2 before `MOVX @R0, A`; do not change P2 inside the loop
- ☐ **Enhanced 8052 dual DPTR:** Toggle `AUXR1` bit 0 to switch DPTR0 ↔ DPTR1
- ☐ **Standard program skeleton:** `ORG 0000H` … `END`; conclude with `HERE: SJMP HERE` for halt
- ☐ **Time calculation:** $T = N \times \text{cycles/iter} \times 12 / f_{\text{osc}}$
- ☐ **Final pointer values:** Both source and destination pointers are **one past the last byte transferred** (`base + N`)
- ☐ **Common exam traps to avoid:** Using MOV instead of MOVX for external; forgetting INC; wrong DJNZ target; missing END
- ☐ **Viva favorites:** Why DPTR is 16-bit (64 KB addressing); difference between `MOVC A,@A+PC` and `@A+DPTR`; role of PSW register-bank bits in R0/R1 mapping
- ☐ **Industrial context to mention:** "This block transfer is the foundation of all DMA-style data movement in embedded systems, used in sensor buffering, bootloader code movement, and display refresh."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sorting of an Array of Numbers Stored in the Memory Location (Ascending/Descending Order)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRUL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND IOT LABORATORY > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Sorting of an Array of Numbers Stored in the Memory Location (Ascending/Descending Order) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Array Sorting in Memory** is the process of rearranging a finite, contiguous sequence of data elements (bytes/words) stored sequentially in the internal/external Random Access Memory (RAM) of a microcontroller into a specified logical order — either **Ascending Order** (smallest value at the lowest memory address, largest at the highest) or **Descending Order** (largest at the lowest address, smallest at the highest) — by means of repeated pairwise comparison and conditional data swapping, executed through an iterative algorithm such as **Bubble Sort**, **Selection Sort**, or **Insertion Sort**. In the KTU **PCRUL408 (Microcontrollers and IoT Laboratory)** context, the sorting routine is typically implemented in **8051 Assembly Language (ASM51)**, where the array resides in the **Internal RAM (Data Memory: 0x00 – 0x7F)** or **External Data Memory (XDATA: 0x0000 – 0xFFFF)**, and is referenced indirectly using the **Data Pointer (DPTR)** or the **R0/R1 registers** with the `@Rn` indirect addressing mode. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For PCRUL408, the **Bubble Sort algorithm** is the prescribed sorting technique because it (a) requires only register-level operations, (b) uses the in-place memory swap primitive (`XCH` / `XCHD`), and (c) maps cleanly onto the 8051's **two-pointer indirect addressing** capability. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Bubble-Up" Intuition Imagine **5 transparent water-filled bottles** placed on a shelf, each containing a numbered cork floating inside. The shelf has 5 slots numbered 0 to 4 (these are your **memory addresses**). The bottle at slot 0 currently holds a cork with the number **45**, slot 1 holds **12**, slot 2 holds **78**, slot 3 holds **23**, slot 4 holds **56**. You want the numbers to "bubble up" like air bubbles in water — the **smallest cork** rising to slot 0, the **largest** sinking to slot 4. So you walk along the shelf from left to right. At each step, you **pick up two adjacent bottles, compare the corks inside**, and if the left cork is heavier (larger number), you **swap their positions**. You keep walking back and forth until no more swaps are needed — meaning everything is in the right order. This is **Bubble Sort**: the larger (or smaller) elements "bubble" toward their final positions after each full pass. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Vocabulary for KTU Viva:** > - **Array (Vector):** A contiguous block of `N` memory locations, each holding one data element of the same size (typically 1 byte for 8051). > - **Pass:** One complete traversal of the array from the first element to the last. > - **Swap:** An atomic exchange of two memory contents using `XCH A, @R0` or via a temporary register. > - **Sorted Flag (SWAPPED):** A bit/flag (often `00H` of the bit-addressable RAM) used to detect early termination when no swaps occurred in a pass. ### Standard Metrics (KTU Lab Standards) | Metric | Standard Value (8051) | |---|---| | Register Width | **8 bits** | | Addressable Internal RAM | **128 bytes (0x00 – 0x7F)** | | Bit-Addressable Region | **0x20 – 0x2F (16 bytes = 128 bits)** | | Typical Array Size in Lab | **5 to 10 elements** | | Clock Cycle per `XCH` | **12 oscillator cycles** (standard 8051) | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Bubble Sort Logic Flow Bubble Sort works on the principle of **iterative adjacent comparison and conditional swap**. The high-level algorithm for an array `A[0..N-1]` of size `N` sorted in **ascending order** is: **Step 1 — Initialization:** Load the array size `N` into a counter (e.g., `R7`). Initialize a "swapped in this pass" flag (e.g., bit `00H` of `20H`) to `0` (false). Set the pass counter (e.g., `R6`) to `N-1`. **Step 2 — Outer Loop (Pass Control):** For each pass `i` from `0` to `N-2`, do Steps 3–6. This is implemented in 8051 as a `DJNZ` (Decrement and Jump if Not Zero) loop on `R6`. **Step 3 — Reset Swap Flag:** At the start of each new pass, clear the swap flag (`CLR 00H`). This flag will tell us whether any swap happened — if not, the array is already sorted and we can terminate early. **Step 4 — Inner Loop (Pairwise Compare):** Point `R0` (indirect address pointer) to the **first element** of the array (`A[0]`). Set the inner-loop counter `R5` to `N-1-i` (the number of comparisons needed in pass `i`). For each `j` from `0` to `N-2-i`, do Step 5. **Step 5 — Compare & Conditional Swap:** - Load the current element into the Accumulator: `MOV A, @R0` (loads `A[j]`). - Increment `R0`: `INC R0`. - Compare with the next element: `CJNE A, @R0, NO_SWAP` (this jumps if `A[j] ≠ A[j+1]`). - **For Ascending:** If `A[j] > A[j+1]`, swap. This is implemented using the carry flag: after `CJNE`, if `CY = 1`, then `A[j] < A[j+1]`, so no swap; if `CY = 0`, then `A[j] > A[j+1]`, so swap. - **For Descending:** Reverse the condition — swap if `A[j] < A[j+1]`. **Step 6 — Repeat Inner Loop:** Decrement `R5` and if not zero, jump back to Step 5: `DJNZ R5, INNER_LOOP`. **Step 7 — Early Termination Check:** After the inner loop, check the swap flag. If no swap occurred (flag still `0`), the array is sorted — `JNB 00H, SORTED` jumps to the end. **Step 8 — Decrement Pass Counter and Repeat:** `DJNZ R6, OUTER_LOOP`. After `N-1` passes, the array is fully sorted. ### 2.2 The `XCH` Swap Primitive — Atomic In-Place Exchange The 8051's **`XCH A, @Rn`** (Exchange Accumulator with the byte addressed by Rn) is the canonical swap mechanism. It performs an atomic exchange between the Accumulator and a memory location in **one instruction cycle category (12 clock cycles)**. For an in-place swap of two memory bytes `M[X]` and `M[X+1]` where `R0 = X`: ```asm MOV A, @R0 ; A ← M[X] (save first element) INC R0 ; R0 ← X+1 XCH A, @R0 ; A ↔ M[X+1] (atomic swap, A now holds M[X+1]) DEC R0 ; R0 ← X XCH A, @R0 ; A ↔ M[X] (atomic swap, A now holds M[X] original, M[X] now holds original M[X+1]) ``` After these 4 instructions, `M[X]` and `M[X+1]` have been exchanged, and the accumulator holds the original `M[X]` value. Total cost: **48 clock cycles per swap**. ### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step | Step | Why It Exists | |---|---| | Outer loop on `R6 = N-1` | Mathematically guarantees that after `N-1` passes, the array is sorted even in the worst case (reverse-sorted input). | | Inner loop bound `N-1-i` | After pass `i`, the last `i` elements are already in their final positions (they "bubbled" to the end), so we don't need to re-examine them. | | Swap flag with early termination | Best-case optimization — an already-sorted array (e.g., `1, 2, 3, 4, 5`) finishes in **1 pass** instead of 4. | | `CJNE` + Carry Flag trick | `CJNE` is the only 8051 instruction that simultaneously **compares two bytes and sets the Carry flag** based on magnitude — perfect for branching on `<` vs `≥`. | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet | Element | Symbol / Opcode | Purpose | Clock Cycles | |---|---|---|---| | Indirect Load | `MOV A, @R0` | Read array element at address in `R0` | **12** | | Indirect Store | `MOV @R0, A` | Write accumulator to array element | **12** | | Atomic Swap | `XCH A, @R0` | Exchange `A` with memory at `R0` | **12** | | Compare & Branch | `CJNE A, @R0, rel` | Sets `CY=1` if `A < @R0`, else `CY=0` | **24** | | Decrement & Loop | `DJNZ Rn, rel` | Decrement `Rn`, jump if not zero | **24** | | Bit Test & Jump | `JB bit, rel` / `JNB bit, rel` | Branch on a bit in `20H–2FH` | **24** | | Pass Counter | `R6` (typically) | Holds `N-1` for outer loop | — | | Inner Counter | `R5` (typically) | Holds `N-1-i` for inner loop | — | | Address Pointer | `R0` (typically) | Indirect pointer to current array element | — | | Swap Flag | Bit `00H` (i.e., byte `20H`, bit 0) | Set when any swap occurs in a pass | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examination Tip:** You will be asked to write the assembly program on your own. Memorize the `XCH`-based swap block and the `CJNE`+carry-flag trick — these two primitives are the **only** non-trivial logic in the entire experiment. Everything else is just loop scaffolding. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Bubble sort in a microcontroller context is not used for performance — it is used for **pedagogy and small-scale embedded tasks**: - **Sensor Data Ranking:** Sorting the last 10 temperature readings to find min/max/median for a thermostatically controlled HVAC system. - **Median Filtering:** Removing spike noise in ADC readings by taking the median of the last 5 samples (requires sorting). - **Actuator Priority Scheduling:** In multi-actuator embedded systems, sorting pending tasks by urgency flag before servicing. - **Calibration Tables:** Sorting raw calibration coefficients in ascending magnitude before interpolation. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Trace — Ascending Sort on a Sample Array **Input Array** (stored in internal RAM starting at address `30H`): | Address | `30H` | `31H` | `32H` | `33H` | `34H` | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Value | `45` | `12` | `78` | `23` | `56` | | Index | `0` | `1` | `2` | `3` | `4` | So `N = 5`, `R6 = 4` (outer loop counter), `R5 = 4` initially (inner loop counter). **Pass 1** (i = 0, R5 will run from 4 down to 1, i.e., 4 comparisons): | Step | `R0` (points to) | `A` after `MOV A,@R0` | `@R0+1` | `CJNE` result | Action | Array State | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `30H` (`45`) | `45` | `12` | `CY=0` (since `45 > 12`) | **SWAP** | `[12, 45, 78, 23, 56]` | | 2 | `31H` (`45`) | `45` | `78` | `CY=1` (since `45 < 78`) | No swap | `[12, 45, 78, 23, 56]` | | 3 | `32H` (`78`) | `78` | `23` | `CY=0` (since `78 > 23`) | **SWAP** | `[12, 45, 23, 78, 56]` | | 4 | `33H` (`78`) | `78` | `56` | `CY=0` (since `78 > 56`) | **SWAP** | `[12, 45, 23, 56, 78]` | End of Pass 1: `78` has "bubbled" to its final position at index 4. Swap flag = 1. **R5 decremented 4 times → R5 = 0 → exit inner loop. R6 = 3.** **Pass 2** (i = 1, R5 runs from 3 down to 1, i.e., 3 comparisons): | Step | `R0` | `A` | `@R0+1` | `CJNE` | Action | Array State | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `30H` | `12` | `45` | `CY=1` | No swap | `[12, 45, 23, 56, 78]` | | 2 | `31H` | `45` | `23` | `CY=0` | **SWAP** | `[12, 23, 45, 56, 78]` | | 3 | `32H` | `45` | `56` | `CY=1` | No swap | `[12, 23, 45, 56, 78]` | End of Pass 2: `56` bubbled to index 3. Swap flag = 1. **R6 = 2.** **Pass 3** (i = 2, R5 = 2, 2 comparisons): | Step | `R0` | `A` | `@R0+1` | `CJNE` | Action | Array State | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `30H` | `12` | `23` | `CY=1` | No swap | `[12, 23, 45, 56, 78]` | | 2 | `31H` | `23` | `45` | `CY=1` | No swap | `[12, 23, 45, 56, 78]` | End of Pass 3: No swap! Swap flag stays 0. The `JNB 00H, SORTED` test **triggers early termination**, skipping Pass 4 entirely. The array is now sorted in ascending order: **`[12, 23, 45, 56, 78]`** stored at addresses `30H` through `34H`. ### 3.2 The "Why" — Mathematical Justification of the Early Termination After **Pass 3**, we made 2 comparisons and **0 swaps**. This means **every adjacent pair is already in correct order** — i.e., `A[0] ≤ A[1] ≤ A[2] ≤ A[3] ≤ A[4]`. By the **transitive property of inequality**, the entire array is sorted. Hence the early-exit condition is mathematically valid, and we save `N-3 = 2` more comparisons and 1 full pass. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Ascending vs. Descending Logic | Aspect | Ascending Sort | Descending Sort | |---|---|---| | **Goal** | Smallest at low address | Largest at low address | | **`CJNE A, @R0, ...` Carry Flag `CY=1` means** | `A < @R0` (correct, no swap) | `A > @R0` (correct, no swap) | | **Swap Condition (in code)** | Swap if `CY = 0` (i.e., `A ≥ @R0`) | Swap if `CY = 1` (i.e., `A < @R0`) | | **Logical Operator** | `JNC SKIP_SWAP` (Jump if No Carry → don't skip → swap) | `JC SKIP_SWAP` (Jump if Carry → don't skip → swap) | | **Result on same input** | `[12, 23, 45, 56, 78]` | `[78, 56, 45, 23, 12]` | | **Code Difference** | Only **one conditional jump instruction** changes | — | ### 3.4 Exhaustive Memory Layout & Register Map (during execution) | Register | Role | Initial Value | Changes During Execution | |---|---|---|---| | `R0` | Indirect pointer to current array element | `30H` | Increments by 1 after each comparison; reset to `30H` at start of each pass | | `R5` | Inner loop counter (comparisons remaining) | `N-1` | Decremented by `DJNZ R5, ...`; reloaded with `N-1-i` at start of each pass | | `R6` | Outer loop counter (passes remaining) | `N-1` | Decremented by `DJNZ R6, ...` | | `R7` | Array size `N` (read-only) | `05H` | Unchanged | | `A` | Accumulator — temporary holding register | — | Holds each compared value; holds swap intermediate | | `20H.0` (bit `00H`) | Swap flag | `0` (CLR at start of each pass) | Set to `1` by `SETB 00H` on every swap | ### 3.5 The Exhaustive Ascending Sort Assembly Program (8051, ASM51) ```asm ORG 0000H LJMP MAIN ORG 0030H ARRAY: DB 45H, 12H, 78H, 23H, 56H ; 5-element array at 30H N EQU 05H ; Array size ORG 0100H MAIN: MOV R7, #N ; R7 ← 5 (array size) MOV R6, #N-1 ; R6 ← 4 (outer loop counter, N-1 passes) OUTER: CLR 00H ; Clear swap flag (bit 0 of 20H) MOV R5, R6 ; R5 ← R6 (inner counter = N-1-i) MOV R0, #30H ; R0 ← 30H (point to array start) INNER: MOV A, @R0 ; A ← A[j] INC R0 ; R0 ← j+1 CJNE A, @R0, SWAP_CHK ; Compare A[j] with A[j+1] SJMP NEXT ; If equal, no swap SWAP_CHK: JNC DO_SWAP ; If CY=0 (i.e., A[j] > A[j+1]), swap SJMP NEXT ; Else, skip swap DO_SWAP: XCH A, @R0 ; A ↔ A[j+1] (atomic) DEC R0 ; R0 ← j XCH A, @R0 ; A ↔ A[j] (atomic, A now holds old A[j]) SETB 00H ; Mark that a swap happened NEXT: DJNZ R5, INNER ; R5--, if not zero, repeat inner JNB 00H, DONE ; If no swap in this pass, sorted! DJNZ R6, OUTER ; R6--, if not zero, next pass DONE: SJMP DONE ; Infinite loop — array is sorted END ``` > [!NOTE] > **Exhaustive Note on the `CJNE` / Carry Logic:** The instruction `CJNE A, @R0, SWAP_CHK` performs a magnitude comparison. Internally, it computes `A − @R0` and sets the Carry flag as a **borrow indicator**: `CY = 1` if `A < @R0` (borrow required), and `CY = 0` if `A ≥ @R0` (no borrow). For **ascending sort**, we swap only when `A > @R0`, which corresponds to `CY = 0` — hence we test with `JNC DO_SWAP` (Jump if No Carry → go to swap). For **descending sort**, the condition flips: we swap when `A < @R0`, i.e., `CY = 1`, so the line becomes `JC DO_SWAP`. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Bubble Sort Algorithm (Ascending) ```mermaid flowchart TD start1([START]) --> init1["R6 = N minus 1
R7 = N
R0 = 30H base address"] init1 --> outerLoop{"R6 != 0 ?"} outerLoop -- No --> done1([END Sorted]) outerLoop -- Yes --> clrFlag["CLR 00H
Reset swap flag"] clrFlag --> loadR5["R5 = R6
Inner counter = N-1-i"] loadR5 --> loadR0["R0 = 30H
Point to A index 0"] loadR0 --> innerLoop{"R5 != 0 ?"} innerLoop -- No --> checkFlag{"00H == 0 ?"} innerLoop -- Yes --> loadA["A = M of R0
Read current element"] loadA --> incR0["R0 = R0 + 1
Advance pointer"] incR0 --> cjne["CJNE A versus M of R0
Compare with next element"] cjne --> jcTest{"CY = 0 ?
i.e. A greater than next"} jcTest -- Yes --> doSwap["XCH swap block
Set 00H flag"] jcTest -- No --> next1[Next inner iteration] doSwap --> next1 next1 --> decR5["R5 = R5 minus 1"] decR5 --> innerLoop checkFlag -- Yes --> decR6["R6 = R6 minus 1"] checkFlag -- No --> done1 decR6 --> outerLoop ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Memory and Register Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CPU[8051 CPU Core] ACC[Accumulator A] R0[R0 Pointer] R5[R5 Inner Counter] R6[R6 Outer Counter] R7[R7 Array Size N] FLAG[Bit 00H Swap Flag] end subgraph RAM[Internal RAM 00H to 7FH] A30[30H Index 0] A31[31H Index 1] A32[32H Index 2] A33[33H Index 3] A34[34H Index 4] end R0 -- "indirect address" --> A30 R0 -. "after INC" .-> A31 R0 -. "after INC" .-> A32 R0 -. "after INC" .-> A33 R0 -. "after INC" .-> A34 ACC -- "MOV A, M of R0" --> A30 A30 -- "XCH A, M of R0" --> ACC R5 -- "DJNZ controls" --> inner[Inner Loop] R6 -- "DJNZ controls" --> outer[Outer Loop] FLAG -- "JNB tests" --> early[Early Exit Check] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Data Flow — One Swap Operation in Detail ```mermaid flowchart LR step1["Step 1: MOV A, M of R0
A holds A index j"] --> step2["Step 2: INC R0
