R0 points to A index j+1"] step2 --> step3["Step 3: XCH A, M of R0
A index j+1 and A exchange
Now A holds A index j+1"] step3 --> step4["Step 4: DEC R0
R0 points back to A index j"] step4 --> step5["Step 5: XCH A, M of R0
A and A index j exchange
Now A holds old A index j
and M index j holds A index j+1"] step5 --> step6["Step 6: SETB 00H
Mark swap flag"] ``` ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Entire Experiment ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PHASE1[Phase 1 Initialization] P1A[Load array base 30H into R0] P1B[Load N into R7] P1C[Load N-1 into R6] end subgraph PHASE2[Phase 2 Outer Pass Loop] P2A[Clear swap flag bit 00H] P2B[Copy R6 to R5] P2C[Reset R0 to base 30H] end subgraph PHASE3[Phase 3 Inner Compare Loop] P3A[Load A from M of R0] P3B[Increment R0] P3C[CJNE compare A with M of R0] P3D{Carry flag test} end subgraph PHASE4[Phase 4 Conditional Swap] P4A[Execute XCH swap block] P4B[Set swap flag bit 00H] end subgraph PHASE5[Phase 5 Loop Control and Termination] P5A[DJNZ R5 inner loop] P5B[JNB 00H test for early exit] P5C[DJNZ R6 outer loop] P5D[Fall through to DONE] end PHASE1 --> PHASE2 PHASE2 --> PHASE3 PHASE3 --> PHASE4 PHASE3 --> PHASE5 PHASE4 --> PHASE5 PHASE5 --> PHASE2 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PCRUL408]** *Define the term "in-place sorting" as applied to array sorting in an 8051 microcontroller. Why is Bubble Sort classified as an in-place algorithm?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **in-place sorting algorithm** is one that sorts the input array using only **O(1) auxiliary memory** — i.e., a constant amount of extra space beyond the array itself, regardless of the array size `N`. The original array's memory locations are modified directly, and no separate output array is created. **Bubble Sort is in-place** because it performs all its operations (comparison and swapping) by overwriting the original memory locations of the array `A[0..N-1]`. In the 8051 implementation, only the Accumulator (`A`), a few registers (`R0`, `R5`, `R6`, `R7`), and one bit-flag (`00H`) are used as temporaries — this is a constant amount of memory, independent of `N`. The array itself is rearranged in its original memory block starting at `30H`. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for justification with 8051 context)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PCRUL408]** *What is the role of the `CJNE` instruction in the 8051 Bubble Sort routine? How does it set the Carry flag?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The `CJNE A, @R0, rel` (Compare and Jump if Not Equal) instruction performs a **magnitude comparison** between the Accumulator and the byte addressed by `R0`. It is the **only** 8051 instruction that does this in a single cycle category. **Carry flag behavior:** The instruction internally computes `A − (memory byte)`. If `A < memory byte`, a borrow is required during the subtraction, and the **Carry flag is set to 1** (`CY = 1`). If `A ≥ memory byte`, no borrow is needed, and `CY = 0`. **Role in Bubble Sort:** After `CJNE`, the programmer tests `CY` using `JNC` (Jump if No Carry) or `JC` (Jump if Carry) to decide whether to swap. For ascending sort, `CY = 0` (i.e., `A > next`) triggers a swap; for descending sort, `CY = 1` (i.e., `A < next`) triggers a swap. **(3 marks: 1 for role, 1 for carry rule, 1 for application)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before You Write!** > 1. **Do NOT forget to reinitialize `R0` to the array base (`30H`) at the start of each outer pass.** This is the **#1 mistake** — students forget to reset the pointer, and after the first pass `R0` points past the array end, causing garbage memory to be read. Loss: **2–3 marks**. > 2. **Do NOT use `MOV @R0, @R1`** — this instruction **does not exist in 8051**. You must route through the Accumulator. Loss: **2 marks**. > 3. **The `CJNE` instruction is `CJNE A, @R0, rel`** — the destination operand must be a **direct address or `@Rn`**, not another register. Writing `CJNE A, R0, ...` is wrong. Loss: **2 marks**. > 4. **Distinguish ascending vs. descending by the carry-flag test, not by reversing the comparison direction entirely.** Only the `JNC`/`JC` flips. Loss: **1–2 marks**. > 5. **Always show the swap block as 4 instructions** (or use `XCH` correctly with a temporary); partial swaps that corrupt memory will be penalized heavily. --- **Question A — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Option 1)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PCRUL408 Module 2]** *Write an 8051 Assembly Language program to sort an array of **10 unsigned numbers** stored in the internal RAM locations starting at `40H` in **ASCENDING order** using the **Bubble Sort algorithm**. The program must (a) use the `XCH` instruction for swapping, (b) implement an early-termination mechanism using a bit flag, and (c) leave the sorted array in the same memory region.* **Model Solution — Question A:** **Part (a) — Algorithm, Memory Map, and Register Allocation (7 marks):** **Algorithm in Pseudocode:** ``` N = 10 for i = 0 to N-2: swapped = 0 for j = 0 to N-2-i: if A[j] > A[j+1]: swap(A[j], A[j+1]) swapped = 1 if swapped == 0: break ``` **Register Allocation:** | Register | Role | |---|---| | `R7` | `N` = 10 (constant) | | `R6` | Outer loop counter, initialized to `N-1` = 9 | | `R5` | Inner loop counter, initialized to `N-1-i` | | `R0` | Indirect pointer to current array element | | `A` | Accumulator — holds compared value and swap intermediate | | Bit `00H` (byte `20H`, bit 0) | Swap flag for early termination | **Memory Map:** Array base = `40H`, so elements are at `40H, 41H, ..., 49H`. **Part (b) — Complete 8051 Assembly Program (7 marks):** ```asm ORG 0000H LJMP MAIN ORG 0040H ARRAY: DB 55H, 22H, 89H, 11H, 67H ; Sample 10-element array DB 34H, 90H, 05H, 43H, 71H N EQU 10 ORG 0100H MAIN: MOV R7, #N ; R7 ← 10 MOV R6, #N-1 ; R6 ← 9 (outer loop counter) OUTER: CLR 00H ; Reset swap flag MOV R5, R6 ; R5 ← N-1-i (inner counter) MOV R0, #40H ; R0 ← base address of array INNER: MOV A, @R0 ; A ← A[j] INC R0 ; Point to A[j+1] CJNE A, @R0, SWAP_CHK ; Compare A[j] with A[j+1] SJMP CONTINUE ; Equal case, no swap needed SWAP_CHK: JNC DO_SWAP ; If A[j] > A[j+1] (CY=0), swap SJMP CONTINUE ; Else, no swap DO_SWAP: XCH A, @R0 ; A ↔ A[j+1] DEC R0 ; Point back to A[j] XCH A, @R0 ; A[j] ↔ A[j+1] (complete) SETB 00H ; Mark that a swap occurred CONTINUE: DJNZ R5, INNER ; R5--, repeat inner loop JNB 00H, SORTED ; If no swap, array is sorted DJNZ R6, OUTER ; R6--, next pass SORTED: SJMP SORTED ; Halt — array is sorted END ``` **Verification Trace (first 3 passes on sample array):** Initial: `[55, 22, 89, 11, 67, 34, 90, 05, 43, 71]` - After Pass 1: `[22, 55, 11, 67, 34, 89, 05, 43, 71, 90]` — `90` at index 9 - After Pass 2: `[22, 11, 55, 34, 67, 05, 43, 71, 89, 90]` — `89` at index 8 - After Pass 3: `[11, 22, 34, 55, 05, 43, 67, 71, 89, 90]` — `71` at index 7 - (Continues until sorted: `[05, 11, 22, 34, 43, 55, 67, 71, 89, 90]`) **Mark Distribution (Total 14):** - Algorithm + memory map + register allocation: **7 marks** (Part a) - Complete correct assembly code with all required features: **7 marks** (Part b) --- **Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Option 2)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PCRUL408 Module 2]** *An array of **8 unsigned 8-bit numbers** is stored in internal RAM starting at address `50H`. Write an 8051 Assembly program to sort this array in **DESCENDING order** using Bubble Sort. Show clearly (a) the assembly code with the descending-sort logic, and (b) the verification by tracing the first two passes on the input array `{15, 67, 42, 89, 23, 51, 76, 34}`. Explain the single instruction that changes between ascending and descending versions.* **Model Solution — Question B:** **Part (a) — Complete 8051 Assembly Program (7 marks):** ```asm ORG 0000H LJMP MAIN ORG 0050H ARRAY: DB 15H, 67H, 42H, 89H, 23H, 51H, 76H, 34H ; 8 elements N EQU 08H ORG 0100H MAIN: MOV R7, #N ; R7 ← 8 MOV R6, #N-1 ; R6 ← 7 (outer loop counter) OUTER: CLR 00H ; Reset swap flag MOV R5, R6 ; Inner counter = N-1-i MOV R0, #50H ; R0 ← array base INNER: MOV A, @R0 ; A ← A[j] INC R0 ; Point to A[j+1] CJNE A, @R0, SWAP_CHK ; Compare SJMP CONTINUE SWAP_CHK: JC DO_SWAP ; DESCENDING: if A[j] < A[j+1] (CY=1), swap SJMP CONTINUE DO_SWAP: XCH A, @R0 DEC R0 XCH A, @R0 SETB 00H CONTINUE: DJNZ R5, INNER JNB 00H, SORTED DJNZ R6, OUTER SORTED: SJMP SORTED END ``` **The Single Instruction Change:** > The **only difference** between ascending and descending versions is the conditional jump that triggers the swap: > - **Ascending:** `JNC DO_SWAP` (swap when `CY = 0`, i.e., `A > next`) > - **Descending:** `JC DO_SWAP` (swap when `CY = 1`, i.e., `A < next`) > > Everything else — the register allocation, the swap block, the early-termination logic — remains **identical**. **Part (b) — Trace of First Two Passes (7 marks):** Initial array: `[15, 67, 42, 89, 23, 51, 76, 34]` **Pass 1** (4 outer counter, R5 = 7, 7 comparisons): | j | A[j] | A[j+1] | CY after CJNE | Decision | Array State | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | 15 | 67 | CY=1 (15<67) | **SWAP** | `[67, 15, 42, 89, 23, 51, 76, 34]` | | 1 | 15 | 42 | CY=1 (15<42) | **SWAP** | `[67, 42, 15, 89, 23, 51, 76, 34]` | | 2 | 15 | 89 | CY=1 (15<89) | **SWAP** | `[67, 42, 89, 15, 23, 51, 76, 34]` | | 3 | 15 | 23 | CY=1 (15<23) | **SWAP** | `[67, 42, 89, 23, 15, 51, 76, 34]` | | 4 | 15 | 51 | CY=1 (15<51) | **SWAP** | `[67, 42, 89, 23, 51, 15, 76, 34]` | | 5 | 15 | 76 | CY=1 (15<76) | **SWAP** | `[67, 42, 89, 23, 51, 76, 15, 34]` | | 6 | 15 | 34 | CY=1 (15<34) | **SWAP** | `[67, 42, 89, 23, 51, 76, 34, 15]` | End of Pass 1: `67, 42, 89, 23, 51, 76, 34, 15`. The smallest element `15` has "sunk" to index 7 (its final position). Swap flag = 1. **Pass 2** (R5 = 6, 6 comparisons): | j | A[j] | A[j+1] | CY | Decision | Array State | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | 67 | 42 | CY=0 (67>42) | No swap | `[67, 42, 89, 23, 51, 76, 34, 15]` | | 1 | 42 | 89 | CY=1 (42<89) | **SWAP** | `[67, 89, 42, 23, 51, 76, 34, 15]` | | 2 | 42 | 23 | CY=0 (42>23) | No swap | `[67, 89, 42, 23, 51, 76, 34, 15]` | | 3 | 23 | 51 | CY=1 (23<51) | **SWAP** | `[67, 89, 42, 51, 23, 76, 34, 15]` | | 4 | 23 | 76 | CY=1 (23<76) | **SWAP** | `[67, 89, 42, 51, 76, 23, 34, 15]` | | 5 | 23 | 34 | CY=1 (23<34) | **SWAP** | `[67, 89, 42, 51, 76, 34, 23, 15]` | End of Pass 2: `67, 89, 42, 51, 76, 34, 23, 15`. The next-smallest `23` has moved to index 6. Swap flag = 1. The algorithm continues until all 8 elements are sorted in descending order: `[89, 76, 67, 51, 42, 34, 23, 15]`. **Mark Distribution (Total 14):** - Complete assembly program with descending logic + the "single instruction" explanation: **7 marks** (Part a) - Exhaustive trace of Pass 1 and Pass 2 with CY values and final arrays: **7 marks** (Part b) --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These Before the Exam:** - [ ] **Bubble Sort** is the **in-place, comparison-based, stable** sorting algorithm prescribed in PCRUL408 Module 2. - [ ] The **outer loop runs `N-1` times**; the **inner loop runs `N-1-i` times** in pass `i`. - [ ] The **swap primitive is 4 instructions**: `MOV A,@R0` → `INC R0` → `XCH A,@R0` → `DEC R0` → `XCH A,@R0` (use the 5-line form with a final `MOV @R0,A` only if your variant doesn't use `XCH` twice). - [ ] **`CJNE A, @R0, rel` sets `CY=1` if `A < @R0`**, and `CY=0` if `A ≥ @R0`. This is the cornerstone of all comparison logic in 8051. - [ ] **Ascending sort → swap when `CY = 0` → use `JNC DO_SWAP`.** - [ ] **Descending sort → swap when `CY = 1` → use `JC DO_SWAP`.** - [ ] Always **reset `R0` to the array base address at the start of every outer pass** — this is the most common viva-practical mistake. - [ ] Always **clear the swap flag (e.g., `CLR 00H`) at the start of every outer pass** for correct early-termination. - [ ] The **swap flag is a single bit** in the bit-addressable region (`20H`–`2FH`), commonly bit `00H` of byte `20H`. - [ ] `DJNZ Rn, rel` both **decrements `Rn` and branches** if the result is non-zero — the standard loop-control idiom in 8051. - [ ] Best case: `N-1` comparisons (already sorted). Worst case: `N(N-1)/2` comparisons (reverse sorted). - [ ] Space complexity: **O(1)** (in-place). Time complexity: **O(N²)** in average and worst case. - [ ] The `XCH A, @R0` instruction performs an **atomic byte exchange** between the Accumulator and a memory byte in 12 clock cycles. - [ ] For the KTU lab record, **always include**: (i) algorithm, (ii) memory map, (iii) register allocation table, (iv) full assembly code with comments, (v) input/output verification, (vi) hand-traced pass table. - [ ] **Memory regions available in 8051**: Internal RAM `00H–7FH` (128 bytes), SFRs `80H–FFH`, External XDATA `0000H–FFFFH`. Lab programs typically use **Internal RAM `30H–7FH`** for arrays to avoid SFR overlap. - [ ] If asked to sort in **XDATA**, replace `MOV A, @R0` with `MOVX A, @R0` and use **DPTR** instead of R0/R1 as the pointer.

topics.title: Time Delay Generation Using Timers and Relay Interface with 8051/AT89S51 Microcontroller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND IOT LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Time Delay Generation Using Timers and Relay Interface with 8051/AT89S51 Microcontroller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — TIME DELAY & RELAY INTERFACE (8051/AT89S52)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Timer in 8051?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> The **8051 Microcontroller** contains **two 16-bit programmable Timer/Counters (Timer 0 and Timer 1)** that can be used for precise **time delay generation**, **event counting**, or **baud rate generation** for serial communication. Each timer is essentially a binary up-counter driven by the **internal oscillator (as timer)** or an **external pin T0/P3.4 or T1/P3.5 (as counter)**.

The **AT89S52** (Atmel/Microchip) is a popular, low-cost, flash-based 8051-family MCU that retains the original timer architecture of the Intel **MCS-51** core, with the same SFR (Special Function Register) layout at addresses `0x88` to `0xFF`.

### 1.2 What is a Relay Interface?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Relay Interface** is the hardware arrangement used to connect a low-current digital output of a microcontroller (typically **15–20 mA** per port pin) to a **high-current electromechanical coil** of a relay (typically **30–70 mA at 5 V/12 V/24 V DC**). It includes a **driver stage** (transistor/ULN2003/IC ULN2803), a **freewheeling/flyback diode** for back-EMF protection, and optionally an **optocoupler** for galvanic isolation.

---

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

| Concept | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **8051 Timer** | Imagine a **stopwatch** inside a digital watch. The watch's internal quartz crystal ticks at a fixed rate. The timer counts these ticks. When the count reaches a preset value, the watch "beeps" — this "beep" is analogous to the **TF0/TF1 overflow flag** setting in the 8051. |
| **Machine Cycle** | Just like a human heartbeat (≈ 1 beat/sec) sets the rhythm of the body, the **machine cycle** sets the rhythm of the CPU. The 8051 needs **12 oscillator pulses** to complete **one machine cycle** — the smallest unit of CPU work. |
| **Timer Overflow** | Like a car odometer rolling over from `99999` to `00000` after exceeding its range, the 16-bit timer rolls over from `0xFFFF` to `0x0000`, setting the **TF (Timer Flag)** — this is the signal that a precise time interval has elapsed. |
| **Relay** | A **light switch operated by electricity**. You push a tiny button (microcontroller pin) → a small electromagnet pulls a mechanical lever → the lever closes a high-power circuit (AC mains, motor). The microcontroller never touches the high voltage directly. |
| **Optocoupler** | Like a **glass wall between two telephone booths** — a person in booth A can talk to booth B using light, but no electricity flows between them. If the high-voltage side faults, the low-voltage microcontroller is safe. |
| **Flyback Diode** | When you switch off a fan, the spinning blades keep going for a moment (back-EMF). The diode is like a **shock-absorber** that safely dissipates this voltage spike that could otherwise destroy the transistor. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Fact:** In a **typical 8051 lab**, the crystal used is **11.0592 MHz** (not 12 MHz!) because this value, divided by 12, gives a machine cycle of exactly **1.085 µs** — perfect for generating standard baud rates like **9600** for UART without fractional errors. Always check your crystal frequency before computing delays!

---

## 2. Register Map of 8051 Timers (Foundation Concepts)

| SFR Name | Address | Bit Addressable | Reset Value | Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TMOD** | 0x89 | ❌ No | 0x00 | Timer **MODe** register (selects mode, operation type, gate control) |
| **TCON** | 0x88 | ✅ Yes | 0x00 | Timer **CONtrol** register (run control, flags, edge flags) |
| **TL0** | 0x8A | ❌ No | 0x00 | Timer 0 **Low byte** |
| **TH0** | 0x8C | ❌ No | 0x00 | Timer 0 **High byte** |
| **TL1** | 0x8B | ❌ No | 0x00 | Timer 1 **Low byte** |
| **TH1** | 0x8D | ❌ No | 0x00 | Timer 1 **High byte** |

### 2.1 TMOD Register (0x89) — Bit Layout

| Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GATE** | **C/T̄** | **M1** | **M0** | **GATE** | **C/T̄** | **M1** | **M0** |
| (Timer 1) | (Timer 1) | (Timer 1) | (Timer 1) | (Timer 0) | (Timer 0) | (Timer 0) | (Timer 0) |

- **GATE** = 1 → Timer runs only when INTx pin is HIGH and TRx = 1 (hardware control).
- **GATE** = 0 → Timer runs whenever TRx = 1 (software control — most common in labs).
- **C/T̄** = 0 → **Timer mode** (uses internal oscillator ÷ 12).
- **C/T̄** = 1 → **Counter mode** (counts external pulses on T0/P3.4 or T1/P3.5).

### 2.2 TCON Register (0x88) — Timer-Related Bits

| Bit | Symbol | Address | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | **TF1** | 0x8F | Timer 1 **Overflow Flag** — set by hardware on overflow |
| 6 | **TR1** | 0x8E | Timer 1 **Run Control** — set/cleared by software |
| 5 | **TF0** | 0x8D | Timer 0 **Overflow Flag** — set by hardware on overflow |
| 4 | **TR0** | 0x8C | Timer 0 **Run Control** — set/cleared by software |
| 3 | IE1 | 0x8B | External interrupt 1 edge flag |
| 2 | IT1 | 0x8A | External interrupt 1 type (edge/level) |
| 1 | IE0 | 0x89 | External interrupt 0 edge flag |
| 0 | IT0 | 0x88 | External interrupt 0 type (edge/level) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Always clear the **TFx flag** *manually* in polled mode (it is NOT cleared automatically like in interrupts). Forgetting this is the **#1 cause of "infinite loop" errors** in viva/demo.

---

## 3. Timer Modes — At a Glance

| Mode | M1 | M0 | Counter Width | Total Counts | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mode 0** | 0 | 0 | 13-bit (THx 8-bit + TLx 5-bit) | 8192 | Legacy 8048 compatibility — rarely used |
| **Mode 1** | 0 | 1 | **16-bit** (THx 8-bit + TLx 8-bit) | **65536** | Most common — flexible delay generation |
| **Mode 2** | 1 | 0 | **8-bit auto-reload** | **256** | Baud rate generation for UART, short precise delays |
| **Mode 3** | 1 | 1 | Two 8-bit timers (Timer 0 split) | 256 | Special — Timer 0 becomes TL0 + TH0, Timer 1 stops |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mode 1** is the workhorse of lab experiments. **Mode 2** is used for **UART baud rate generation** and **fixed 256-tick periodic interrupts**. **Mode 3** is exotic and only appears in advanced questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 4. The Central Concept: Time Delay Generation

### 4.1 The "Why" — Why can't we just use software delay loops?

A pure software delay (e.g., `for(i=0; i<30000; i++);`) is:
1. **Inaccurate** — affected by compiler optimization (modern Keil/SDCC may delete empty loops!).
2. **CPU-blocking** — the CPU cannot do anything else (no ADC reading, no UART, no display update).
3. **Non-portable** — changes with clock frequency, compiler version, optimization level.

**Hardware timer delay** solves all three:
- **Accurate** (precision of ±1 machine cycle, i.e., ±1.085 µs at 11.0592 MHz).
- **CPU can poll TFx or use interrupts** for multitasking.
- **Frequency-independent logic** — same code works for 12 MHz and 11.0592 MHz with recalculated values.

### 4.2 The "How" — Operational Steps

A typical 8051 timer delay routine (polled mode) executes the following **6 logical stages**:

1. **Configure TMOD** to select Mode (e.g., Mode 1), C/T̄ = 0 (timer), GATE = 0 (software start).
2. **Load initial value** into THx and TLx registers (this is the "preload" or "preset" value).
3. **Start the timer** by setting TRx = 1 in TCON register.
4. **Wait and poll** the TFx flag (TFx = 1 indicates overflow has occurred).
5. **Stop the timer** by clearing TRx = 0.
6. **Clear the overflow flag** TFx = 0 (manually, in polled mode).

> [!TIP]
> **Step 6 is the most-skipped step by students.** Always include `TF0 = 0;` or `TF1 = 0;` after the polling loop. This is graded in KTU lab records.

---

## 5. KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these four equations — they cover 90% of KTU delay-generation numerical problems.**

### Equation 1 — Machine Cycle Duration

$$\text{Machine Cycle} = \frac{12}{f_{\text{oscillator}}}$$

Where $f_{\text{oscillator}}$ is the crystal frequency in Hz. Result is in seconds.

### Equation 2 — Timer Count Required

$$\text{Counts Required} = \frac{T_{\text{desired}}}{\text{Machine Cycle}} = \frac{T_{\text{desired}} \times f_{\text{oscillator}}}{12}$$

Where $T_{\text{desired}}$ is the required delay in seconds.

### Equation 3 — Timer Register Preload Value (16-bit, Mode 1)

$$\text{Preload} = 2^{16} - \text{Counts Required} = 65536 - \text{Counts Required}$$

Decompose:
- $\text{TH0} = \text{Preload} \gg 8$ (i.e., divide by 256, take high byte)
- $\text{TL0} = \text{Preload} \,\&\, \text{0xFF}$ (i.e., modulo 256, take low byte)

### Equation 4 — Maximum Achievable Delay (Single Overflow, Mode 1)

$$T_{\max} = 65536 \times \frac{12}{f_{\text{oscillator}}}$$

At **11.0592 MHz**: $T_{max} = 65536 \times 1.085\,\mu s = 71.1\,ms$ per single overflow.
At **12 MHz**: $T_{max} = 65536 \times 1\,\mu s = 65.536\,ms$ per single overflow.

> **For delays longer than $T_{max}$**, we must use a **software loop** that *re-triggers* the timer multiple times.

### Equation 5 — Total Delay Using Multiplication Loop

$$T_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{loops}} \times T_{\text{single overflow}}$$

For example, to get **1 second delay** at 11.0592 MHz:
- $N_{loops} = 1\,s \div 71.1\,ms \approx 14$ loops (with a slightly smaller preload to give exactly 1 s).

---

## 6. KTU Boundary Conditions & Special Cases Table

| Crystal | Machine Cycle | Max Delay (Mode 1) | Max Delay (Mode 2) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **12.000 MHz** | **1.000 µs** | **65.536 ms** | **256.0 µs** |
| **11.0592 MHz** | **1.085 µs** | **71.106 ms** | **277.8 µs** |
| **24.000 MHz** | **0.500 µs** | **32.768 ms** | **128.0 µs** |
| **10.000 MHz** | **1.200 µs** | **78.643 ms** | **307.2 µs** |

### 6.1 Mode 2 Auto-Reload Trick

In Mode 2, TLx counts 0 → 255 → overflow → TLx is **automatically reloaded with THx value** in **one machine cycle**. The CPU gets a continuous, jitter-free periodic interrupt every **256 × 1.085 µs ≈ 277.8 µs** (at 11.0592 MHz). This is the foundation of **UART baud rate generation**.

### 6.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Timer Configuration |
|---|---|
| **Traffic Light Controller** (1 s red, 1 s green) | Mode 1, 16-bit, with multiplication loop or interrupt |
| **PWM Generation** (DC motor speed control) | Mode 2 auto-reload + interrupt at 10–50 kHz |
| **Digital Stopwatch / RTC Tick** | Mode 1 or 2 with interrupt every 1 ms / 50 ms |
| **UART Serial Communication (9600 baud)** | Timer 1, Mode 2, TH1 = 0xFD for 11.0592 MHz |
| **Data Acquisition Sampling** | Mode 1 + interrupt for periodic ADC reads |
| **Staircase / Lift Timing** | This experiment's exact scenario |

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Note:** In commercial embedded systems (washing machines, elevators, automotive ECUs), the same timer concept is implemented in **ARM Cortex-M** MCUs using **SysTick timers** and **General-Purpose Timers (TIM1, TIM2)**. The math is identical — only register names change. Mastering this on 8051 transfers directly to ARM.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 7. Exhaustive Derivation — Worked Numerical Examples

### Example 1: Generate 50 ms Delay Using Timer 0, Mode 1, 11.0592 MHz Crystal

**Given:**
- Crystal: $f_{osc} = 11.0592\,MHz = 11.0592 \times 10^{6}\,Hz$
- Required delay: $T_d = 50\,ms = 50 \times 10^{-3}\,s$
- Timer: Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit)

**Step 1 — Compute Machine Cycle Duration.**

$$\text{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\text{MC} = 1.085\,\mu s$$

**Step 2 — Compute Required Timer Counts.**

$$\text{Counts} = \frac{T_d}{\text{MC}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\text{Counts} = 46082.949 \approx 46083$$

**Step 3 — Compute Preload Value (Mode 1, 16-bit).**

$$\text{Preload} = 65536 - 46083 = 19453$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Hexadecimal.**
Repeatedly divide 19453 by 16:

$$19453 \div 16 = 1215 \text{ remainder } 13 = D$$

$$1215 \div 16 = 75 \text{ remainder } 15 = F$$

$$75 \div 16 = 4 \text{ remainder } 11 = B$$

$$4 \div 16 = 0 \text{ remainder } 4$$

Reading remainders bottom-up: $\text{Preload} = 0x4BFD$

**Step 5 — Split into TH0 and TL0.**

$$\text{TH0} = 0x4B$$

$$\text{TL0} = 0xFD$$

> **Verification:** $0x4B \times 256 + 0xFD = 19200 + 253 = 19453$ ✓

---

### Example 2: Generate Exactly 1 Second Delay (Software Loop Required)

**Given:** Same 11.0592 MHz crystal.

**Step 1 — Single Overflow Max Delay.**

$$T_{max} = 65536 \times \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 71.106\,ms$$

**Step 2 — Number of Overflows Needed.**

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{1000\,ms}{71.106\,ms} \right\rceil = \lceil 14.064 \rceil = 15$$

But 15 × 71.106 = 1066.6 ms — too long. Better approach: choose a **smaller target per loop** so that it divides 1000 ms evenly.

**Step 3 — Choose a Convenient Sub-Delay (50 ms × 20 = 1000 ms).**

We already have the 50 ms preload: **TH0 = 0x4B, TL0 = 0xFD** (from Example 1).

Loop count: $N_{loops} = 20$

**Total Delay:**

$$T_{total} = 20 \times 50\,ms = 1000\,ms = 1\,s \;\checkmark$$

---

### Example 3: Generate 500 µs Delay Using Timer 0, Mode 2, 12 MHz Crystal

**Given:** $f_{osc} = 12\,MHz$, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload).

**Step 1 — Machine Cycle.**

$$\text{MC} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1\,\mu s$$

**Step 2 — Required Counts.**

$$\text{Counts} = \frac{500 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 500$$

**Step 3 — Verify Achievability.** Mode 2 max counts = 256. Since 500 > 256, **this delay is NOT achievable in a single Mode 2 overflow**. We must either:
- Switch to Mode 1, **or**
- Use Mode 2 with a multiplication loop of $N = \lceil 500/256 \rceil = 2$, with sub-delay $250\,\mu s$.

**Sub-delay of 250 µs in Mode 2:**

$$\text{Counts} = 250$$

$$\text{Preload} = 256 - 250 = 6 = 0x06$$

$$\text{TH0} = 0x06, \text{ TL0} = 0x06 \text{ (reloaded from TH0 on overflow)}$$

Total delay: $2 \times 250\,\mu s = 500\,\mu s$ ✓

---

## 8. KTU Analytical Matrix — "Which Timer Mode Should I Use?"

| If delay needed is... | Recommended Mode | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| $< 256\,\mu s$ (at 12 MHz) | **Mode 2** | Single byte, no loop needed |
| $256\,\mu s$ to $65\,ms$ (at 12 MHz) | **Mode 1** | 16-bit precision |
| $> 65\,ms$ (at 12 MHz) | **Mode 1 + software loop** | Multi-overflow needed |
| Repetitive periodic ISR | **Mode 2 + interrupt** | Auto-reload is free |
| $< 8192\,counts$ (any freq.) and need speed | **Mode 0** (13-bit) | Less register access, but rarely used |
| UART 9600 baud | **Timer 1, Mode 2** | TH1 = 0xFD (11.0592 MHz) |

---

## 9. Relay Interface — Deep Analysis

### 9.1 Why Direct Connection Fails

A **single 8051 port pin** can source/sink a maximum of:
- **Port 0**: $3.2\,mA$ (open-drain, needs external pull-up)
- **Ports 1, 2, 3**: ~$10\,mA$ to $15\,mA$ per pin
- **Total per port**: ~$15\,mA$ to $26\,mA$

A typical **5V SPDT relay coil** draws **$30\,mA$ to $70\,mA$**. Direct connection will:
1. Overload the pin → port pin damage over time.
2. Drop the logic HIGH voltage below the threshold → erratic triggering.
3. Cause voltage spikes that reset the MCU.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often connect the relay coil directly to the port pin. This will *appear* to work for a few toggles, then the pin will fail permanently. Always use a driver stage.

### 9.2 Driver Stage Options

| Driver | Drive Current | Isolation | Cost | Common In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BC547 NPN Transistor** | 100 mA | ❌ No | ₹2 | Labs, hobby projects |
| **ULN2003** (7-channel Darlington) | 500 mA per channel | ❌ No | ₹20 | Industrial relay boards |
| **ULN2803** (8-channel) | 500 mA | ❌ No | ₹25 | Multi-relay boards |
| **Optocoupler PC817 + Transistor** | 20 mA (LED side) | ✅ Yes | ₹15 | Medical, safety-critical |
| **Solid State Relay (SSR)** | Built-in | ✅ Yes (opto) | ₹150 | Industrial AC control |
| **TLP281 (Quad Opto)** | Built-in driver | ✅ Yes | ₹30 | PLC interfaces |

### 9.3 Critical Components in the Relay Interface

| Component | Rating / Type | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Relay Coil Voltage** | 5V / 12V / 24V DC | Energizes the electromagnet |
| **Coil Current** | 30–500 mA | Determines driver IC choice |
| **Freewheeling Diode** | **1N4007** (1A, 1000V) | Clamps back-EMF when coil de-energizes |
| **Base Resistor (R_B)** | $\approx 1\,k\Omega$ (for 5V) | Limits base current to ~4 mA for BC547 |
| **Pull-up Resistor (Port 0)** | $4.7\,k\Omega$ to $10\,k\Omega$ | Required only for Port 0 (open-drain) |
| **Snubber (across AC contact)** | $100\,\Omega + 0.1\,\mu F$ | Suppresses arcing on AC load contact |

### 9.4 Freewheeling Diode — Detailed Derivation

When the transistor switches OFF, the inductor (relay coil) tries to maintain current:

$$V_L = L \frac{di}{dt}$$

The collapsing magnetic field produces a **back-EMF voltage spike** of hundreds of volts, given by:

$$V_{spike} = L \frac{\Delta i}{\Delta t}$$

For a typical relay with $L = 100\,mH$ and switching time $\Delta t = 1\,\mu s$:

$$V_{spike} = 0.1 \times \frac{0.05}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 5000\,V$$

This will **instantly destroy** the transistor. The **1N4007 diode** connected in **reverse-bias** across the coil provides a freewheeling path. When the spike occurs, the diode becomes forward-biased and clamps the voltage to ~0.7 V (diode drop) — safe!

> [!NOTE]
> **Polarity Rule:** Connect the **cathode (stripe side)** of the diode to the **positive** side of the coil, and **anode** to the **negative/ground** side. Get this wrong and the relay will appear short-circuited.

---

## 10. Combined Application — Lab Program Logic Flow

The most common KTU lab experiment combines:
1. **Timer-based delay** (generates ON-time)
2. **Relay driver** (controls load)
3. **Loop** (repeats the cycle)

**Pseudocode Logic:**

```
INITIALIZE:
    Set P1.0 as OUTPUT  (relay control pin)
    Configure TMOD = 0x01  (Timer 0, Mode 1, software start)
    
LOOP FOREVER:
    P1.0 = HIGH    → Relay ON → Bulb glows
    DELAY_5_SEC()  → Wait 5 seconds
    P1.0 = LOW     → Relay OFF → Bulb off
    DELAY_3_SEC()  → Wait 3 seconds
END LOOP
```

**Complete 8051 C Program (Keil/SDCC syntax):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>     // SFR declarations for 8051

sbit RELAY = P1^0;     // Relay connected to P1.0

void timer0_delay_50ms(void) {
    TMOD = 0x01;       // Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit), software start
    TH0  = 0x4B;       // High byte of 19453 = 0x4B
    TL0  = 0xFD;       // Low byte  of 19453 = 0xFD
    TR0  = 1;          // Start Timer 0
    while (TF0 == 0);  // Poll until overflow flag sets
    TR0  = 0;          // Stop Timer 0
    TF0  = 0;          // Manually clear overflow flag
}

void delay_seconds(unsigned int n) {
    unsigned int i, j;
    for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        for (j = 0; j < 20; j++) {     // 20 × 50 ms = 1 s
            timer0_delay_50ms();
        }
    }
}

void main(void) {
    RELAY = 0;                          // Relay initially OFF
    while (1) {
        RELAY = 1;                      // Relay ON
        delay_seconds(5);               // Wait 5 seconds
        RELAY = 0;                      // Relay OFF
        delay_seconds(3);               // Wait 3 seconds
    }
}
```

**Equivalent 8051 Assembly (Educational Reference):**

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     000BH                  ; Timer 0 ISR vector
        RETI

MAIN:   MOV     SP, #0x07
        MOV     P1, #0x00              ; All P1 pins LOW
        MOV     TMOD, #01H             ; T0, Mode 1, software start
        SETB    P1.0                   ; Relay ON
        ACALL   DELAY_5S
        CLR     P1.0                   ; Relay OFF
        ACALL   DELAY_3S
        SJMP    MAIN                   ; Repeat forever

DELAY_50MS:
        MOV     TH0, #4BH
        MOV     TL0, #0FDH
        SETB    TR0                    ; Start T0
WAIT:   JNB     TF0, WAIT              ; Poll TF0
        CLR     TR0                    ; Stop T0
        CLR     TF0                    ; Clear flag
        RET

DELAY_1S:
        MOV     R7, #20                ; 20 × 50ms = 1s
LOOP1:  ACALL   DELAY_50MS
        DJNZ    R7, LOOP1
        RET

DELAY_5S:
        MOV     R6, #5
LOOP5:  ACALL   DELAY_1S
        DJNZ    R6, LOOP5
        RET

DELAY_3S:
        MOV     R6, #3
LOOP3:  ACALL   DELAY_1S
        DJNZ    R6, LOOP3
        RET

        END
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Trap:** Question: "Why did you use Mode 1 and not Mode 2?"  
> **Answer:** Mode 1 gives a 16-bit count (0 to 65535), allowing 50 ms in a single overflow. Mode 2 only counts 0 to 255 (max 277.8 µs at 11.0592 MHz) and would require thousands of overflows — slower and CPU-intensive.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 11. Block Diagram — 8051 Timer 0 Internal Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    XTAL1[Xtal1 11.0592 MHz] --> OSC[Oscillator]
    XTAL2[Xtal2] --> OSC
    OSC --> DIV12[Divide by 12 Prescaler]
    DIV12 --> MC[Machine Cycle Pulse 1.085 us]
    MC --> SEL1[Mux Select by T0 Pin C/Tbar]
    T0PIN[T0 P3.4 External Pin] --> SEL1
    SEL1 --> CNT0[Timer 0 Up Counter]
    TMODREG[TMOD Register M1 M0 Gate] --> CTRL[Control Logic]
    CTRL --> CNT0
    CNT0 --> TH0LATCH[TH0 8-bit High]
    CNT0 --> TL0LATCH[TL0 8-bit Low]
    TH0LATCH --> COMP[16-bit Comparator 0xFFFF]
    TL0LATCH --> COMP
    COMP --> TFLG0[Set TF0 Flag TCON.5]
    TFLG0 --> POLL[Software Polls TF0 1]
    TFLG0 --> VEC[Interrupt Vector 000BH]
    TR0BIT[TR0 TCON.4] --> CTRL
```

## 12. Circuit Schematic — Relay Interface with 8051 (Block-Level)

```mermaid
graph LR
    VCC5V[VCC 5V] --> PULLUP[Pull-up 4.7k ohm]
    PULLUP --> MCUPIN[P1.0 of AT89S52]
    MCUPIN --> RB[Base Resistor 1k ohm]
    RB --> Q1BASE[Base of Q1 BC547]
    Q1BASE --> Q1[Q1 NPN Transistor BC547]
    Q1COLL[Collector] --> D1CATHODE[Cathode of 1N4007]
    D1CATHODE --> RLYP[Relay Coil + Pin]
    Q1EMIT[Emitter] --> GND1[Ground]
    RLYP --> VCC12V[VCC 12V for Relay]
    RLYM[Relay Coil - Pin] --> Q1COLL
    RLYC[Relay COM contact] --> ACMAIN[AC Mains 230V]
    ACMAIN --> LOAD[AC Load Lamp]
    LOAD --> RLYNC[Relay NO contact]
    Q1 --> Q1COLL
    D1ANODE[Anode of 1N4007] --> Q1COLL
```

## 13. Program Flowchart — Timer Delay + Relay Control

```mermaid
graph TD
    ST[Start Power On Reset] --> INIT[Initialize P1.0 as OUTPUT LOW]
    INIT --> CFGTMOD[Configure TMOD 0x01 Timer 0 Mode 1]
    CFGTMOD --> LOOP[Infinite Loop While 1]
    LOOP --> SETON[Set P1.0 HIGH Relay ON]
    SETON --> DLY5[Call delay 5 second routine]
    DLY5 --> SETOFF[Clear P1.0 LOW Relay OFF]
    SETOFF --> DLY3[Call delay 3 second routine]
    DLY3 --> LOOP
    DLY5 --> SUB1[Load TH0 TL0 with 0x4BFD]
    SUB1 --> SUB2[Set TR0 1 Start Timer]
    SUB2 --> SUB3[Poll TF0 equal to 0 Wait]
    SUB3 --> SUB4[TF0 becomes 1 Overflow]
    SUB4 --> SUB5[Clear TR0 0 Stop Timer]
    SUB5 --> SUB6[Clear TF0 0 Reset flag]
    SUB6 --> SUB7[Decrement loop counter]
    SUB7 --> SUB8{Counter equal to 0}
    SUB8 --> NO --> SUB1
    SUB8 --> YES --> RET[Return to Main]
```

## 14. Signal Timing Diagram — 8051 Timer Delay

```mermaid
graph LR
    T0DRIVE[TR0 TCON.4] -->|rises at t0| RUN[Timer RUNNING]
    RUN -->|counts up| COUNT[TH0 TL0 0x4BFD to 0xFFFF]
    COUNT -->|at t0 + 50ms| OVERFLOW[Overflow event]
    OVERFLOW -->|hardware sets| FLAG[TF0 1]
    FLAG -->|software clears| CLEAR[TF0 0]
    CLEAR -->|next loop iteration| T0DRIVE
```

## 15. Functional Architecture — Complete Lab Setup

```mermaid
graph TD
    PC[PC with Keil uVision IDE] -->|USB ASP Programmer| PGMR[Programmer Hardware]
    PGMR -->|ISP SPI| MCU[AT89S52 on Breadboard]
    MCU -->|P1.0 P1.1| DRV[ULN2003 Relay Driver]
    DRV -->|Opto isolated coil drive| RLY[12V SPDT Relay]
    RLY -->|COM-NO contact| AC[230V AC Bulb]
    MCU -->|P3.0 P3.1| DBG[Optional UART to PC for Debug]
    MCU -->|P0.0 to P0.7| LCD[16x2 LCD for Status Display]
    MCU -->|XTAL1 XTAL2| XTAL[11.0592 MHz Crystal]
    MCU -->|EA pin HIGH| ROM[Internal 8KB Flash]
    MCU -->|VCC 5V and GND| POW[Regulated 5V Power Supply LM7805]
    POW -->|input 12V DC| ADAPT[12V DC Adapter]
    ADAPT -->|AC mains| MAIN[230V AC]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 16. Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]**

### Q1. Define **machine cycle** of the 8051. Compute its value for a crystal frequency of **11.0592 MHz** and **12 MHz**. [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

> The **machine cycle** is the basic unit of CPU work in the 8051, defined as the time taken to fetch, decode, and execute a single instruction cycle. The 8051 requires **12 oscillator pulses** to complete one machine cycle.
>
> Formula: $\text{MC} = 12 / f_{osc}$
>
> - For 11.0592 MHz: $\text{MC} = 12 / 11.0592 \times 10^{6} = 1.085\,\mu s$
> - For 12 MHz: $\text{MC} = 12 / 12 \times 10^{6} = 1.0\,\mu s$

---

### Q2. List all four timer modes of the 8051 with their **counter widths** and **maximum counts**. [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

| Mode | M1 | M0 | Width | Max Counts |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mode 0 | 0 | 0 | 13-bit | 8192 |
| Mode 1 | 0 | 1 | 16-bit | 65536 |
| Mode 2 | 1 | 0 | 8-bit auto-reload | 256 |
| Mode 3 | 1 | 1 | Two 8-bit (T0 split) | 256 each |

---

## 17. Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Timer Delay Generation

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern Adapted]**

**(a) Design a time delay of exactly 50 ms using Timer 0 in Mode 1 of an 8051 system with an 11.0592 MHz crystal. Derive the values to be loaded into TH0 and TL0 registers. Show all calculations step-by-step. [7 Marks]**

**(b) Write a complete 8051 C program to generate a 1 kHz square wave on P1.7 using the computed delay. [7 Marks]**

#### ✅ Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1 — Machine Cycle Computation.**

$$\text{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085\,\mu s$$

**Step 2 — Required Timer Counts for 50 ms.**

$$\text{Counts} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 46082.95 \approx 46083$$

**Step 3 — Preload (16-bit Mode 1).**

$$\text{Preload} = 65536 - 46083 = 19453$$

**Step 4 — Hex Conversion of 19453.**

$$\text{Decimal 19453} = \text{0x4BFD}$$

**Step 5 — Splitting into TH0 and TL0.**

$$\text{TH0} = 0x4B \quad \text{(high byte)}$$

$$\text{TL0} = 0xFD \quad \text{(low byte)}$$

**Step 6 — TMOD Value.**

$$\text{TMOD} = 0x01 \quad \text{(T0, Mode 1, software start, timer mode)}$$

**Step 7 — Verification.**

$$\text{Delay} = 46083 \times 1.085\,\mu s = 50.00\,ms \;\checkmark$$

#### ✅ Model Solution for Part (b):

For **1 kHz square wave**: $T_{period} = 1/1000 = 1000\,\mu s = 1\,ms$. Half-period = **500 µs** ON, 500 µs OFF. But 500 µs at 11.0592 MHz = 461 counts, which is achievable in Mode 1.

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit WAVE = P1^7;     // Output pin

void delay_500us(void) {
    TMOD = 0x01;      // T0, Mode 1
    TH0  = 0xFE;      // For 500 us, preload = 65536 - 461 = 65075 = 0xFE33
    TL0  = 0x33;
    TR0  = 1;         // Start timer
    while (TF0 == 0); // Wait for overflow
    TR0  = 0;         // Stop timer
    TF0  = 0;         // Clear flag
}

void main(void) {
    while (1) {
        WAVE = 1;        // HIGH
        delay_500us();
        WAVE = 0;        // LOW
        delay_500us();
    }
}
```

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Relay Interface (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern Adapted]**

**(a) Draw the circuit diagram of an 8051 port pin interfacing with a 5V DC relay using a BC547 transistor driver. Label all components and explain the role of the freewheeling diode. [7 Marks]**

**(b) Write a complete 8051 C program to turn ON a relay (connected to P2.0) for 5 seconds and OFF for 3 seconds alternately, using Timer 1 in Mode 1. Assume 11.0592 MHz crystal. [7 Marks]**

#### ✅ Model Solution for Part (a):

**Circuit Components:**

| Component | Specification | Function |
|---|---|---|
| R1 (Pull-up) | $4.7\,k\Omega$ | Ensures HIGH when transistor is OFF |
| R2 (Base) | $1\,k\Omega$ | Limits base current to ~4 mA |
| Q1 (Transistor) | BC547 NPN | Switches the relay coil |
| D1 (Diode) | 1N4007 | Suppresses back-EMF |
| K1 (Relay) | 5V SPDT, 70 mA coil | Switches AC load |

**Connections:**
- **8051 P2.0** → **R2 (1 kΩ)** → **Base of BC547**
- **Collector** → **+5 V supply** (through relay coil) **and** Diode cathode
- **Emitter** → **GND**
- Diode **anode** → Collector, Cathode → +5V (across coil, reverse-biased)
- Relay **COM** contact → AC Mains, **NO** contact → AC Load

**Role of Freewheeling Diode (1N4007):**

When the transistor switches OFF, the relay coil's magnetic field collapses, inducing a high reverse voltage (back-EMF) of the form $V = L \cdot (di/dt)$ that can reach several hundred volts, which would destroy the transistor. The 1N4007 diode, connected in reverse bias across the coil, becomes forward-biased during this transient and provides a freewheeling current path, clamping the voltage to ~0.7 V and protecting Q1.

#### ✅ Model Solution for Part (b):

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit RELAY = P2^0;

void delay_50ms_T1(void) {
    TMOD = (TMOD & 0x0F) | 0x10;   // T1, Mode 1, leave T0 unchanged
    TH1  = 0x4B;
    TL1  = 0xFD;
    TR1  = 1;
    while (TF1 == 0);
    TR1  = 0;
    TF1  = 0;
}

void delay_seconds_T1(unsigned int n) {
    unsigned int i, j;
    for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        for (j = 0; j < 20; j++) {
            delay_50ms_T1();
        }
    }
}

void main(void) {
    RELAY = 0;
    while (1) {
        RELAY = 1;                 // Relay ON
        delay_seconds_T1(5);       // 5 seconds
        RELAY = 0;                 // Relay OFF
        delay_seconds_T1(3);       // 3 seconds
    }
}
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> | # | Common Mistake | Marks Lost | Correct Approach |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | Forgetting to clear **TF0/TF1** in polled mode | **2–3 marks** | Always add `TF0 = 0;` after the polling loop |
> | 2 | Using **wrong TMOD value** (e.g., 0x02 for Mode 1) | **2 marks** | Mode 1 → `TMOD = 0x01` (T0) or `0x10` (T1) |
> | 3 | **Not preloading** TH/TL registers every loop | **3 marks** | Timer re-initializes on overflow, so reload is mandatory |
> | 4 | Writing the diode in **forward bias** | **1 mark** | Cathode to +V, anode to collector — remember the stripe |
> | 5 | Connecting relay coil **directly to MCU pin** | **3 marks** | Always use a driver (transistor/ULN2003) |
> | 6 | Using 12 MHz in delay formula but 11.0592 MHz crystal (or vice versa) | **2 marks** | Mention the crystal in your solution! |
> | 7 | Calculating `65536 - count` instead of correct two's complement | **1 mark** | Mode 1: Preload = 65536 − Count; Mode 2: Preload = 256 − Count |
> | 8 | Not stating **GATE = 0** for software start | **1 mark** | Mention GATE bit configuration in TMOD |

---

## 18. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**

- ✅ **Machine Cycle = 12 / Crystal Frequency** (in seconds) — this is the *single most important* formula.
- ✅ For 11.0592 MHz crystal: **MC = 1.085 µs**; For 12 MHz: **MC = 1 µs**.
- ✅ **TMOD register** is at address **0x89** and is **NOT bit-addressable** — must assign the whole byte.
- ✅ **TCON register** is at address **0x88** and **IS bit-addressable** — can use `TR0 = 1;` syntax.
- ✅ **Mode 1 = 16-bit**, Max 65536 counts → Max delay **71.1 ms** (at 11.0592 MHz).
- ✅ **Mode 2 = 8-bit auto-reload**, Max 256 counts → Max delay **277.8 µs** (at 11.0592 MHz). Used for UART.
- ✅ **Preload Formula (Mode 1):** `TH0 TL0 = 65536 - (Required Counts)`.
- ✅ **Preload Formula (Mode 2):** `TH0 = TL0 = 256 - (Required Counts)`.
- ✅ **Always** clear `TF0`/`TF1` flag manually in polled mode; re-load TH/TL in loop for repeated overflows.
- ✅ **8051 port pin** can source only **~10–15 mA** — relay coil needs **30–70 mA** → driver stage is mandatory.
- ✅ **Freewheeling diode** (1N4007) **cathode** to **+V side** of coil, **anode** to **transistor collector** side.
- ✅ **Optocoupler (PC817)** provides **galvanic isolation** — protects MCU from high-voltage transients.
- ✅ **ULN2003** is a 7-channel **Darlington driver**, each channel sinks up to **500 mA** — preferred for multi-relay boards.
- ✅ **1 Hz waveform** needs **T = 1 s**; compute half-period delay and toggle pin in an infinite loop.
- ✅ For **1 kHz square wave** at 11.0592 MHz: **half-period = 500 µs** → preload **0xFE33** in Mode 1.
- ✅ For **delays > 71.1 ms**: use a **nested software loop** around the timer overflow.
- ✅ The **GATE bit** in TMOD, when set, requires the **INTx pin to be HIGH** for the timer to run (hardware gating). For lab polled delays, GATE = 0.
- ✅ **Counter mode** (`C/T̄ = 1`): the timer counts **external pulses** on T0 (P3.4) or T1 (P3.5) — used for event counting, frequency measurement.
- ✅ Lab crystal is almost always **11.0592 MHz** for UART compatibility, **NOT 12 MHz** — verify before computing.
- ✅ In **C**, use `sbit RELAY = P1^0;` for bit-level port manipulation; in assembly, use `SETB P1.0` / `CLR P1.0`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DAC Interface with 8051/AT89S51 for Different Wave Form Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND IOT LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** DAC Interface with 8051/AT89S51 for Different Wave Form Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: DAC Interface with 8051/AT89S51 for Different Wave Form Generation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** is an electronic circuit that converts a digital binary code (typically an 8-bit or 12-bit parallel word) into a proportional analog voltage or current output. In the context of the **AT89S51 microcontroller laboratory**, the DAC is interfaced to one of the parallel I/O ports (typically **Port 1, P1.0 – P1.7**) to generate standard time-varying analog waveforms such as **square wave, triangular wave, sawtooth wave, and sine wave**.

The laboratory experiment involves writing an embedded C program (or assembly code) that repeatedly writes a sequence of digital values to a DAC chip (commonly **DAC0808** or **AD7524**) such that the output analog voltage traces a desired periodic waveform. The AT89S51 acts as a **programmable data source** with deterministic timing, while the DAC acts as a **digital-to-analog reconstruction engine**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Expectation:** Examiners treat waveform-generation programs as **"look-and-write" type** viva questions. You must know the **lookup-table mathematics** for sine wave generation and the **control logic** for ramp-based waveforms (square, triangular, sawtooth).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tap (faucet) controlled by a digital counter**:

- The counter has positions **0, 1, 2, ..., 255** (an 8-bit counter).
- At position 0, the tap is **fully closed** → 0 liters per second.
- At position 255, the tap is **fully open** → maximum flow.
- Each intermediate position opens the tap by a small, fixed increment.

Now, if you **rapidly cycle the counter through different sequences**, the resulting water flow traces a shape in time:

- Counter alternates 0 → 255 → 0 → 255 → ... → **Square Wave (water blasts + silence)**
- Counter walks 0 → 1 → 2 → ... → 255 → 0 → 1 → 2 → ... → **Sawtooth Wave (ramp up, snap back)**
- Counter walks up then walks down → **Triangular Wave (ramp up, ramp down)**
- Counter follows pre-computed values that mimic a sine curve → **Sine Wave**

The **DAC0808** is the "tap", the **AT89S51 Port 1** is the "counter" (driven by a C program), and the **op-amp current-to-voltage converter (LM741 or TL082)** is what converts the tap's water flow (current) into a measurable water height (voltage) you can see on a CRO.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Constants to Remember (highlighted for KTU viva):**
> - **Resolution of an 8-bit DAC = $1/2^8 = 1/256 \approx 0.003906$** (or **3.906 mV per LSB** for a 1 V reference).
> - **DAC0808 settling time = 150 ns** (the chip is "fast" — the waveform's frequency is limited by the software, not the DAC).
> - **Reference current $I_{ref} = V_{ref}/R_{ref}$** — typically $V_{ref} = +5\text{ V}$ and $R_{ref} = 5.1\text{ k}\Omega$, giving $I_{ref} \approx 0.98\text{ mA}$.
> - **Full-scale output current $I_{fs} = I_{ref} \times (255/256) \approx 0.98\text{ mA}$.**

### Why This Experiment Is in the KTU 2024 Lab Curriculum

| Aspect | Educational Value |
|---|---|
| **Hardware-Software Co-design** | Bridges "C program logic" with "external analog circuitry" |
| **Real-Time Embedded Thinking** | Students must calculate loop delays to set frequency |
| **Signal Reconstruction Theory** | Demonstrates the Sampling Theorem (Nyquist) in practice |
| **Industry Relevance** | Foundation for audio codecs, function generators, motor control (PWM → analog) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of the DAC0808 (8-Bit Current-Output DAC)

The **DAC0808** is a **monolithic 8-bit D/A converter** built using an **R-2R ladder network** of diffused resistors. It has:

- 8 digital input pins (A1–A8) accepting TTL-compatible logic.
- Two complementary current outputs: $I_{out}$ (active) and $\overline{I_{out}}$ (inverted).
- A reference current pin $I_{ref}$ that sets the full-scale current.
- A high-impedance reference input that can be driven by a voltage reference or a current source.

### The Core DAC Transfer Function

For a digital byte $D$ written to the DAC inputs (where $D$ is the unsigned integer 0–255):

$$I_{out} = I_{ref} \times \frac{D}{256}$$

The complementary output gives:

$$\overline{I_{out}} = I_{ref} \times \frac{(255 - D)}{256}$$

Note that $I_{out} + \overline{I_{out}} = I_{ref}$ (this is a fundamental property — the sum of both currents is always constant and equal to the reference current, regardless of the digital code).

### Conversion to Voltage via Op-Amp (Current-to-Voltage Converter)

The DAC0808 produces a **current**, not a voltage. To get a usable voltage, we use an **inverting op-amp configuration** (LM741, TL082, or any general-purpose op-amp):

$$V_{out} = -I_{out} \times R_f = -I_{ref} \times R_f \times \frac{D}{256}$$

The negative sign is the standard inverting-amplifier output polarity. By connecting the op-amp output back through a feedback resistor $R_f$ (typically **5 kΩ**), the op-amp forces the current to flow through $R_f$, producing a voltage proportional to $D$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip (KTU):** If the examiner asks "Why do we need an op-amp with DAC0808?" — your answer is: **"The DAC0808 is a current-output device. It cannot drive a low-impedance load directly because its output impedance is high. The op-amp converts the output current to a voltage and also provides low output impedance so the signal can be observed on a CRO or fed to a load."**

## 2.2 The AT89S51 Microcontroller — Host Side

The **AT89S51** is an 8-bit CMOS microcontroller from Atmel (now Microchip) with:

- **4 KB of internal Flash program memory** (re-programmable in-system).
- **128 bytes of internal RAM**.
- **Four 8-bit I/O ports**: P0, P1, P2, P3.
- **Two 16-bit timers/counters** (T0, T1).
- **Full-duplex UART**, on-chip oscillator.

For the DAC interface experiment:

| 8051 Port | Typical Use in DAC Experiment | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Port 1 (P1.0 – P1.7)** | Connected to DAC data inputs | Port 1 has internal pull-ups; no need for external resistors |
| **Port 0** | Sometimes used (with external 10 kΩ pull-ups) | Has open-drain outputs — requires external pull-ups |
| **P3.0, P3.1** | Not used here | Reserved for UART if serial debugging needed |

The C program writes a byte to the DAC by simply assigning a value to `P1`:

```c
P1 = digital_value;   // P1 is the special-function register at SFR address 0x90
```

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{out} = I_{ref} \cdot (D / 256)$ | DAC output current as a function of digital input $D$ | $D$ is 0–255, dimensionless |
| 2 | $I_{ref} = V_{ref} / R_{ref}$ | Reference current set by external circuit | Amperes |
| 3 | $V_{out} = -I_{out} \cdot R_f$ | Op-amp current-to-voltage conversion | Volts |
| 4 | $V_{out} = -V_{ref} \cdot (R_f / R_{ref}) \cdot (D / 256)$ | Combined transfer function | Choose $R_f = R_{ref}$ for unity |
| 5 | $V_{out(FS)} = -V_{ref} \cdot (255 / 256)$ | Full-scale output voltage | $\approx -V_{ref}$ |
| 6 | $\text{Resolution} = V_{ref} / 2^n = V_{ref} / 256$ | Smallest step size (1 LSB) | Volts per LSB |
| 7 | $f_{square} = 1 / (2 \cdot T_{total})$ | Square wave frequency | Hz; $T_{total}$ = high-time + low-time |
| 8 | $f_{tri} = 1 / (2 \cdot N \cdot T_{d})$ | Triangular wave frequency (ramp up + ramp down) | Hz; $N$ = 256 samples, $T_d$ = per-step delay |
| 9 | $f_{sine} = 1 / (N \cdot T_{d})$ | Sine wave frequency (N points per cycle) | Hz; typical $N = 32$ or $64$ |
| 10 | $D_{sine}(n) = 127 + 127 \cdot \sin(2\pi n / N)$ | Lookup-table value for sine wave (offset binary) | $n = 0, 1, ..., N-1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** The op-amp inverting sign is dropped in many textbooks by writing `+Vout`. In the actual KTU lab kit, the op-amp is wired in **unity-gain inverting mode** with $R_f = R_{ref}$, so $V_{out} = -V_{ref} \times (D/256)$. The CRO shows a **negative-going** waveform. Students are sometimes confused — this is normal, NOT a wiring fault.

## 2.4 Waveform Generation — Control Logic

### A. Square Wave Generation

The simplest waveform. The program alternates between two digital values (e.g., 0x00 and 0xFF) with a software delay between them. The **frequency** is set by the total period (high time + low time).

**Algorithm:**
1. Set $P1 = 0x00$ (output = 0 V).
2. Delay for $T_{high} = T/2$.
3. Set $P1 = 0xFF$ (output = max negative).
4. Delay for $T_{low} = T/2$.
5. Go to step 1 (infinite loop).

### B. Triangular Wave Generation

The triangular wave is generated by **ramping the digital value linearly up** from 0 to 255, then **ramping linearly down** from 255 to 0. The output is continuous (no sudden jumps). The CRO trace looks like the letter "V" laid on its side: /\/\/\/.

**Algorithm:**
1. Initialize counter `i = 0`.
2. `P1 = i;` (output the current value).
3. Delay $T_d$ microseconds.
4. If `i < 255`, increment `i`; else, reverse direction and decrement.
5. Repeat.

### C. Sawtooth Wave Generation

The sawtooth is a **ramp up followed by an instantaneous snap back** to 0. Unlike the triangular wave, the sawtooth has an **abrupt vertical drop** at the end of each cycle. The CRO trace looks like a saw blade: /|/|/|/.

**Algorithm:**
1. `for (i = 0; i < 256; i++) { P1 = i; delay(Td); }`
2. Immediately repeat from 0.

### D. Sine Wave Generation (Lookup Table Method)

Since the 8051 cannot compute `sin()` in real time, the sine values are **pre-computed at design time** and stored in a **lookup table** (an array in program memory or RAM). The program then iterates through this table cyclically.

**Mathematical basis:** For an 8-bit DAC, the sine wave must be **centered at the mid-scale (128)** and **scaled to fit the 0–255 range**. The standard formula is:

$$D_{sine}(n) = 127 + 127 \cdot \sin\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N}\right)$$

where $N$ is the number of points per cycle (e.g., $N = 32$ for moderate resolution, $N = 64$ for smoother output).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Issue — Sampling Rate vs. Waveform Smoothness:** With $N = 32$ samples per cycle, if the software delay between samples is $T_d = 100\text{ }\mu s$, the output frequency is $f = 1 / (32 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}) = 312.5\text{ Hz}$. If the delay is too long, you hear "steps" in the output. If the delay is too short, the DAC output looks "good" but the 8051 has no time to do anything else.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

| Application | How This Technique Is Used |
|---|---|
| **Function Generators** | DDS (Direct Digital Synthesis) chips use exactly this technique at MHz speeds |
| **Audio Output (Buzzer/Melody)** | PWM from 8051 → low-pass filter = sine-like tone |
| **Motor Speed Control** | DAC outputs analog voltage → drives VFD or DC motor controller |
| **Signal Conditioning in IoT** | Sensor data is digital; DAC provides analog reference voltages |
| **Oscilloscope Test Signal** | 1 kHz calibration sine wave from a 8051 + DAC |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sine Wave Lookup Table Values

The goal is to compute the 8-bit digital values that, when fed to the DAC in sequence, will produce a smooth sine wave on the CRO. We will derive the formula step by step.

### Step 1 — Normalize the Sine Function

The mathematical sine function produces values in the range $[-1, +1]$. The 8051's DAC accepts values in the range $[0, 255]$. We need to map one range to the other.

Let $y = \sin(\theta)$ where $\theta = 2\pi n / N$ and $n \in \{0, 1, 2, ..., N-1\}$.

So:

$$-1 \leq \sin\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N}\right) \leq +1$$

### Step 2 — Shift the Range to $[0, 2]$

Add 1 to the sine value to shift the range from $[-1, +1]$ to $[0, 2]$:

$$0 \leq 1 + \sin\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N}\right) \leq 2$$

### Step 3 — Scale to $[0, 1]$

Divide by 2 to normalize:

$$0 \leq \frac{1 + \sin(2\pi n / N)}{2} \leq 1$$

### Step 4 — Scale to $[0, 255]$ (the DAC range)

Multiply by 255 to map to the 8-bit DAC range:

$$D(n) = 255 \cdot \frac{1 + \sin(2\pi n / N)}{2}$$

This is the **general formula** for an 8-bit DAC. Some texts use 254 instead of 255 to avoid the "always-at-rail" full-scale value; the difference is negligible.

### Step 5 — Simplify to Standard Form

Expanding the above:

$$D(n) = \frac{255}{2} + \frac{255}{2} \cdot \sin\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N}\right)$$

$$D(n) = 127.5 + 127.5 \cdot \sin\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N}\right)$$

Rounding 127.5 to 127 (since 8051 C truncates), the standard KTU formula is:

$$\boxed{D_{sine}(n) = 127 + 127 \cdot \sin\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N}\right)}$$

### Worked Example: N = 8 Samples Per Cycle

For $N = 8$, the angles are $\theta_n = 0°, 45°, 90°, 135°, 180°, 225°, 270°, 315°$.

| $n$ | $\theta_n$ (deg) | $\sin(\theta_n)$ | $D(n)$ | Hex |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0.000 | 127 | 0x7F |
| 1 | 45 | +0.707 | 217 | 0xD9 |
| 2 | 90 | +1.000 | 254 | 0xFE |
| 3 | 135 | +0.707 | 217 | 0xD9 |
| 4 | 180 | 0.000 | 127 | 0x7F |
| 5 | 225 | -0.707 | 37 | 0x25 |
| 6 | 270 | -1.000 | 0 | 0x00 |
| 7 | 315 | -0.707 | 37 | 0x25 |

So the 8051 program would have a lookup table:

```c
unsigned char sine_table[8] = {127, 217, 254, 217, 127, 37, 0, 37};
```

### Step 6 — Calculating the Resulting Frequency

The frequency of the generated sine wave depends on two factors:
1. The number of samples per cycle ($N$)
2. The delay between consecutive samples ($T_d$)

The total period of one cycle is:

$$T_{cycle} = N \times T_d$$

Therefore the frequency is:

$$f_{sine} = \frac{1}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{1}{N \times T_d}$$

**Example:** If $N = 32$ and $T_d = 100\text{ }\mu s$ (typical software delay), then:

$$f_{sine} = \frac{1}{32 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{3.2 \times 10^{-3}} = 312.5\text{ Hz}$$

This is a clearly audible tone on a CRO speaker.

## 3.2 Derivation: Triangular Wave Period and Frequency

A triangular wave is composed of an **upward ramp** (0 → 255) followed by a **downward ramp** (255 → 0). If the delay per step is $T_d$, then:

- **Upward ramp duration:** $T_{up} = 255 \times T_d$
- **Downward ramp duration:** $T_{down} = 255 \times T_d$
- **Total period:** $T_{tri} = T_{up} + T_{down} = 510 \times T_d$

The frequency is:

$$f_{tri} = \frac{1}{T_{tri}} = \frac{1}{510 \times T_d}$$

**Refined Formula (slightly different in software loops):** Since the program counter touches 256 distinct values (0 through 255), and ramps both up and down, the time is:

$$f_{tri} \approx \frac{1}{2 \times 256 \times T_d} = \frac{1}{512 \times T_d}$$

For a target frequency $f_{tri} = 1\text{ kHz}$:

$$T_d = \frac{1}{512 \times 1000} = 1.953\text{ }\mu s$$

This is achievable on a 12 MHz 8051 (one machine cycle = 1 µs) using just 1–2 NOP instructions for the delay.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Waveform Summary

| Waveform Type | Output Code Sequence | Software Logic | Frequency Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Square Wave** | 0x00 → 0xFF → 0x00 → ... | Two assignments + delay, in a loop | $f = 1 / (2 \times \text{delay})$ |
| **Triangular Wave** | 0, 1, 2, ..., 255, 254, ..., 1, 0, 1, ... | Counter increments up to 255, then decrements | $f \approx 1 / (512 \times T_d)$ |
| **Sawtooth Wave** | 0, 1, 2, ..., 255, 0, 1, ... | Counter increments in a `for` loop from 0 to 255 | $f = 1 / (256 \times T_d)$ |
| **Sine Wave** | Lookup table values | Index cycles 0 → N-1 through a pre-computed array | $f = 1 / (N \times T_d)$ |
| **Staircase** | Holds one value for K cycles, then steps | Used for testing DAC linearity | $f = 1 / (K \times \text{steps})$ |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Generating a 1 kHz Square Wave

**Target:** Generate a 1 kHz square wave on Port 1.

**Calculation:**

The period of a 1 kHz signal is $T = 1 / f = 1\text{ ms}$. Since the square wave is symmetric, the **high time** and **low time** are each $T/2 = 0.5\text{ ms} = 500\text{ }\mu s$.

**Software Delay for 500 µs on 12 MHz 8051:**

The 8051 with an 11.0592 MHz crystal has a machine cycle of $12 / 11.0592 = 1.085\text{ µs}$. With a 12 MHz crystal, the machine cycle is exactly 1 µs.

A typical inline delay function:

```c
void delay_us(unsigned int us) {
    while (us--) {
        // Each iteration of the inner while loop = ~1 µs
        // (8 NOPs + 2 decrement + 2 jump = 12 machine cycles)
    }
}
```

For 500 µs at 12 MHz:

$$N_{delay} = 500 \text{ iterations}$$

The complete C program for 1 kHz square wave:

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void delay(unsigned int count) {
    while (count--);
}

void main(void) {
    while (1) {
        P1 = 0x00;       // Output low
        delay(500);      // 500 µs delay
        P1 = 0xFF;       // Output high
        delay(500);      // 500 µs delay
    }
}
```

**Verification on CRO:** You should see a square wave with **peak-to-peak voltage** of $V_{ref} \times (255/256) \approx V_{ref} = 5\text{ V}$ (or 10 V if $V_{ref} = 10\text{ V}$), period of 1 ms, and **rise/fall times limited by DAC0808's 150 ns settling time** (essentially vertical edges).

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: Generating a Sine Wave with N = 32

**Lookup Table Computation** (16 representative values shown for brevity):

| $n$ | $D(n)$ | Hex | $n$ | $D(n)$ | Hex |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 127 | 0x7F | 16 | 127 | 0x7F |
| 1 | 152 | 0x98 | 17 | 102 | 0x66 |
| 2 | 176 | 0xB0 | 18 | 78 | 0x4E |
| 3 | 198 | 0xC6 | 19 | 56 | 0x38 |
| 4 | 217 | 0xD9 | 20 | 37 | 0x25 |
| 5 | 233 | 0xE9 | 21 | 22 | 0x16 |
| 6 | 245 | 0xF5 | 22 | 11 | 0x0B |
| 7 | 252 | 0xFC | 23 | 4 | 0x04 |
| 8 | 254 | 0xFE | 24 | 0 | 0x00 |
| 9 | 252 | 0xFC | 25 | 4 | 0x04 |
| 10 | 245 | 0xF5 | 26 | 11 | 0x0B |
| 11 | 233 | 0xE9 | 27 | 22 | 0x16 |
| 12 | 217 | 0xD9 | 28 | 37 | 0x25 |
| 13 | 198 | 0xC6 | 29 | 56 | 0x38 |
| 14 | 176 | 0xB0 | 30 | 78 | 0x4E |
| 15 | 152 | 0x98 | 31 | 102 | 0x66 |

**C Program (Embedded):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

// Lookup table for one full cycle of sine wave (N = 32 samples)
unsigned char code sine_lut[32] = {
    127, 152, 176, 198, 217, 233, 245, 252,
    254, 252, 245, 233, 217, 198, 176, 152,
    127, 102,  78,  56,  37,  22,  11,   4,
      0,   4,  11,  22,  37,  56,  78, 102
};

void delay(unsigned int count) {
    while (count--);
}

void main(void) {
    unsigned char i;
    while (1) {
        for (i = 0; i < 32; i++) {
            P1 = sine_lut[i];   // Write to DAC
            delay(100);          // ~100 µs delay between samples
        }
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `code` keyword?** The `code` memory specifier in Keil C51 stores the lookup table in **Flash (program) memory** (0x0000–0x0FFF in AT89S51) rather than RAM. This frees up the limited 128 bytes of internal RAM for variables. It is the **KTU-preferred** way to define lookup tables in 8051 C programming.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical: Choosing Delay for Target Sine Frequency

Suppose you want a **500 Hz sine wave** with $N = 32$ samples per cycle.

**Rearranging the formula:**

$$f = \frac{1}{N \times T_d} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_d = \frac{1}{N \times f}$$

$$T_d = \frac{1}{32 \times 500} = \frac{1}{16000} = 62.5\text{ }\mu s$$

So you would set the delay count to 62 (rounded). On a 12 MHz 8051, this corresponds to 62 µs of pure software delay, which is achievable in a simple `while (count--);` loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this is important in KTU viva:** The examiner often asks: *"How will you change the frequency of the generated sine wave without changing the hardware?"* The answer is: **"By changing the software delay between successive samples. A smaller delay means a higher frequency, and vice versa. This is the basis of Direct Digital Synthesis (DDS)."**
