**(a) Design and explain an Arduino-based system to read an analog voltage from a potentiometer and display both the raw ADC value and the equivalent voltage on a 16×2 LCD. Provide the complete circuit diagram and program. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Connections:**
- Potentiometer Terminal 1 → Arduino **5V**
- Potentiometer Terminal 3 → Arduino **GND**
- Potentiometer Wiper → Arduino **A0**
- LCD (with I2C) VCC → **5V**, GND → **GND**
- LCD SDA → Arduino **A4**, SCL → Arduino **A5**

**Working Principle:**
The wiper of the potentiometer produces a variable voltage $V_{out} \in [0, 5]\,\text{V}$. The Arduino's internal ADC samples this voltage and produces a 10-bit digital value $D \in [0, 1023]$. The program uses the formula $V_{in} = \dfrac{D \times 5.0}{1023.0}$ to reconstruct the voltage, which is then displayed on both the **Serial Monitor** and the **LCD**.

**Program:**

```cpp
#include <Wire.h>
#include <LiquidCrystal_I2C.h>

LiquidCrystal_I2C lcd(0x27, 16, 2);
int potPin = A0;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(9600);
  lcd.init();
  lcd.backlight();
  lcd.print("ADC System Ready");
  delay(1500);
  lcd.clear();
}

void loop() {
  int raw = analogRead(potPin);
  float v  = (raw * 5.0) / 1023.0;

  Serial.print("ADC: "); Serial.print(raw);
  Serial.print(" | V: ");  Serial.println(v, 3);

  lcd.setCursor(0, 0); lcd.print("ADC: "); lcd.print(raw); lcd.print("   ");
  lcd.setCursor(0, 1); lcd.print("V: ");   lcd.print(v, 2);  lcd.print(" V ");
  delay(300);
}
```

**Output Verification:** When the knob is at **mid-position**, $V_{out} \approx 2.5\,\text{V}$ and $D = 511$. At **fully clockwise**, $D = 1023$ and $V = 5.00\,\text{V}$. At **fully counter-clockwise**, $D = 0$ and $V = 0.00\,\text{V}$.

**(7 marks breakdown: 2 for circuit, 3 for program, 1 for formula, 1 for verification]**

---

**(b) A temperature sensor LM35 outputs 10 mV/°C. It is connected to Arduino A1 through a voltage divider to scale a 0–150 °C range to 0–5 V. Calculate the required resistor ratio, and write the Arduino code to display the temperature on the Serial Monitor. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the maximum sensor output.**
At 150 °C, the LM35 outputs:

$$
V_{sensor,max} = 10\,\text{mV/°C} \times 150\,\text{°C} = 1500\,\text{mV} = 1.5\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 2 — The 1.5 V is within the Arduino's 0–5 V range, so no external voltage divider is strictly needed.** However, to **use the full ADC dynamic range** (and improve resolution), we can amplify the signal.

**Step 3 — Amplification factor required.**

$$
\text{Gain} = \frac{5\,\text{V}}{1.5\,\text{V}} = \frac{10}{3} \approx 3.33
$$

**Step 4 — Using a non-inverting op-amp configuration** with $R_f = 47\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_1 = 22\,\text{k}\Omega$:

$$
A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \frac{47}{22} \approx 3.14
$$

**Step 5 — Arduino code to display temperature.**

```cpp
int sensorPin = A1;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(9600);
}

void loop() {
  int raw = analogRead(sensorPin);
  float voltage = (raw * 5.0) / 1023.0;
  float temperature = voltage * 100.0;   // Since 10 mV/°C => 1 V = 100 °C

  Serial.print("Temp: ");
  Serial.print(temperature, 1);
  Serial.println(" C");
  delay(500);
}
```

**Step 6 — Verification:** At ADC reading of 307 (≈ 1.5 V), the displayed temperature should be **150.0 °C**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the successive approximation method of ADC used in Arduino UNO. Compare its speed and accuracy with the flash and dual-slope types. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Successive Approximation (SAR) Working:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Vin] --> B[Sample and Hold]
    B --> C[Comparator]
    C --> D{SAR Control Logic}
    D --> E[Internal DAC]
    E --> F[Trial Voltage]
    F --> C
    D --> G[Output Register 10 bits]
    G --> H[Digital Output 0 to 1023]
```

**Working Principle:**
1. The SAR starts with the MSB = 1 and all other bits = 0, producing a trial voltage $V_{trial} = V_{ref}/2$.
2. The comparator checks if $V_{in} > V_{trial}$. If yes, the MSB remains 1; if no, it is reset to 0.
3. The next bit is tested, with $V_{trial}$ quartered, and so on.
4. After **10 clock cycles**, the final 10-bit code is locked.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | SAR (Arduino) | Flash | Dual-Slope |
|-----------|---------------|-------|------------|
| Resolution | Medium (10-bit typical) | Low (6–8 bit practical) | Very High (16–24 bit) |
| Speed | Fast (n cycles) | Fastest (1 cycle) | Slow (2ⁿ cycles) |
| Hardware Complexity | Medium | Very High (2ⁿ − 1 comparators) | Low |
| Cost | Low | High | Low |
| Accuracy | Good | Poor at high res | Excellent |
| Use Case | Arduino, microcontrollers | Oscilloscopes, video | Digital multimeters |

**Conclusion for Arduino:** SAR is the best trade-off between speed, cost, and resolution for embedded applications.

**(7 marks: 3 for working, 2 for diagram, 2 for comparison]**

---

**(b) A gas sensor outputs a voltage between 0.2 V and 4.0 V corresponding to gas concentrations of 0 ppm and 1000 ppm respectively. Write the Arduino code to: (i) read the analog value, (ii) convert it to ppm, and (iii) display it on the Serial Monitor with a warning if concentration > 500 ppm. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Establish the linear mapping.**

$$
\text{ppm} = \frac{V_{in} - 0.2}{4.0 - 0.2} \times 1000
$$

**Step 2 — Derive the relationship in terms of ADC reading $D$.**

$$
\text{ppm} = \frac{\left(\dfrac{D \times 5.0}{1023.0}\right) - 0.2}{3.8} \times 1000
$$

**Step 3 — Arduino Code.**

```cpp
int sensorPin = A2;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(9600);
  Serial.println("Gas Monitoring System Initialized");
}

void loop() {
  int raw = analogRead(sensorPin);
  float voltage = (raw * 5.0) / 1023.0;
  float ppm = ((voltage - 0.2) / 3.8) * 1000.0;

  if (ppm < 0)   ppm = 0;
  if (ppm > 1000) ppm = 1000;

  Serial.print("Gas: ");
  Serial.print(ppm, 1);
  Serial.print(" ppm");

  if (ppm > 500) {
    Serial.println("  *** ALERT: HIGH GAS LEVEL ***");
  } else {
    Serial.println("  Status: Normal");
  }
  delay(1000);
}
```

**Step 4 — Verification:**
- $V_{in} = 0.2\,\text{V} \Rightarrow \text{ppm} = 0$
- $V_{in} = 2.1\,\text{V} \Rightarrow \text{ppm} = 500$ (warning threshold)
- $V_{in} = 4.0\,\text{V} \Rightarrow \text{ppm} = 1000$ (maximum)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting `#include <Wire.h>`** before `#include <LiquidCrystal_I2C.h>` will cause a **compilation error** — students lose 2–3 marks here.
> 2. **Wrong I2C address:** Most PCF8574-based LCD modules use **0x27**, but some use **0x3F**. If nothing displays, run an **I2C scanner sketch** first.
> 3. **Forgetting `lcd.init()` and `lcd.backlight()`** in `setup()` will result in a blank screen — this is the #1 debugging issue.
> 4. **Integer division trap:** Writing `raw * 5 / 1023` gives wrong results because the numerator `raw * 5` is computed as an **integer** first. Always use **`raw * 5.0 / 1023.0`** (float literals) to avoid truncation.
> 5. **Confusing 1023 with 1024:** The maximum digital value is **1023**, not 1024. Using 1024 in the formula gives a slightly higher voltage reading (off by 4.88 mV at full scale).
> 6. **Not using `lcd.print("   ")` to clear residual characters:** When the ADC value changes from 1023 to 5, the old digit "1023" leaves a "3" on screen. Always pad with spaces or use `lcd.clear()`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Your Lab Exam!**

- ✅ Arduino UNO ADC is **10-bit**, range **0–1023**, with **6 channels (A0–A5)** and a default $V_{ref}$ of **5 V**.
- ✅ Resolution / step size: **4.88 mV per LSB** ($\frac{5\,\text{V}}{1024}$).
- ✅ Maximum quantization error: **±2.44 mV** ($\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB).
- ✅ Conversion formula: $D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 1023 \right\rfloor$.
- ✅ Voltage reconstruction: $V_{in} = \dfrac{D \times 5.0}{1023.0}$ (use `5.0`, not `5`, to force float).
- ✅ The ATmega328P uses a **SAR (Successive Approximation Register) ADC**, not a flash or dual-slope type.
- ✅ Potentiometer acts as a **voltage divider** — the wiper voltage depends on the ratio of the two resistive segments.
- ✅ The `analogRead(pin)` function returns an **integer** between 0 and 1023. Use `map()` or manual math for voltage conversion.
- ✅ The `Serial.begin(9600)` call **must** be present in `setup()` for Serial Monitor to work.
- ✅ For **I2C LCD**, the required library is `LiquidCrystal_I2C.h` and the typical address is **0x27**.
- ✅ The Serial Monitor **baud rate** in the IDE **must match** the program's `Serial.begin()` value (default 9600).
- ✅ The input voltage to any analog pin **must not exceed 5 V** — higher voltages permanently damage the ATmega328P.
- ✅ Use `analogReference(DEFAULT)` for 5 V, `analogReference(INTERNAL)` for **1.1 V** (improves resolution for low-voltage sensors).
- ✅ The Arduino's `analogRead()` takes approximately **100 µs** (default prescaler = 128), allowing about **10,000 samples per second**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sending Sensor Data from Arduino to Cloud Using WiFi Module ESP8266 and Things Speak Account
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND IOT LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Sending Sensor Data from Arduino to Cloud Using WiFi Module ESP8266 and Things Speak Account

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IoT Cloud Data Telemetry** is the process of capturing real-world physical parameters (temperature, humidity, gas, light, etc.) using embedded sensors, processing the data with a microcontroller, and transmitting it over a wireless network to a remote cloud-based storage and visualization platform using standardized **HTTP/MQTT protocols** for subsequent analytics, monitoring, and control.

In the context of the **KTU PCRUL408** syllabus, this experiment demonstrates a classic **3-tier IoT architecture**:

- **Perception Layer:** Sensors (e.g., DHT11, LM35, LDR, MQ-2).
- **Network Layer:** Arduino UNO + ESP8266 (NodeMCU/WiFi shield).
- **Application Layer:** ThingSpeak Cloud (owned by MathWorks/MATLAB).

## 1.2 Component Definitions

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Arduino UNO** | 8-bit ATmega328P microcontroller, acts as the data aggregator and sensor interface. |
| **ESP8266** | Low-cost **802.11 b/g/n** WiFi SoC manufactured by Espressif Systems. Operates on **2.4 GHz** band. |
| **ThingSpeak** | Open IoT analytics platform that uses **RESTful API** to receive, store, and visualize data. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of this experiment like a "Digital Letter Posting System":**
> 
> 1. **The Sensor** is your *eyes* (it sees temperature, gas, light).
> 2. **The Arduino** is your *brain* (it processes what the eyes see and writes a letter).
> 3. **The ESP8266** is the *postman* (it carries the letter over the air to the internet).
> 4. **ThingSpeak** is the *letterbox* at the post office (it stores every letter and even plots a graph so you can see trends over time).
> 
> Without the postman, your letter never leaves the house. Without the letterbox, no one receives it. The WiFi module and cloud complete the loop of the **Internet of Things (IoT)**.

## 1.4 Key Operational Constants and Standards

- **WiFi Standard:** IEEE **802.11 b/g/n** at **2.4 GHz**.
- **Default ESP8266 Baud Rate:** **115200 bps** (older firmware: 9600).
- **ThingSpeak Update Interval (free license):** minimum **15 seconds** per channel.
- **API Key Length:** **16 alphanumeric characters** (Write Key) and **18 characters** (Read Key).
- **Operating Voltage:** ESP8266 runs on **3.3V** (NOT 5V — this is a common hardware pitfall).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: How Data Reaches the Cloud

The data pipeline follows a strict sequence governed by the **TCP/IP stack**:

1. **Sensor Acquisition** → Analog/Digital signal read by Arduino ADC (10-bit, **0–1023**).
2. **Data Conditioning** → Conversion to engineering units (°C, %, ppm).
3. **Serial Hand-off** → Arduino sends the formatted string via **UART (TX/RX)** to the ESP8266.
4. **WiFi Encapsulation** → ESP8266 wraps the data inside an **HTTP GET request**.
5. **Internet Routing** → Packet travels via router to the ThingSpeak server.
6. **Server Processing** → ThingSpeak parses the URL, authenticates the API key, and stores the data point.

## 2.2 The ThingSpeak REST API – The "Why"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **REST (Representational State Transfer)** is an architectural style where every sensor reading is treated as a *resource* accessed via a unique URL. The **API Key** acts as a password embedded inside the URL, ensuring only authorized devices can push data to your channel.

### 2.2.1 URL Structure Used by the ESP8266

The ESP8266 sends a standard HTTP GET request of the form:

```
GET https://api.thingspeak.com/update?api_key=YOUR_WRITE_KEY&field1=value1&field2=value2
```

Each `field1`, `field2`, `field3`... up to `field8` represents an independent sensor channel on the same ThingSpeak channel.

## 2.3 ESP8266 Operating Modes

The ESP8266 supports three operational modes. For this lab, we use **Station Mode (STA)**:

| Mode | Description | Lab Usage |
|------|-------------|-----------|
| **STA (Station)** | Connects to an existing WiFi router | **Used in this experiment** |
| **AP (Access Point)** | Creates its own WiFi hotspot | Used for direct device configuration |
| **STA + AP** | Both simultaneously | Used in mesh/advanced networks |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Purpose |
|---------------------|------------|-------|---------|
| ADC to Voltage | $V = \frac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{1024}$ | Volts (V) | Convert raw Arduino ADC reading |
| ADC to Temperature (LM35, 10 mV/°C) | $T = \frac{V \times 1000}{10}$ | °C | Linear LM35 conversion |
| DHT11 Humidity | $H = \text{byte2 + (byte3}/10)$ | % RH | From DHT sensor packet |
| DHT11 Temperature | $T = \text{byte4 + (byte5}/10)$ | °C | From DHT sensor packet |
| HTTP Request Time-out | $t_{TO} = 5\text{ s}$ (typical) | seconds | Prevents infinite blocking |
| Minimum Update Interval | $\Delta t \ge 15$ | seconds | ThingSpeak free-tier limit |
| Baud Rate (UART) | $f_{baud} = 115200$ | bps | ESP8266 communication speed |
| Frame Duration | $T_{frame} = \frac{N_{bits}}{f_{baud}}$ | seconds | 10-bit frame = 1 start + 8 data + 1 stop |

## 2.5 Why This System is Used in Engineering

- **Remote Patient Monitoring** (heart rate, SpO2 to cloud).
- **Smart Agriculture** (soil moisture to ThingSpeak dashboard).
- **Industrial SCADA** (gas leakage alerts in chemical plants).
- **Weather Stations** (multi-parameter atmospheric telemetry).
- **Smart Energy Meters** (kWh consumption over WiFi to utility dashboards).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Experimental Procedure, Logic & Code Derivation

## 3.1 Hardware Requirements (Full Inventory)

| # | Component | Quantity | Purpose |
|---|-----------|----------|---------|
| 1 | Arduino UNO | 1 | Master controller |
| 2 | ESP8266 (NodeMCU or WiFi Shield) | 1 | Wireless transmission |
| 3 | DHT11 or LM35 sensor | 1 | Temperature/Humidity sensing |
| 4 | Breadboard + Jumper Wires | As needed | Wiring |
| 5 | USB Cable | 1 | Power + Programming |
| 6 | 3.3V Voltage Regulator / Logic Level Converter | 1 | Protect ESP8266 from 5V Arduino TX |

## 3.2 Circuit Wiring — Step-by-Step

### 3.2.1 Power Connections
1. Connect Arduino **5V** → Breadboard **+ rail**.
2. Connect Arduino **GND** → Breadboard **– rail**.
3. ESP8266 **VCC** → **3.3V** (NOT 5V).
4. ESP8266 **GND** → Breadboard **– rail**.

> [!WARNING]
> Supplying 5V to ESP8266 VCC will permanently damage the module. Use a **logic level converter** or a **3.3V regulator** (AMS1117-3.3) on the TX line from Arduino to ESP8266 RX pin.

### 3.2.2 UART (Serial) Connections
| ESP8266 Pin | Arduino Pin | Direction |
|-------------|-------------|-----------|
| **RX** | TX (pin 1) via 3.3V divider | ESP ← Arduino |
| **TX** | RX (pin 0) | ESP → Arduino |

> Since Arduino's TX outputs 5V and ESP8266's RX tolerates only **3.3V**, a voltage divider is mandatory:
> 
> - **R1 = 1 kΩ** (Arduino TX → midpoint)
> - **R2 = 2 kΩ** (midpoint → GND)
> - Midpoint → ESP8266 RX

### 3.2.3 Sensor Connections
For **DHT11**:
- **VCC** → 5V
- **DATA** → Digital Pin **2** of Arduino
- **GND** → GND
- **10 kΩ pull-up resistor** between DATA and VCC.

## 3.3 ThingSpeak Account Setup (Step-by-Step Derivation)

1. **Step 1:** Navigate to `https://thingspeak.com` and **Sign Up** (MathWorks account).
2. **Step 2:** Click **Channels → My Channels → New Channel**.
3. **Step 3:** Define fields:
   - **Field 1:** Temperature (°C)
   - **Field 2:** Humidity (%)  *(or any other parameter)*
4. **Step 4:** Save channel. Two API keys are auto-generated:
   - **Write API Key** (used by ESP8266 to push data)
   - **Read API Key** (used by clients to fetch data)
5. **Step 5:** Note the **Channel ID** (numeric identifier).
6. **Step 6:** Verify the channel is *Public* under the **Sharing** tab (for free-tier visualization).

## 3.4 ESP8266 Firmware Configuration

The ESP8266 can be programmed in two ways:

### 3.4.1 Method A — AT Command Set (older, two-chip approach)
ESP8266 is controlled by Arduino via **AT commands** over UART.

Essential AT command sequence:

```
AT                 → Test (responds "OK")
AT+CWMODE=1        → Set Station mode
AT+CWJAP="SSID","PASSWORD"   → Connect to WiFi router
AT+CIPMUX=0        → Single connection mode
AT+CIPSTART="TCP","api.thingspeak.com",80   → Open TCP socket
AT+CIPSEND=<length> → Begin sending HTTP request
GET /update?api_key=XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX&field1=25.6 HTTP/1.1
Host: api.thingspeak.com
Connection: close

```

### 3.4.2 Method B — NodeMCU + Arduino Library (modern, single-board approach)
The ESP8266 is flashed directly using the **Arduino IDE**, eliminating the need for an external Arduino for the WiFi part. This is the **recommended KTU approach** for clarity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The lab typically uses **Method A** (Arduino UNO + ESP8266 module) to demonstrate the *separation of concerns* between sensor processing and network transmission. Both are acceptable.

## 3.5 Arduino Sketch — Full Logical Derivation

The complete program structure is divided into **5 logical blocks**:

### 3.5.1 Header and Library Inclusion
```cpp
#include <SoftwareSerial.h>   // For Arduino-to-ESP8266 UART
#include <DHT.h>              // For DHT11 sensor
```
**Why:** `SoftwareSerial.h` is required because Arduino UNO has only one hardware UART, already used for USB debugging. The software UART remaps pins 2 and 3 for ESP8266 communication.

### 3.5.2 Object Initialization
```cpp
SoftwareSerial esp8266(2, 3);  // RX=2, TX=3
#define DHTPIN  4
#define DHTTYPE DHT11
DHT dht(DHTPIN, DHTTYPE);
```

### 3.5.3 Setup() Function Logic
```cpp
void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);          // USB debug
  esp8266.begin(115200);         // ESP8266 link
  dht.begin();                   // DHT sensor
  connectWiFi();                 // Custom function
}
```

### 3.5.4 connectWiFi() Function Derivation

The function sends AT commands sequentially, **each with a 2-second timeout**:

$$
t_{cmd} = 2 \text{ seconds}
$$

This is to prevent the Arduino from hanging if the ESP8266 fails to respond.

```cpp
void connectWiFi() {
  sendCommand("AT",            2000);
  sendCommand("AT+CWMODE=1",   2000);
  sendCommand("AT+CWJAP=\"YOUR_SSID\",\"YOUR_PASSWORD\"", 6000);
}
```

### 3.5.5 Loop() Function — The Core Telemetry Logic

```cpp
void loop() {
  float temperature = dht.readTemperature();
  float humidity    = dht.readHumidity();

  if (isnan(temperature) || isnan(humidity)) {
    Serial.println("Sensor read failed!");
    return;
  }

  String httpRequest = "GET /update?api_key=YOUR_WRITE_KEY";
  httpRequest += "&field1=" + String(temperature);
  httpRequest += "&field2=" + String(humidity);
  httpRequest += " HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: api.thingspeak.com\r\nConnection: close\r\n\r\n";

  sendCommand("AT+CIPSTART=\"TCP\",\"api.thingspeak.com\",80", 3000);
  String cipSend = "AT+CIPSEND=" + String(httpRequest.length());
  sendCommand(cipSend, 2000);
  esp8266.print(httpRequest);

  delay(16000);   // ThingSpeak requires >= 15 s between updates
}
```

### 3.5.6 Helper Function: sendCommand()

The `sendCommand()` helper does four things:
1. Sends the command over `esp8266`.
2. Waits `waitTime` milliseconds.
3. Reads all available response bytes into a String.
4. Echoes the response to the Serial monitor for debugging.

$$
\text{Response Buffer} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{N} \text{esp8266.read()}_i
$$

## 3.6 Numerical Example — Verifying HTTP Request Length

Suppose the read temperature is **25.6 °C** and humidity is **60 %**.

The full HTTP request is:

```
GET /update?api_key=ABC123DEF456GH78&field1=25.60&field2=60.00 HTTP/1.1
Host: api.thingspeak.com
Connection: close
```

Character count (excluding CRLF overhead): approximately **110 bytes**.

The `AT+CIPSEND=110` command tells ESP8266 to expect exactly 110 bytes. The Arduino must then send precisely that many bytes; otherwise the TCP connection will stall.

$$
N_{bytes} = \text{len}(\text{httpRequest}) = 110
$$

## 3.7 Expected Output Verification

1. **Serial Monitor (Arduino side):** prints temperature, humidity, and ESP8266 "OK" responses.
2. **ThingSpeak Dashboard:** the "Temperature" and "Humidity" plots should display new data points every **15–20 seconds**.
3. **Last Entry Status:** on the channel's "Private View", the field *Last entry* should update with a recent timestamp.

## 3.8 Common Runtime Errors and Fixes

| Error Message | Cause | Fix |
|---------------|-------|-----|
| `ERROR CLOSED` | TCP socket not properly closed | Add `AT+CIPCLOSE` after each send |
| No response from ESP8266 | Baud-rate mismatch | Verify both sides use **115200** |
| `WiFi not connected` | Wrong SSID/password | Re-enter credentials exactly |
| `Sensor read failed` | DHT wiring issue / timing | Add 2-second startup delay for DHT |
| `Field value = 0` | Variable not parsed correctly | Check float-to-String conversion |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sensor Layer\nDHT11 / LM35 / LDR]
    M1[Arduino UNO\nATmega328P\nADC + UART]
    W1[ESP8266\nWiFi SoC\n802.11 b/g/n]
    R1[WiFi Router\n2.4 GHz AP]
    C1[ThingSpeak Cloud\nREST API Server]
    V1[Web Dashboard\nCharts and Graphs]

    S1 -- Analog/Digital Signal --> M1
    M1 -- UART TX/RX 115200 bps --> W1
    W1 -- 2.4 GHz WiFi --> R1
    R1 -- Internet TCP/IP --> C1
    C1 -- JSON / HTTPS --> V1
```

## 4.2 Detailed Data-Flow Diagram (DHT → ThingSpeak)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power ON]
    A --> B[Initialize Serial 115200]
    B --> C[Initialize DHT Sensor]
    C --> D[AT Command: Test ESP8266]
    D --> E{ESP8266 OK?}
    E -- No --> F[Print Error and Halt]
    E -- Yes --> G[AT+CWMODE=1 Station Mode]
    G --> H[AT+CWJAP Connect to Router]
    H --> I{Connected?}
    I -- No --> J[Retry Connection]
    I -- Yes --> K[Read Temperature and Humidity]
    K --> L{Reading Valid?}
    L -- No --> M[Wait and Retry]
    L -- Yes --> N[Construct HTTP GET URL]
    N --> O[AT+CIPSTART TCP api.thingspeak.com 80]
    O --> P[AT+CIPSEND Length]
    P --> Q[Transmit HTTP Request]
    Q --> R[Receive Response and HTTP Code]
    R --> S{Response 200 OK?}
    S -- Yes --> T[Update Posted to ThingSpeak]
    S -- No --> U[Log Error and Continue]
    T --> V[Wait 16 Seconds]
    U --> V
    V --> K
```

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Arduino ↔ ESP8266 ↔ ThingSpeak

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant ARD as Arduino UNO
    participant ESP as ESP8266
    participant RTR as WiFi Router
    participant TS as ThingSpeak Server
    participant DB as ThingSpeak Database

    ARD->>ESP: AT (Test)
    ESP-->>ARD: OK
    ARD->>ESP: AT+CWMODE=1
    ESP-->>ARD: OK
    ARD->>ESP: AT+CWJAP=SSID,PWD
    ESP->>RTR: 802.11 Authentication
    RTR-->>ESP: IP Address Assigned
    ESP-->>ARD: WIFI CONNECTED
    loop Every 15-20 seconds
        ARD->>ARD: Read DHT Sensor
        ARD->>ESP: AT+CIPSTART=TCP,api.thingspeak.com,80
        ESP->>TS: TCP SYN
        TS-->>ESP: TCP SYN-ACK
        ESP-->>ARD: Linked
        ARD->>ESP: AT+CIPSEND=N
        ARD->>ESP: HTTP GET URL String
        ESP->>TS: HTTP GET Request
        TS->>DB: Store Field1 Field2
        DB-->>TS: Entry ID
        TS-->>ESP: 200 OK Entry ID
        ESP-->>ARD: Recv N bytes
        ARD->>ESP: AT+CIPCLOSE
    end
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Three-Layer IoT Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PERCEPTION[PERCEPTION LAYER]
        S2[DHT11 Temperature]
        S3[DHT11 Humidity]
        S4[Optional: LDR Light]
    end

    subgraph NETWORK[NETWORK LAYER]
        A2[Arduino UNO MCU]
        E2[ESP8266 WiFi Module]
    end

    subgraph APPLICATION[APPLICATION LAYER]
        T2[ThingSpeak REST API]
        V2[Time-Series Database]
        D2[Web Dashboard]
        M2[MATLAB Analytics]
    end

    S2 --> A2
    S3 --> A2
    S4 --> A2
    A2 -- UART 115200 bps --> E2
    E2 -- WiFi 2.4 GHz --> T2
    T2 --> V2
    V2 --> D2
    V2 --> M2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the role of the ESP8266 module in an IoT system. Mention its operating voltage and default baud rate.**

**Model Answer:**
The ESP8266 is a low-cost WiFi SoC manufactured by Espressif Systems that enables microcontrollers like Arduino UNO to connect to a **2.4 GHz** wireless network and transmit data to cloud servers using **HTTP/MQTT** protocols over TCP/IP.
- **Operating Voltage:** **3.3V DC** (not 5V, to avoid permanent damage).
- **Default Baud Rate:** **115200 bps** for newer firmware; **9600 bps** for older versions.
- It supports **Station (STA)**, **Access Point (AP)**, and **STA+AP** dual modes.
- In this lab, it is used in **STA mode** to upload DHT11 sensor data to the ThingSpeak cloud using a Write API Key embedded in the HTTP GET URL.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**What is ThingSpeak? Mention the minimum update interval allowed for a free-tier account.**

**Model Answer:**
ThingSpeak is an **open-source Internet of Things (IoT) analytics platform** owned by MathWorks that allows users to:
1. **Aggregate** sensor data in the cloud.
2. **Visualize** it through real-time line/bar charts.
3. **Analyze** the data using MATLAB scripts.
4. **Trigger** actions (alerts, tweets, emails) when data crosses thresholds.
- The data is uploaded using a **RESTful API** authenticated by a **Write API Key**.
- For a **free-tier** account, the minimum time gap between two consecutive updates is **15 seconds** (Δt ≥ 15 s). Violating this returns an HTTP **400 Bad Request** error.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Draw and explain the three-layer IoT architecture used in the experiment of sending sensor data to ThingSpeak using Arduino and ESP8266. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the circuit connections between Arduino UNO, ESP8266, and a DHT11 sensor. Why is a logic-level converter needed? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):
The three-layer IoT architecture for the experiment is:

1. **Perception Layer (Sensor Layer):**
   - Consists of the DHT11 sensor that captures physical parameters — **temperature (°C)** and **humidity (% RH)**.
   - Other examples: LDR (light), MQ-2 (gas), LM35 (temperature).

2. **Network Layer (Communication Layer):**
   - The **Arduino UNO** reads the digital data from the DHT11 over a single-wire protocol.
   - The data is passed to the **ESP8266** via **UART (115200 bps)**.
   - The ESP8266 wraps the data in an **HTTP GET** request and transmits it over **WiFi (2.4 GHz)** to the ThingSpeak server using the **TCP/IP protocol stack**.

3. **Application Layer (Service Layer):**
   - ThingSpeak receives, stores, and visualizes the data in real-time.
   - It provides a **dashboard** with time-series graphs, a **MATLAB analytics engine**, and **REST API** endpoints for read/write operations.

> The data flow follows the equation:
> 
> $$ \text{Sensor} \xrightarrow{\text{Digital I/O}} \text{Arduino} \xrightarrow{\text{UART}} \text{ESP8266} \xrightarrow{\text{HTTP/TCP}} \text{ThingSpeak} \xrightarrow{\text{JSON}} \text{Dashboard} $$

#### Model Solution for (b):
**Circuit Connections Table:**

| Component Pin | Connects To | Function |
|---------------|-------------|----------|
| DHT11 VCC | Arduino 5V | Power supply |
| DHT11 DATA | Arduino D4 (+ 10 kΩ pull-up to 5V) | Single-wire data |
| DHT11 GND | Arduino GND | Common ground |
| ESP8266 VCC | 3.3V regulator output | Power (3.3V) |
| ESP8266 GND | Arduino GND | Common ground |
| ESP8266 RX | Arduino TX (via R1=1kΩ, R2=2kΩ divider) | UART receive |
| ESP8266 TX | Arduino RX | UART transmit |

**Why a Logic-Level Converter is Needed:**
- The Arduino UNO operates on **5V logic levels**, but the ESP8266 RX pin tolerates only **3.3V**.
- Applying 5V directly to the ESP8266 RX pin can permanently damage its internal circuitry.
- A **voltage divider** (R1=1kΩ, R2=2kΩ) reduces the 5V Arduino TX signal to ~3.3V at the ESP8266 RX pin.
- The reverse direction (ESP8266 TX → Arduino RX) is safe because **3.3V is recognized as a logic HIGH** by the Arduino's 5V inputs.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the role of the **Write API Key** in the ThingSpeak platform. Construct the complete HTTP GET URL used by the ESP8266 to upload a temperature value of 28.5 °C and humidity of 65 % to a ThingSpeak channel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the Arduino sketch (in pseudo-code / structured form) that connects the ESP8266 to WiFi and uploads the DHT11 sensor data to ThingSpeak every 15 seconds. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):
**Role of Write API Key:**
- The **Write API Key** is a **16-character unique alphanumeric password** that authenticates a device to push data into a specific ThingSpeak channel.
- Without a valid key, the ThingSpeak server returns HTTP **401 Unauthorized** and discards the data.
- It is embedded in the URL query string `?api_key=...`.
- Each channel has a unique key that should **not be shared publicly** to prevent unauthorized uploads.

**Constructed HTTP GET URL:**
For temperature = 28.5 °C and humidity = 65 %, the URL is:

```
GET /update?api_key=ABC123XYZ456MN78&field1=28.50&field2=65.00 HTTP/1.1
Host: api.thingspeak.com
Connection: close
```

Equation form:

$$
\text{URL} = \frac{\text{/update?api_key=WK}}{\text{&field1}=T \, \text{&field2}=H \, \text{HTTP/1.1}}
$$

where `WK` is the Write Key, `T = 28.50` and `H = 65.00`.

#### Model Solution for (b):
**Arduino Sketch (Structured Form):**

```cpp
#include <SoftwareSerial.h>
#include <DHT.h>

SoftwareSerial esp8266(2, 3);  // RX, TX
DHT dht(4, DHT11);
String apiKey = "ABC123XYZ456MN78";

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  esp8266.begin(115200);
  dht.begin();
  
  esp8266.println("AT");
  delay(2000);
  esp8266.println("AT+CWMODE=1");
  delay(2000);
  esp8266.println("AT+CWJAP=\"SSID\",\"PASSWORD\"");
  delay(6000);
}

void loop() {
  float t = dht.readTemperature();
  float h = dht.readHumidity();
  
  if (isnan(t) || isnan(h)) {
    Serial.println("Sensor error");
    return;
  }
  
  String cmd = "AT+CIPSTART=\"TCP\",\"api.thingspeak.com\",80";
  esp8266.println(cmd);
  delay(3000);
  
  String url = "GET /update?api_key=" + apiKey +
               "&field1=" + String(t) +
               "&field2=" + String(h) +
               " HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: api.thingspeak.com\r\nConnection: close\r\n\r\n";
  
  esp8266.println("AT+CIPSEND=" + String(url.length()));
  delay(2000);
  esp8266.print(url);
  delay(3000);
  
  esp8266.println("AT+CIPCLOSE");
  delay(16000);  // >= 15 s ThingSpeak limit
}
```

**Explanation of key logic:**
- The `delay(16000)` enforces the **15-second minimum update interval** of the free ThingSpeak tier.
- `isnan()` check prevents NaN (Not-a-Number) errors when the DHT11 returns invalid readings.
- The URL length is dynamically calculated using `String.length()` so the `AT+CIPSEND` argument matches exactly.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the logic level converter** — applying 5V directly to ESP8266 VCC/RX → −1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Using a 0-second delay** between ThingSpeak updates — the server returns `0` as the entry ID and discards the data → −1 mark.
> 3. **Mismatched baud rates** (Arduino 9600, ESP8266 115200) — silent failure with no "OK" response → −1 mark.
> 4. **Missing `Connection: close` header** — the TCP socket remains open and eventually exhausts the ESP8266's connection memory → −1 mark.
> 5. **Wrong API key pasted** (Read key instead of Write key) — server returns 401 Unauthorized → −1 mark.
> 6. **No pull-up resistor on DHT11 DATA line** — sensor reads become erratic → −1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ESP8266 must be powered with 3.3V**, never 5V; use a voltage divider on the RX line.
- **Default baud rate is 115200 bps**; both Arduino `Serial` and `SoftwareSerial` must match.
- **ThingSpeak minimum update interval is 15 seconds** for the free tier.
- The **Write API Key** is a 16-character string used for upload; the **Read API Key** (18 characters) is used for download.
- The complete URL format is:
  `GET /update?api_key=KEY&field1=v1&field2=v2 HTTP/1.1`
- **Three layers of IoT** are: Perception (sensor), Network (Arduino + ESP8266), Application (ThingSpeak).
- **AT commands** follow a strict order: `AT` → `AT+CWMODE=1` → `AT+CWJAP` → `AT+CIPSTART` → `AT+CIPSEND` → data → `AT+CIPCLOSE`.
- **DHT11** uses a single-wire protocol requiring a **10 kΩ pull-up resistor** on the DATA line.
- The Arduino **ATmega328P ADC is 10-bit** (0–1023), so voltage = $V \cdot (1024/V_{ref})$.
- Always check for `isnan()` after DHT reads to avoid propagating invalid data to the cloud.
- The **Channel ID** is a numeric identifier visible in the ThingSpeak URL when viewing a channel.
- ThingSpeak supports **up to 8 fields per channel** — field1 through field8.
- The HTTP response from ThingSpeak is the **entry ID number** (a positive integer if successful, `0` if rate-limited).
- The **2.4 GHz** WiFi band has a range of roughly **30–50 m indoors** and up to **100 m outdoors** with line-of-sight.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Develop and Test the Mini IoT Projects Using Arduino with ESP8266/Raspberry Pi
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND IOT LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Develop and Test the Mini IoT Projects Using Arduino with ESP8266/Raspberry Pi

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Aligned)

A **Mini IoT Project** in the context of PCRUL408 is a constrained-scope, end-to-end embedded system implementation that integrates **sensing**, **local processing/actuation**, and **wireless network communication** to a remote cloud endpoint or client device. The development platform may be based on the **Arduino ecosystem interfaced with the ESP8266 Wi-Fi SoC** or a standalone **Raspberry Pi single-board computer (SBC)**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for this laboratory, a Mini IoT project must demonstrate the complete **IoT Reference Architecture (3-layer or 5-layer model)** as defined by the **IEEE / ISO/IEC 30141 IoT Standard**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A Mini IoT project must implement *at least* the **Perception Layer** (sensors), **Network Layer** (ESP8266 / Raspberry Pi Wi-Fi/Ethernet), and **Application Layer** (cloud dashboard like ThingSpeak / Blynk / local HTTP client). The Processing Layer is optional but rewarded.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Nervous System"

Imagine your **human body** as an IoT system:
- **Sensors** (eyes, skin, ears) → the **Perception Layer**. They collect data from the environment (light, temperature, pressure).
- **The spinal cord and brain** → the **Network & Processing Layer**. In our projects, this is the **ESP8266** (a tiny brain with built-in Wi-Fi) or the **Raspberry Pi** (a full-grown brain with OS capability).
- **Your hand moving in response** → the **Actuator / Application Layer** (relay, LED, motor, or a cloud dashboard displaying real-time charts).

A **Mini IoT Project** is essentially building a *miniature digital organism* that can *sense*, *think*, and *communicate* over the internet — just like a smartwatch reporting your heartbeat to your phone.

## 1.3 Platform Selection — Arduino + ESP8266 vs. Raspberry Pi

| Parameter | **Arduino + ESP8266** | **Raspberry Pi (3/4/Zero 2 W)** |
|---|---|---|
| **OS** | None (bare-metal firmware) | Full Linux (Raspberry Pi OS) |
| **Compute Power** | 80 MHz Tensilica L106 | 1.0–1.8 GHz Quad-core ARM Cortex |
| **Power** | ~70–240 mA | ~500–1500 mA |
| **Language** | C/C++ (Arduino) / Lua (NodeMCU) | Python 3, C, Node-RED |
| **Best For** | Low-power sensor nodes, battery projects | Edge AI, camera (OpenCV), gateways |
| **Cost** | ~₹250–500 | ~₹3000–7000 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Lab Viva:** *Arduino UNO* alone **cannot** connect to Wi-Fi — it requires an **ESP8266 (ESP-01)** module as a serial-attached Wi-Fi coprocessor, OR you use a **NodeMCU / Wemos D1 Mini** (which is essentially an *ESP8266 with Arduino bootloader*). The Raspberry Pi has Wi-Fi built-in.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The IoT Project Development Lifecycle (KTU Laboratory Standard)

A Mini IoT project must follow a structured engineering lifecycle, mirroring industry practice:

1. **Problem Definition:** State the objective (e.g., *monitor classroom temperature and SMS the caretaker if it exceeds 35°C*).
2. **Hardware Selection:** Choose MCU, sensors, actuators, and power source.
3. **Schematic & Wiring:** Document using Fritzing or Tinkercad.
4. **Firmware / Software Development:** Write embedded code (Arduino sketch) or Python script.
5. **Communication Protocol Implementation:** HTTP (REST/MQTT) or WebSocket.
6. **Cloud / Local Server Integration:** Use ThingSpeak, Blynk, Firebase, or a local Flask server.
7. **Testing & Validation:** Use Serial Monitor, Wireshark, and ping/curl for network verification.
8. **Documentation & Demo:** Viva-voce and lab record submission.

## 2.2 Core Hardware & Communication Theory

### 2.2.1 The ESP8266 — The Wi-Fi Workhorse
- It is a **32-bit Tensilica L106 microcontroller** with integrated **TCP/IP stack** and **802.11 b/g/n** radio.
- Operates on **3.3 V logic** — *never connect its RX/TX directly to a 5 V Arduino UNO*, or you will fry it.
- Default baud rate: **115200 bps** (older modules: 9600 bps).
- AT command set: `AT+CWMODE=1` (station), `AT+CIPMUX=1` (multi-connection), `AT+CWJAP="SSID","PASS"`.

### 2.2.2 The Raspberry Pi — The Edge Computer
- Has **GPIO pins (3.3 V logic)**, similar pinout to Arduino but with Linux underneath.
- Supports libraries like `RPi.GPIO`, `gpiozero`, `paho-mqtt` (for MQTT), and `Flask` (for HTTP).

### 2.2.3 Communication Protocols — The Lifeblood of IoT

| Protocol | Transport | Port | Use Case in Mini Projects |
|---|---|---|---|
| **HTTP / REST** | TCP | 80 | Pushing data to ThingSpeak via GET/POST |
| **MQTT** | TCP | 1883 | Lightweight pub/sub (Adafruit IO, HiveMQ) |
| **CoAP** | UDP | 5683 | Constrained devices (rare in lab) |
| **WebSocket** | TCP | 80/443 | Real-time dashboards (Node-RED) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Reference Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FOR VIVA:** The following equations/values are the most frequently asked in KTU lab exams. Memorize the table.

| Quantity / Concept | Formula or Value | Unit | Engineering Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ESP8266 Logic Level** | $V_{IH} = 3.3\,\text{V}$ max | Volts | Voltage divider required for 5V Arduino TX |
| **Voltage Divider (TX level shift)** | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Volts | Use $R_1 = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$ to step 5V → ~3.3V |
| **ADC Resolution (Arduino Uno)** | $\text{Step} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{10}} = \dfrac{5}{1024} \approx 4.88\,\text{mV}$ | mV/LSB | Reading analog sensor voltage |
| **ADC Resolution (ESP8266)** | $\text{Step} = \dfrac{3.3}{1024} \approx 3.22\,\text{mV}$ | mV/LSB | Only **1 ADC pin** (A0), 0–3.3V range |
| **MQTT Topic Pattern** | `device/<id>/sensor/<type>` | String | Standard pub/sub hierarchy |
| **HTTP GET request size** | Typical ThingSpeak URL: ~200 bytes | Bytes | Energy budget for battery nodes |
| **Sampling Theorem (Nyquist)** | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Hz | To avoid aliasing of sensor data |
| **Power Dissipation (LED)** | $P = V \cdot I = (V_s - V_f) \cdot I_f$ | Watts | Current-limiting resistor calc |
| **ESP8266 Current Spike** | Peak ~**300 mA** during TX | mA | Requires a **decoupled 3.3V regulator** (AMS1117) |
| **Raspberry Pi GPIO Source Current** | Max **16 mA per pin**, **50 mA total** | mA | Use a transistor/MOSFET for relays |

# 3. Step-by-Step Project Development & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Reference Mini Project — "Wi-Fi Enabled Temperature & Humidity Monitor with Cloud Logging"

This is a canonical KTU lab experiment. We will derive the full build, code, and math.

### 3.1.1 Hardware Bill of Materials (BOM)
1. **NodeMCU V3 (ESP8266-based)** × 1  *(equivalent to Arduino+ESP8266 combo)*
2. **DHT11 / DHT22** Temperature & Humidity sensor × 1
3. **Breadboard + Jumper Wires**
4. **Micro-USB cable** for power and programming
5. **ThingSpeak account** (free) — provides a **Channel ID** and **Write API Key**

### 3.1.2 Circuit Wiring Logic
- **DHT VCC** → **3V3** pin of NodeMCU
- **DHT GND** → **GND** pin of NodeMCU
- **DHT DATA** → **D2 (GPIO4)** pin of NodeMCU
- A **10 kΩ pull-up resistor** between DATA and VCC (required for DHT bus idle-high)

### 3.1.3 Software Library Dependencies (Arduino IDE)
Install via Library Manager:
- `DHT sensor library` by Adafruit
- `Adafruit Unified Sensor`
- `ThingSpeak` by MathWorks

### 3.1.4 Exhaustive Arduino Sketch (Step-by-Step Annotated)

```cpp
// ===== Step 1: Include libraries =====
#include <ESP8266WiFi.h>          // Built-in Wi-Fi driver
#include <ThingSpeak.h>          // Cloud client
#include "DHT.h"                  // DHT sensor driver

// ===== Step 2: Define constants =====
#define DHTPIN    D2              // DATA pin mapped to GPIO4
#define DHTTYPE   DHT11           // Sensor family
const char* ssid     = "YOUR_WIFI_SSID";
const char* password = "YOUR_WIFI_PASS";

// ===== Step 3: ThingSpeak credentials =====
unsigned long channelID = 1234567;     // Replace with your Channel ID
const char* writeAPIKey = "XXXXXXXXXXXX";  // Replace with Write API Key

// ===== Step 4: Initialize objects =====
DHT dht(DHTPIN, DHTTYPE);
WiFiClient client;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  dht.begin();
  WiFi.begin(ssid, password);

  // Wait for connection (max 20 seconds)
  Serial.print("Connecting Wi-Fi");
  int timeout = 0;
  while (WiFi.status() != WL_CONNECTED && timeout < 40) {
    delay(500);
    Serial.print(".");
    timeout++;
  }

  if (WiFi.status() == WL_CONNECTED) {
    Serial.println("\nConnected! IP: " + WiFi.localIP().toString());
  } else {
    Serial.println("\nWi-Fi FAILED — check SSID/password");
  }

  ThingSpeak.begin(client);
}

void loop() {
  // Read sensor with slow bus (DHT11 needs ~1s gap)
  float humidity    = dht.readHumidity();
  float temperature = dht.readTemperature();  // Celsius

  if (isnan(humidity) || isnan(temperature)) {
    Serial.println("DHT read FAILED — check wiring");
    delay(2000);
    return;
  }

  // Build ThingSpeak fields
  ThingSpeak.setField(1, temperature);
  ThingSpeak.setField(2, humidity);

  // Push to cloud (HTTP POST, port 1842 for ThingSpeak MQTT, 80 for HTTP)
  int httpCode = ThingSpeak.writeFields(channelID, writeAPIKey);

  if (httpCode == 200) {
    Serial.println("Channel update successful.");
  } else {
    Serial.println("Problem updating channel. HTTP error code: " + String(httpCode));
  }

  delay(20000);  // ThingSpeak free tier: 15-second minimum interval
}
```

### 3.1.5 Derivation — Why a 10 kΩ Pull-up on the DHT Line?

The DHT11 uses an **open-drain single-wire protocol**. When the sensor is not actively driving the line low, the bus must be **pulled high** by an external resistor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation of Pull-up Current:** The DHT specs state $V_{OL} \le 0.4\,\text{V}$ at $I_{OL} = 1\,\text{mA}$. With $V_{CC} = 3.3\,\text{V}$, the pull-up current when the line is idle is:
>
> $$I_{pullup} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{OH}}{R_{pullup}}$$
>
> Choosing $R_{pullup} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ gives $I_{pullup} = 330\,\mu\text{A}$, which is well within the 3.3V rail's capability and ensures $V_{OH} \approx 3.3\,\text{V}$ (no significant drop).

### 3.1.6 Derivation — Voltage Divider for Arduino-UNO → ESP-01 Communication

If using an **Arduino UNO (5V logic)** to communicate with an **ESP-01 (3.3V logic)** via UART, a level shifter is mandatory. A simple resistive divider is used on the UNO's **TX** line:

$$
V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

$$
V_{out} = 5 \cdot \frac{2\,\text{k}\Omega}{1\,\text{k}\Omega + 2\,\text{k}\Omega} = 5 \cdot \frac{2}{3} = 3.33\,\text{V}
$$

This is just above the **3.3 V** level — safe for the ESP8266 RX pin (max absolute rating is **3.6 V**).

> **Verification:** The ESP8266 datasheet guarantees $V_{IH} \ge 0.75 \cdot V_{IO} = 0.75 \cdot 3.3 = 2.475\,\text{V}$. Since $3.33\,\text{V} > 2.475\,\text{V}$, the logic HIGH is reliably recognized. ✔

### 3.1.7 Project Verification — Testing Methodology (for KTU Lab Record)

| Test Case | Expected Result | Verification Method |
|---|---|---|
| Wi-Fi Connection | IP printed on Serial | `Serial.println(WiFi.localIP())` |
| DHT11 Reading | Valid T (0–50°C) & H (20–90%) | Compare with a commercial hygrometer |
| ThingSpeak Update | HTTP 200 response | Serial Monitor + ThingSpeak graph |
| Failure Mode 1 | "DHT read FAILED" if wire loose | Pull DATA wire out, observe |
| Failure Mode 2 | "Wi-Fi FAILED" if router off | Power off AP, observe timeout |

## 3.2 Alternative Project — Raspberry Pi Based Motion-Triggered Image Capture

### 3.2.1 Components
- Raspberry Pi 3/4
- PIR Motion Sensor (HC-SR501)
- Pi Camera Module v2

### 3.2.2 Wiring
- PIR VCC → Pin 2 (5V)
- PIR GND → Pin 6 (GND)
- PIR OUT → Pin 11 (GPIO17)
- Camera → CSI ribbon slot

### 3.2.3 Python Script (Exhaustive)

```python
# Step 1: Imports
from gpiozero import MotionSensor
from picamera2 import Picamera2
import time, requests

# Step 2: Initialize hardware
pir = MotionSensor(17)
picam = Picamera2()
picam.configure(picam.create_still_configuration())
picam.start()

SERVER_URL = "http://your-flask-server.local/upload"
ALERT_WEBHOOK = "https://maker.ifttt.com/trigger/motion/with/key/YOUR_KEY"

def on_motion():
    print("Motion detected!")
    timestamp = int(time.time())
    filename = f"motion_{timestamp}.jpg"
    picam.capture_file(filename)

    # Send via HTTP POST
    with open(filename, "rb") as f:
        try:
            r = requests.post(SERVER_URL, files={"image": f}, timeout=5)
            print(f"Upload status: {r.status_code}")
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"Upload failed: {e}")

    # Trigger IFTTT alert
    try:
        requests.get(ALERT_WEBHOOK, timeout=5)
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Alert failed: {e}")

print("PIR sensor armed. Waiting for motion...")
pir.when_motion = on_motion

# Keep script alive
while True:
    time.sleep(1)
```

> **Note on `gpiozero`:** The library automatically handles the **bounce time** of the PIR (default 2 seconds), so we don't need manual `time.sleep()` debouncing.

## 3.3 Project Architecture Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Arduino + ESP8266 (NodeMCU) | Raspberry Pi |
|---|---|---|
| **Boot Time** | < 1 second | 20–60 seconds |
| **File System** | Flash (1–4 MB) | microSD (8–128 GB) |
| **Camera Support** | External (TTL serial) | Native CSI camera |
| **Real-Time Performance** | Excellent (deterministic) | Poor (non-RT Linux kernel) |
| **MQTT Support** | `PubSubClient.h` library | `paho-mqtt` Python lib |
| **Power Backup** | Easy (battery + deep sleep) | Hard (needs PMIC) |
| **Typical Lab Project** | Temp/Humidity logger | Image upload, edge AI |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]**
**List any three differences between Arduino UNO + ESP8266 and Raspberry Pi as IoT development platforms.**

**Model Answer (any three):**
1. **Operating System:** Arduino runs no OS (bare-metal); Raspberry Pi runs a full Linux OS (Raspberry Pi OS).
2. **Compute Power:** Arduino's ATmega328P is 8-bit/16 MHz; Raspberry Pi is 64-bit quad-core 1.0+ GHz ARM Cortex.
3. **Connectivity:** Arduino UNO needs an external ESP8266 for Wi-Fi; Raspberry Pi has onboard Wi-Fi and Bluetooth.
4. **Power:** Arduino draws ~50 mA (battery friendly); Raspberry Pi needs a stable 5V/3A supply.
5. **Programming Language:** Arduino uses C/C++ via Arduino IDE; Raspberry Pi commonly uses Python 3.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]**
**What is the role of MQTT protocol in an IoT project? Mention the default port number.**

**Model Answer:**
**MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** is a lightweight **publish/subscribe** messaging protocol designed for low-bandwidth, high-latency, or unreliable networks — ideal for IoT. Instead of a client directly polling a server, devices *publish* messages to a *topic* (e.g., `home/temperature`) on an **MQTT broker** (like Mosquitto, HiveMQ, or Adafruit IO). Other clients *subscribe* to that topic to receive updates.

It uses **TCP**, has a tiny **2-byte fixed header**, and the default port is **1883** (port 8883 for MQTT over TLS/SSL).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Design and explain a Mini IoT project to monitor the **soil moisture and temperature** of a greenhouse and **log the data to ThingSpeak cloud** for remote monitoring. List the components and draw the block diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write the complete **Arduino sketch** for ESP8266 (NodeMCU) to read the soil moisture sensor (analog) and DHT11 (digital) and push the values to ThingSpeak every 20 seconds. Explain the code section by section. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

**Project Title:** *Greenhouse Soil-Moisture & Temperature Monitoring using NodeMCU + ThingSpeak*

**1. Problem Statement:**
Greenhouse farmers need to remotely monitor **soil moisture (%)** and **ambient temperature (°C)** to automate irrigation. The system must push data to a cloud dashboard (ThingSpeak) so the farmer can view real-time graphs on a mobile browser.

**2. List of Components:**

| S.No | Component | Quantity | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | NodeMCU V3 (ESP8266) | 1 | Wi-Fi enabled MCU |
| 2 | Soil Moisture Sensor (capacitive) | 1 | Analog moisture sensing |
| 3 | DHT11 Sensor | 1 | Temperature + Humidity |
| 4 | Breadboard & Wires | 1 set | Wiring |
| 5 | Micro-USB Cable | 1 | Power + Programming |
| 6 | ThingSpeak Account | 1 | Cloud dashboard (free tier) |

**3. Block Diagram:** (see Section 4.1 — Perception layer with soil sensor, Network layer NodeMCU, App layer ThingSpeak).

**4. Working Principle:**
- The **capacitive soil moisture sensor** outputs an analog voltage (0–3.3 V) proportional to soil water content. Dry soil → high voltage; wet soil → low voltage.
- The **DHT11** uses a single-wire digital protocol to transmit calibrated temperature and humidity values.
- The NodeMCU reads these, converts them to engineering units, and forms an **HTTP GET request** to ThingSpeak's REST API.
- The farmer logs into ThingSpeak's web dashboard to see real-time charts and can configure email alerts when moisture falls below a threshold.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

**Complete Arduino Sketch:**

```cpp
#include <ESP8266WiFi.h>
#include <ThingSpeak.h>
#include "DHT.h"

#define DHTPIN   D4
#define DHTTYPE  DHT11
#define SOILPIN  A0

const char* ssid     = "Greenhouse_WiFi";
const char* password = "secure123";

unsigned long chID    = 987654;
const char* apiKey    = "ABC123XYZ";

DHT dht(DHTPIN, DHTTYPE);
WiFiClient client;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  dht.begin();
  WiFi.begin(ssid, password);
  while (WiFi.status() != WL_CONNECTED) {
    delay(500); Serial.print(".");
  }
  Serial.println("\nWiFi connected. IP: " + WiFi.localIP().toString());
  ThingSpeak.begin(client);
}

void loop() {
  float t = dht.readTemperature();
  float h = dht.readHumidity();
  int soilRaw = analogRead(SOILPIN);          // 0–1023
  int soilPct = map(soilRaw, 1023, 400, 0, 100); // Empirical calibration

  if (isnan(t) || isnan(h)) { Serial.println("DHT FAIL"); return; }

  ThingSpeak.setField(1, t);
  ThingSpeak.setField(2, h);
  ThingSpeak.setField(3, soilPct);

  int code = ThingSpeak.writeFields(chID, apiKey);
  Serial.println("HTTP: " + String(code));
  delay(20000);
}
```

**Code Explanation (Section-by-Section):**

1. **Lines 1–4:** Include the ESP8266 Wi-Fi driver, ThingSpeak REST client, and DHT sensor library.
2. **Lines 6–12:** Define pin assignments, Wi-Fi credentials, and the ThingSpeak channel credentials.
3. **`setup()`:** Initializes the DHT sensor, connects to Wi-Fi, and prepares the ThingSpeak client. The `while` loop blocks until an IP is assigned.
4. **`loop()`:** Reads temperature and humidity from the DHT11. Reads the raw analog value (0–1023) from the soil sensor. The `map()` function linearly scales this to a 0–100 % moisture range (calibration constants must be adjusted for the specific sensor).
5. **`ThingSpeak.setField()`:** Three different fields (1=Temperature, 2=Humidity, 3=Soil %) are populated.
6. **`ThingSpeak.writeFields()`:** Sends the data via an HTTP POST to ThingSpeak's cloud.
7. **`delay(20000)`:** Respects the ThingSpeak free-tier **15-second minimum update interval**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. Forgetting that **ThingSpeak free tier requires a 15-second minimum** between writes — `delay(15000)` is mandatory; otherwise, the HTTP returns code `-301` (rate limit).
> 2. Using `Serial.begin(9600)` on a NodeMCU without changing the IDE's board baud — the default NodeMCU baud is **115200**.
> 3. The capacitive soil sensor's `map()` range is **non-universal** — you must experimentally calibrate the wet (raw max) and dry (raw min) values for your specific probe.
> 4. Not casting the `map()` return — it is an `int`, not a `float`.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a **Raspberry Pi based smart surveillance system** that captures an image upon detecting motion via a PIR sensor and uploads it to a Flask web server. Draw the block diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a **Python script** for the Raspberry Pi to detect motion, capture an image using the Pi Camera, and upload it to a local Flask server with the timestamp as the filename. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

**Architecture:**

The system has four functional blocks:

1. **Sensing Block:** A **HC-SR501 PIR sensor** outputs a digital HIGH (3.3 V) on its OUT pin when it detects infrared motion from a human body.
2. **Processing Block:** The **Raspberry Pi 4** runs a Python script using the `gpiozero` library to monitor GPIO 17. Upon receiving a HIGH, it triggers the **Pi Camera Module v2** (8 MP Sony IMX219) to capture a still image.
3. **Communication Block:** The captured JPEG is uploaded via **HTTP POST** using the `requests` library to a **Flask web server** running on a laptop or cloud VM. Optionally, an **IFTTT webhook** is fired to send an SMS/email alert.
4. **Storage/Display Block:** The Flask server stores the image in a directory and serves it back to the operator's browser.

**Block Diagram:** (refer to Section 4.4 — Raspberry Pi hardware/software/network architecture).

**Advantages over Arduino:** The Pi can directly run a web server, handle image processing (OpenCV), and store data — Arduino would need an SD card module and cannot easily handle JPEG images.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

**Complete Python Script:**

```python
from gpiozero import MotionSensor
from picamera2 import Picamera2
import requests, time, os

# --- Hardware setup ---
pir = MotionSensor(17)
cam = Picamera2()
cam.configure(cam.create_still_configuration())
cam.start()
time.sleep(2)  # Camera warm-up

# --- Server endpoint ---
SERVER_URL = "http://192.168.1.100:5000/upload"
SAVE_DIR = "/home/pi/captures"
os.makedirs(SAVE_DIR, exist_ok=True)

def handle_motion():
    ts = time.strftime("%Y%m%d_%H%M%S")
    fname = f"{SAVE_DIR}/motion_{ts}.jpg"
    cam.capture_file(fname)
    print(f"[{ts}] Captured {fname}")

    with open(fname, "rb") as f:
        try:
            r = requests.post(SERVER_URL,
                              files={"image": (os.path.basename(fname), f, "image/jpeg")},
                              data={"timestamp": ts},
                              timeout=10)
            print(f"Upload status: {r.status_code}")
        except requests.exceptions.RequestException as e:
            print(f"Upload failed: {e}")

print("Surveillance system armed.")
pir.when_motion = handle_motion
while True:
    time.sleep(1)
```

**Code Explanation:**

1. **Imports:** `gpiozero` abstracts GPIO pin reading; `picamera2` is the official libcamera-based Python API (replacing the deprecated `picamera`); `requests` handles HTTP.
2. **Initialization:** The PIR is on **GPIO17**; the camera is configured for still capture and given **2 seconds** to auto-adjust exposure and white balance.
3. **`handle_motion()`:** A callback function is registered to `pir.when_motion`. It generates a timestamped filename, captures the JPEG, and POSTs the file as **multipart/form-data** to the Flask server.
4. **Main loop:** The `while True` keeps the Python interpreter alive — `gpiozero` runs the motion detection in a background thread.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls (KTU Examiner's Notes):**
> 1. Using the **old `picamera` library** on Bullseye/Bookworm — it no longer works. Use `picamera2`.
> 2. Forgetting the **camera warm-up delay** of at least 2 seconds — first capture will be black/white.
> 3. PIR sensors have a **2.5 second lockout** after a trigger; rapid events will be missed.
> 4. The Raspberry Pi CSI camera must be **enabled** via `sudo raspi-config` → Interface Options → Camera.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (must be in your brain by exam day):**

- 🔹 **NodeMCU = ESP8266 + Arduino bootloader** on a single board (no separate ESP-01 needed).
- 🔹 **Logic Level Rule:** ESP8266 GPIO is **3.3 V only** — never apply 5 V or you will damage it permanently.
- 🔹 **Voltage Divider Formula** for 5 V → 3.3 V: use $1\,\text{k}\Omega$ + $2\,\text{k}\Omega$ in series, tap from the middle.
- 🔹 **Analog Resolution:** Arduino = 1024 steps over 5 V (4.88 mV); ESP8266 = 1024 steps over 3.3 V (3.22 mV).
- 🔹 **Wi-Fi credentials** must be exact; misspelled SSID = infinite loop in `setup()` (add a timeout guard).
- 🔹 **MQTT** is **lightweight pub/sub over TCP port 1883**; HTTP/REST is **request-response over TCP port 80**.
- 🔹 **ThingSpeak free tier** has a **15-second minimum update interval**; exceeding it returns HTTP code `-301`.
- 🔹 **DHT11 range:** Temperature 0–50 °C (±2 °C), Humidity 20–90 % (±5 %); **DHT22** has wider range and better accuracy.
- 🔹 **PIR sensor** needs a **warm-up time of 30–60 seconds** after power-on before reliable motion detection.
- 🔹 **Raspberry Pi GPIO current limit:** 16 mA per pin, 50 mA total — always use a transistor/MOSFET driver for relays/motors.
- 🔹 **MQTT Topic Format** is hierarchical: e.g., `lab/cse/room304/temperature`.
- 🔹 **Cloud Platforms you can be asked about:** ThingSpeak (MathWorks), Blynk (mobile-first), Adafruit IO (MQTT), Firebase (Google), AWS IoT Core (industry).
- 🔹 **IoT Layers to memorize:** 3-layer (Perception / Network / Application) **OR** 5-layer (Perception / Transport / Processing / Application / Business).
- 🔹 **Code must always include** a `Serial.begin()` for debugging and a `delay()` between cloud writes to respect rate limits.
- 🔹 **Lab record must show:** circuit diagram, working code, output screenshot, cloud dashboard screenshot, and conclusion.
